text:
Large Language Models

This slide acts as a section divider for the part of the lecture focused specifically on Large Language Models. After explaining basic language modeling, n-gram models, neural language models, likelihood maximization, and gradient descent, the lecture is now ready to discuss LLMs as large-scale neural language models. The title indicates a transition from the training foundations to the architecture and categories of modern LLMs. Although the slide contains no additional technical details, it signals that the following slides will likely explain transformers, encoder and decoder architectures, and the different types of LLMs used for understanding and generation. For students, this is a useful checkpoint: the earlier slides explained the problem that LLMs solve, namely predicting and generating language; this section will explain the model family that made today’s powerful systems possible.

Key concepts: Large Language Models, Section transition, Neural language models, Transformers, LLM architecture

metadata.index_text: Slide 26. Large Language Models. Section divider introducing the LLM architecture and transformer-based model discussion.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide acts as a section divider for the part of the lecture focused specifically on Large Language Models. After explaining basic language modeling, n-gram models, neural language models, likelihood maximization, and gradient descent, the lecture is now ready to discuss LLMs as large-scale neural language models. The title indicates a transition from the training foundations to the architecture and categories of modern LLMs. Although the slide contains no additional technical details, it signals that the following slides will likely explain transformers, encoder and decoder architectures, and the different types of LLMs used for understanding and generation. For students, this is a useful checkpoint: the earlier slides explained the problem that LLMs solve, namely predicting and generating language; this section will explain the model family that made today’s powerful systems possible.

text:
LLM Architecture

This slide states that an LLM is a type of transformer model and defines a transformer as a neural network that learns context and meaning by tracking relationships in sequential data. The diagram on the right shows the classic transformer architecture with an encoder stack and a decoder stack. At the bottom, input embeddings and output embeddings convert tokens into numerical vectors. Positional encoding is added because transformers do not naturally process tokens in order like recurrent networks; positional information tells the model where each token appears in the sequence. The encoder side contains repeated blocks with multi-head attention, add-and-normalize operations, and feed-forward layers. Multi-head attention allows the model to focus on different relationships between tokens, such as subject-verb links, references, or semantic associations. The decoder side includes masked multi-head attention, encoder-decoder attention, feed-forward layers, and final linear and softmax layers that produce output probabilities. Masked attention prevents the decoder from looking at future tokens during generation. The Nx labels indicate that these blocks are stacked multiple times, creating depth. The final softmax converts model outputs into a probability distribution over possible next tokens. The key lesson is that transformers are powerful because they model relationships among tokens directly through attention, enabling them to learn context and meaning from sequences more effectively than earlier architectures.

Key concepts: LLM architecture, Transformer, Attention, Encoder, Decoder, Positional encoding, Softmax

metadata.index_text: Slide 27. LLM Architecture. LLMs are transformer models. Transformers learn context and meaning through relationships in sequential data. Diagram shows encoder and decoder stacks with embeddings positional encoding attention feed-forward add and norm softmax output probabilities.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide states that an LLM is a type of transformer model and defines a transformer as a neural network that learns context and meaning by tracking relationships in sequential data. The diagram on the right shows the classic transformer architecture with an encoder stack and a decoder stack. At the bottom, input embeddings and output embeddings convert tokens into numerical vectors. Positional encoding is added because transformers do not naturally process tokens in order like recurrent networks; positional information tells the model where each token appears in the sequence. The encoder side contains repeated blocks with multi-head attention, add-and-normalize operations, and feed-forward layers. Multi-head attention allows the model to focus on different relationships between tokens, such as subject-verb links, references, or semantic associations. The decoder side includes masked multi-head attention, encoder-decoder attention, feed-forward layers, and final linear and softmax layers that produce output probabilities. Masked attention prevents the decoder from looking at future tokens during generation. The Nx labels indicate that these blocks are stacked multiple times, creating depth. The final softmax converts model outputs into a probability distribution over possible next tokens. The key lesson is that transformers are powerful because they model relationships among tokens directly through attention, enabling them to learn context and meaning from sequences more effectively than earlier architectures.

text:
Transformer Architecture: Encoder and Decoder

This slide simplifies the transformer architecture into two main components: the encoder and the decoder. The encoder receives input tokens, such as a prompt, and encodes them with contextual understanding. This means that each input token is converted into a vector representation that depends not only on the token itself but also on surrounding tokens. For example, the word “bank” should be represented differently in “river bank” and “bank account.” The slide says the encoder produces one vector per input token, meaning each token gets a contextual embedding. The decoder accepts input tokens and generates new tokens. In generation tasks, the decoder uses previous output tokens and possibly encoded input information to predict the next token. The central diagram shows embeddings at the bottom feeding into the encoder and decoder. The encoder produces internal representations, and the decoder produces output through a softmax layer. The dashed connection suggests that generated outputs can be fed back into the decoder step by step during autoregressive generation. This slide helps students understand that transformers are not one single fixed structure; they are built from components that can be used in different ways. Encoder components are strong for understanding input, decoder components are strong for producing output, and together they can transform an input sequence into an output sequence.

Key concepts: Transformer architecture, Encoder, Decoder, Embeddings, Contextual representation, Token generation

metadata.index_text: Slide 28. Transformer Architecture. Encoder encodes prompts with contextual understanding and produces one vector per input token. Decoder accepts input tokens and generates new tokens.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide simplifies the transformer architecture into two main components: the encoder and the decoder. The encoder receives input tokens, such as a prompt, and encodes them with contextual understanding. This means that each input token is converted into a vector representation that depends not only on the token itself but also on surrounding tokens. For example, the word “bank” should be represented differently in “river bank” and “bank account.” The slide says the encoder produces one vector per input token, meaning each token gets a contextual embedding. The decoder accepts input tokens and generates new tokens. In generation tasks, the decoder uses previous output tokens and possibly encoded input information to predict the next token. The central diagram shows embeddings at the bottom feeding into the encoder and decoder. The encoder produces internal representations, and the decoder produces output through a softmax layer. The dashed connection suggests that generated outputs can be fed back into the decoder step by step during autoregressive generation. This slide helps students understand that transformers are not one single fixed structure; they are built from components that can be used in different ways. Encoder components are strong for understanding input, decoder components are strong for producing output, and together they can transform an input sequence into an output sequence.

text:
Transformer Architecture: Encoder-Only, Encoder-Decoder, and Decoder-Only Models

This slide compares three major transformer model families: encoder-only models, encoder-decoder models, and decoder-only models. The left diagram shows encoder-only models, where input passes through an encoder and produces an output representation. These models are usually used for understanding tasks such as classification, embedding generation, and token labeling. The center diagram shows encoder-decoder models, where one part encodes the input and another part decodes an output sequence. This structure is useful when the task transforms one sequence into another, such as translation or summarization. The right diagram shows decoder-only models, where input goes into a decoder stack and output is generated autoregressively. Decoder-only models are commonly used for open-ended text generation, chatbots, and general-purpose LLMs such as GPT-style systems. The visual layout emphasizes that the same transformer building blocks can be arranged differently depending on the task. Encoder-only models mainly understand; decoder-only models mainly generate; encoder-decoder models combine understanding of a source sequence with generation of a target sequence. The dashed feedback arrows in the decoder-related diagrams indicate that generated tokens can be repeatedly fed back into the model to produce longer outputs. This slide is important because later examples such as BERT, GPT, and T5 correspond directly to these three architectural categories.

Key concepts: Encoder-only models, Encoder-decoder models, Decoder-only models, Transformer families, Autoregressive generation

metadata.slide_title: Transformer Architecture: Encoder-Only, Encoder-Decoder, and Decoder-Only Models
metadata.index_text: Slide 29. Transformer Architecture. Comparison of encoder-only, encoder-decoder, and decoder-only models. Encoder-only for understanding, encoder-decoder for sequence transformation, decoder-only for generation.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide compares three major transformer model families: encoder-only models, encoder-decoder models, and decoder-only models. The left diagram shows encoder-only models, where input passes through an encoder and produces an output representation. These models are usually used for understanding tasks such as classification, embedding generation, and token labeling. The center diagram shows encoder-decoder models, where one part encodes the input and another part decodes an output sequence. This structure is useful when the task transforms one sequence into another, such as translation or summarization. The right diagram shows decoder-only models, where input goes into a decoder stack and output is generated autoregressively. Decoder-only models are commonly used for open-ended text generation, chatbots, and general-purpose LLMs such as GPT-style systems. The visual layout emphasizes that the same transformer building blocks can be arranged differently depending on the task. Encoder-only models mainly understand; decoder-only models mainly generate; encoder-decoder models combine understanding of a source sequence with generation of a target sequence. The dashed feedback arrows in the decoder-related diagrams indicate that generated tokens can be repeatedly fed back into the model to produce longer outputs. This slide is important because later examples such as BERT, GPT, and T5 correspond directly to these three architectural categories.

text:
Transformer Architecture: Translation with Encoder-Decoder Models

This slide explains encoder-decoder transformers through a translation example. The input sentence is “Hur mår du?” and the output sentence is “How are you?” The slide states that the encoder extracts features from an input sequence, while the decoder uses those features to produce an output sentence. In the diagram, the encoder stack processes the source sentence and creates contextual representations of its tokens. These representations capture the meaning of the Swedish phrase rather than just individual word identities. The decoder stack then uses these encoded features to generate the English translation token by token. The arrows from the encoder stack to the decoder stack show that the decoder is conditioned on information extracted by the encoder. This is why encoder-decoder models are suitable for tasks where the output depends closely on an input sequence but may have a different length, structure, or language. Translation is a clear example because the model must understand the source sentence and generate an equivalent sentence in another language. The slide helps students distinguish encoder-decoder transformers from decoder-only models: a decoder-only chatbot may continue a prompt, but an encoder-decoder model explicitly separates input understanding from output generation. The same architecture can also support summarization, question answering over a passage, and text-to-code generation.

Key concepts: Encoder-decoder architecture, Machine translation, Encoder features, Decoder generation, Sequence-to-sequence modeling

metadata.slide_title: Transformer Architecture: Translation with Encoder-Decoder Models
metadata.index_text: Slide 30. Transformer Architecture translation case. Encoder extracts features from input sentence Hur mår du. Decoder uses features to produce output sentence How are you.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains encoder-decoder transformers through a translation example. The input sentence is “Hur mår du?” and the output sentence is “How are you?” The slide states that the encoder extracts features from an input sequence, while the decoder uses those features to produce an output sentence. In the diagram, the encoder stack processes the source sentence and creates contextual representations of its tokens. These representations capture the meaning of the Swedish phrase rather than just individual word identities. The decoder stack then uses these encoded features to generate the English translation token by token. The arrows from the encoder stack to the decoder stack show that the decoder is conditioned on information extracted by the encoder. This is why encoder-decoder models are suitable for tasks where the output depends closely on an input sequence but may have a different length, structure, or language. Translation is a clear example because the model must understand the source sentence and generate an equivalent sentence in another language. The slide helps students distinguish encoder-decoder transformers from decoder-only models: a decoder-only chatbot may continue a prompt, but an encoder-decoder model explicitly separates input understanding from output generation. The same architecture can also support summarization, question answering over a passage, and text-to-code generation.

text:
Encoder-Only LLMs

This slide describes encoder-only language models and their typical tasks. Encoder-only models are suited for tasks that require understanding language rather than generating long new text. They process the whole input sequence and produce contextual representations that can be used for classification, tagging, similarity, or retrieval. The slide gives examples such as toxicity classification and sentiment analysis. In toxicity classification, the model reads a text and predicts whether it contains harmful or offensive language. In sentiment analysis, it reads text such as a tweet or review and predicts whether the sentiment is positive, negative, or neutral. The example model is BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. “Bidirectional” is important because BERT-style encoders can use context from both the left and right sides of a token when forming representations. This makes them strong for understanding tasks because each word is interpreted in the context of the full sentence. The slide mentions sentiment analysis of tweets as a practical example: the model would read a tweet and classify its emotional attitude. The key idea is that encoder-only models are not primarily designed to generate responses token by token. Instead, they transform input text into rich representations that downstream classifiers or task heads can use.

Key concepts: Encoder-only models, BERT, Language understanding, Sentiment analysis, Toxicity classification, Bidirectional context

metadata.index_text: Slide 31. Encoder Only LLMs. Suited for language understanding tasks such as toxicity classification and sentiment analysis. Example: BERT.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide describes encoder-only language models and their typical tasks. Encoder-only models are suited for tasks that require understanding language rather than generating long new text. They process the whole input sequence and produce contextual representations that can be used for classification, tagging, similarity, or retrieval. The slide gives examples such as toxicity classification and sentiment analysis. In toxicity classification, the model reads a text and predicts whether it contains harmful or offensive language. In sentiment analysis, it reads text such as a tweet or review and predicts whether the sentiment is positive, negative, or neutral. The example model is BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. “Bidirectional” is important because BERT-style encoders can use context from both the left and right sides of a token when forming representations. This makes them strong for understanding tasks because each word is interpreted in the context of the full sentence. The slide mentions sentiment analysis of tweets as a practical example: the model would read a tweet and classify its emotional attitude. The key idea is that encoder-only models are not primarily designed to generate responses token by token. Instead, they transform input text into rich representations that downstream classifiers or task heads can use.

text:
Decoder-Only LLMs

This slide explains decoder-only LLMs, the model family most associated with modern text generation and chatbots. Decoder-only models are suited for generating language and content because they predict the next token based on previous tokens. The slide lists story writing, blog generation, open-domain question answering, and virtual assistants as examples. In story writing, the model continues or creates narrative text. In blog generation, it produces structured informational content. In open-domain question answering, it attempts to answer questions across many topics. In virtual assistants and chatbots, it generates conversational responses based on the user’s prompt and conversation history. The example given is GPT-3, or Generative Pretrained Transformer 3. GPT-style models are pretrained on large amounts of text using next-token prediction, which teaches them to generate fluent continuations. During interaction, the user’s prompt becomes the context, and the model generates one token at a time until the answer is complete. Unlike encoder-only models, decoder-only models usually use causal or masked self-attention, meaning they cannot look at future tokens when predicting the next token. This makes them naturally aligned with autoregressive generation. The slide’s key message is that when the task requires producing new text, decoder-only LLMs are often the appropriate architecture.

Key concepts: Decoder-only models, GPT-3, Text generation, Chatbots, Open-domain QA, Autoregressive modeling

metadata.index_text: Slide 32. Decoder Only LLMs. Suited for generating language and content such as story writing blog generation open-domain Q&A and virtual assistants. Example: GPT-3.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains decoder-only LLMs, the model family most associated with modern text generation and chatbots. Decoder-only models are suited for generating language and content because they predict the next token based on previous tokens. The slide lists story writing, blog generation, open-domain question answering, and virtual assistants as examples. In story writing, the model continues or creates narrative text. In blog generation, it produces structured informational content. In open-domain question answering, it attempts to answer questions across many topics. In virtual assistants and chatbots, it generates conversational responses based on the user’s prompt and conversation history. The example given is GPT-3, or Generative Pretrained Transformer 3. GPT-style models are pretrained on large amounts of text using next-token prediction, which teaches them to generate fluent continuations. During interaction, the user’s prompt becomes the context, and the model generates one token at a time until the answer is complete. Unlike encoder-only models, decoder-only models usually use causal or masked self-attention, meaning they cannot look at future tokens when predicting the next token. This makes them naturally aligned with autoregressive generation. The slide’s key message is that when the task requires producing new text, decoder-only LLMs are often the appropriate architecture.

text:
Encoder-Decoder LLMs

This slide describes encoder-decoder LLMs, which combine the encoder and decoder components of the transformer architecture. These models are suited for tasks requiring both understanding and generation. The encoder reads and represents the input, while the decoder generates an output conditioned on that representation. The slide gives examples such as translation, text-to-code, and summarization. Translation requires understanding the meaning of a sentence in one language and generating an equivalent sentence in another. Summarization requires understanding a longer document and generating a shorter version that preserves key information. Text-to-code requires understanding a natural-language instruction and generating code that implements it. The example model is T5, or Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer. T5 frames many NLP tasks as text-to-text problems: the input is text, and the output is also text. This unified framing allows one architecture to handle multiple tasks. The slide’s main point is that encoder-decoder models are flexible because they separate input comprehension from output generation while connecting the two through attention. They are especially effective when the output should be tightly grounded in a specific input sequence, unlike open-ended decoder-only generation where the model mainly continues the prompt.

Key concepts: Encoder-decoder models, T5, Text-to-text, Translation, Summarization, Text-to-code

metadata.index_text: Slide 33. Encoder Decoder LLMs. Combine encoder and decoder components. Suited for understanding plus generation tasks such as translation text-to-code and summarization. Example: T5.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide describes encoder-decoder LLMs, which combine the encoder and decoder components of the transformer architecture. These models are suited for tasks requiring both understanding and generation. The encoder reads and represents the input, while the decoder generates an output conditioned on that representation. The slide gives examples such as translation, text-to-code, and summarization. Translation requires understanding the meaning of a sentence in one language and generating an equivalent sentence in another. Summarization requires understanding a longer document and generating a shorter version that preserves key information. Text-to-code requires understanding a natural-language instruction and generating code that implements it. The example model is T5, or Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer. T5 frames many NLP tasks as text-to-text problems: the input is text, and the output is also text. This unified framing allows one architecture to handle multiple tasks. The slide’s main point is that encoder-decoder models are flexible because they separate input comprehension from output generation while connecting the two through attention. They are especially effective when the output should be tightly grounded in a specific input sequence, unlike open-ended decoder-only generation where the model mainly continues the prompt.

text:
Multimodal LLMs

This slide introduces multimodal LLMs by contrasting them with unimodal LLMs. A unimodal LLM is trained on a single type of data, such as text. Because it only receives text during training and inference, it lacks direct visual context. For example, a text-only model can answer questions about images only if the image is described in text; it cannot inspect the image itself. Multimodal LLMs are broader systems that understand and generate content across multiple modalities, such as text, images, audio, video, or sensor data. A modality is a type or channel of information. By combining modalities, a model can answer questions about an image, describe a video, interpret speech, or connect visual and textual information. The slide then defines vision-language models as multimodal models that learn from images and text. These models are especially important because many real-world tasks involve both visual content and language: image captioning, visual question answering, document understanding, medical image explanation, and robotics instruction. The key educational point is that multimodal models extend LLM-style reasoning and generation beyond plain text. They allow AI systems to connect language with perception, making them more useful for tasks where the input is not only written words but also visual or sensory information.

Key concepts: Multimodal LLMs, Unimodal LLMs, Vision-language models, Images and text, Audio, Video, Sensor data

metadata.index_text: Slide 34. Multimodal LLMs. Unimodal LLMs use one modality such as text and lack visual context. Multimodal LLMs understand and generate across text images audio video and sensors. Vision-language models learn from images and text.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces multimodal LLMs by contrasting them with unimodal LLMs. A unimodal LLM is trained on a single type of data, such as text. Because it only receives text during training and inference, it lacks direct visual context. For example, a text-only model can answer questions about images only if the image is described in text; it cannot inspect the image itself. Multimodal LLMs are broader systems that understand and generate content across multiple modalities, such as text, images, audio, video, or sensor data. A modality is a type or channel of information. By combining modalities, a model can answer questions about an image, describe a video, interpret speech, or connect visual and textual information. The slide then defines vision-language models as multimodal models that learn from images and text. These models are especially important because many real-world tasks involve both visual content and language: image captioning, visual question answering, document understanding, medical image explanation, and robotics instruction. The key educational point is that multimodal models extend LLM-style reasoning and generation beyond plain text. They allow AI systems to connect language with perception, making them more useful for tasks where the input is not only written words but also visual or sensory information.

text:
Visual Language Models

This slide explains the main idea and components of visual language models. The central idea is to unify image and text representations and feed the combined representation to a textual decoder for generation. In practice, the model must convert an image into features that can be understood by a language model. The first main component is the vision encoder. It extracts image features and encodes them into a representation suitable for the language decoder. Examples include ResNet and Vision Transformer, or ViT. A vision encoder may identify visual patterns such as edges, shapes, objects, spatial layout, colors, and high-level semantic content. The second component is the language decoder. It receives the encoded visual features along with any textual prompt and generates language outputs such as descriptions, captions, or answers. Examples of language decoders include GPT-2 and GPT-3. The diagram shows image patches going into a visual encoder, then through a linear projection so that visual features align with the language model’s embedding space. Textual prompts are tokenized and embedded, then combined with visual tokens and passed through decoder blocks. Positional embeddings help the decoder interpret token order. The output is generated text. The slide is important because it shows that VLMs are often built by connecting a visual feature extractor to a language generator, allowing the model to respond linguistically to visual information.

Key concepts: Visual language models, Vision encoder, Language decoder, Image-text representation, Linear projection, Visual tokens, Caption generation

metadata.index_text: Slide 35. Visual Language Models. Main idea: unify image and text representation and feed to textual decoder. Components: vision encoder such as ResNet or ViT and language decoder such as GPT-2 or GPT-3.

It extracts image features and encodes them into a representation suitable for the language decoder. Examples include ResNet and Vision Transformer, or ViT. A vision encoder may identify visual patterns such as edges, shapes, objects, spatial layout, colors, and high-level semantic content. The second component is the language decoder. It receives the encoded visual features along with any textual prompt and generates language outputs such as descriptions, captions, or answers. Examples of language decoders include GPT-2 and GPT-3. The diagram shows image patches going into a visual encoder, then through a linear projection so that visual features align with the language model’s embedding space. Textual prompts are tokenized and embedded, then combined with visual tokens and passed through decoder blocks. Positional embeddings help the decoder interpret token order. The output is generated text. The slide is important because it shows that VLMs are often built by connecting a visual feature extractor to a language generator, allowing the model to respond linguistically to visual information.

text:
VLMs: Tasks and Examples

This slide demonstrates what a vision-language model can do using an image of cats and several task prompts. On the left, the slide lists different capabilities: object localization, zero-shot segmentation, zero-shot visual question answering, and one-shot learning with instructions. Object localization asks whether one cat is behind another, requiring the model to understand spatial relationships in the image. The output states that one cat is behind the other and identifies which cat is facing the camera and which is facing away. Zero-shot segmentation asks the model to segment the striped cat. The corresponding output image highlights regions corresponding to cats, showing that the model can identify and localize visual objects without being trained specifically on that exact instruction at inference time. Zero-shot visual question answering asks about the breed of the cats, and the model answers that they appear to be domestic shorthair cats. The one-shot learning example provides extra instruction: striped cats are called tabby cats, then asks what breed the cats are. The model uses that instruction and answers that the cats are tabby cats, also explaining that tabbies have distinctive coat patterns. The slide shows that VLMs are not limited to captioning. They can combine visual perception, language understanding, instruction following, segmentation-like localization, and reasoning over image content. The examples also illustrate zero-shot and one-shot behavior, where the model performs tasks from prompts without task-specific retraining.

Key concepts: Vision-language models, Object localization, Zero-shot segmentation, Visual question answering, One-shot learning, Instruction following

metadata.index_text: Slide 36. VLMs. Example with cats shows object localization, zero-shot segmentation, zero-shot visual QA, and one-shot learning with instructions. The model answers spatial questions segments striped cat and identifies cat type.

On the left, the slide lists different capabilities: object localization, zero-shot segmentation, zero-shot visual question answering, and one-shot learning with instructions. Object localization asks whether one cat is behind another, requiring the model to understand spatial relationships in the image. The output states that one cat is behind the other and identifies which cat is facing the camera and which is facing away. Zero-shot segmentation asks the model to segment the striped cat. The corresponding output image highlights regions corresponding to cats, showing that the model can identify and localize visual objects without being trained specifically on that exact instruction at inference time. Zero-shot visual question answering asks about the breed of the cats, and the model answers that they appear to be domestic shorthair cats. The one-shot learning example provides extra instruction: striped cats are called tabby cats, then asks what breed the cats are. The model uses that instruction and answers that the cats are tabby cats, also explaining that tabbies have distinctive coat patterns. The slide shows that VLMs are not limited to captioning. They can combine visual perception, language understanding, instruction following, segmentation-like localization, and reasoning over image content. The examples also illustrate zero-shot and one-shot behavior, where the model performs tasks from prompts without task-specific retraining.

text:
Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP)

This slide introduces CLIP, or Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining, and explains how it differs from traditional vision-language models. Traditional vision-language models may generate captions or textual descriptions for images. CLIP, however, does not primarily generate text descriptions. Instead, it learns a joint representation space where images and text can be compared directly. This means an image and a text description can each be converted into embeddings, and their similarity can be measured numerically. If the image shows a dog and the text says “a photo of a dog,” their embeddings should be close. If the text says “a car on a road,” the embeddings should be farther apart. The slide also explains why this is useful for downstream tasks. One important capability is zero-shot image classification: the model can classify an image into one of several classes without being specifically trained on those classes as a traditional classifier. It can compare the image embedding to text prompts representing candidate labels and choose the closest one. The slide also mentions zero-shot image retrieval and text-based image generation. Retrieval means finding images that match a text query or finding text that matches an image. The main concept is that CLIP learns alignment between language and vision, making it a powerful foundation for tasks that require comparing images and text rather than directly generating text.

Key concepts: CLIP, Contrastive learning, Joint representation space, Image-text similarity, Zero-shot classification, Image retrieval

metadata.index_text: Slide 37. CLIP. Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining learns a joint image-text representation space. It does not generate captions; it compares images and text directly and enables zero-shot classification retrieval and text-based generation.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces CLIP, or Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining, and explains how it differs from traditional vision-language models. Traditional vision-language models may generate captions or textual descriptions for images. CLIP, however, does not primarily generate text descriptions. Instead, it learns a joint representation space where images and text can be compared directly. This means an image and a text description can each be converted into embeddings, and their similarity can be measured numerically. If the image shows a dog and the text says “a photo of a dog,” their embeddings should be close. If the text says “a car on a road,” the embeddings should be farther apart. The slide also explains why this is useful for downstream tasks. One important capability is zero-shot image classification: the model can classify an image into one of several classes without being specifically trained on those classes as a traditional classifier. It can compare the image embedding to text prompts representing candidate labels and choose the closest one. The slide also mentions zero-shot image retrieval and text-based image generation. Retrieval means finding images that match a text query or finding text that matches an image. The main concept is that CLIP learns alignment between language and vision, making it a powerful foundation for tasks that require comparing images and text rather than directly generating text.

text:
CLIP: Contrastive Pretraining Framework

This slide shows the CLIP training mechanism in more detail. CLIP jointly trains a text encoder and an image encoder to predict the correct image-text pair. On the left, a text input such as a phrase or caption is passed into a text encoder, shown as a transformer, producing text embeddings. An image is passed into an image encoder, such as ResNet or ViT, producing image embeddings. The center matrix represents pairwise similarities between multiple text embeddings and multiple image embeddings. The diagonal cells correspond to correct image-text pairs, while the off-diagonal cells correspond to mismatched pairs. The training objective is contrastive: maximize cosine similarity for correct image-text pairs and minimize cosine similarity for dissimilar pairs. Cosine similarity measures how close two vectors point in the embedding space. If the image and caption match, their vectors should be aligned. If they do not match, their vectors should be pushed apart. This training approach allows the model to learn semantic alignment from natural language supervision: captions and image-text pairs teach the model what visual concepts correspond to what words. The citation at the bottom refers to the CLIP paper by Radford and colleagues, titled “Learning transferable visual models from natural language supervision.” The educational takeaway is that CLIP learns by comparing many candidate pairs, not by generating captions. This makes it especially useful as a representation model for retrieval, classification, and multimodal matching.

Key concepts: CLIP training, Text encoder, Image encoder, Cosine similarity, Contrastive pretraining, Correct pairs, Non-diagonal negatives

text:
Prompting and Prompt Engineering

This slide is a section divider introducing prompting and prompt engineering. Prompting refers to the way users provide instructions, questions, examples, or context to a language model. Prompt engineering is the practice of designing those prompts carefully so that the model produces more accurate, useful, structured, or reliable completions. This section follows naturally from the earlier discussion of prompts and completions because interacting with an LLM is not only about the model itself; it is also about how the task is expressed. A vague prompt may produce a vague answer, while a detailed prompt with role, context, format, and examples can guide the model toward the desired behavior. The slide does not contain additional details, but it signals that the lecture will now focus on how to use LLMs during inference rather than how they are trained. This includes zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting, as well as configuration parameters that control generation behavior.

Key concepts: Prompting, Prompt engineering, LLM inference, Instructions, Context design

metadata.index_text: Slide 39. Prompting and prompt engineering. Section divider introducing how prompts are designed to guide LLM outputs.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide is a section divider introducing prompting and prompt engineering. Prompting refers to the way users provide instructions, questions, examples, or context to a language model. Prompt engineering is the practice of designing those prompts carefully so that the model produces more accurate, useful, structured, or reliable completions. This section follows naturally from the earlier discussion of prompts and completions because interacting with an LLM is not only about the model itself; it is also about how the task is expressed. A vague prompt may produce a vague answer, while a detailed prompt with role, context, format, and examples can guide the model toward the desired behavior. The slide does not contain additional details, but it signals that the lecture will now focus on how to use LLMs during inference rather than how they are trained. This includes zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting, as well as configuration parameters that control generation behavior.

text:
Prompting and Prompt Engineering: Prompt, Model, Completion, and Context Window

This slide revisits the prompt-completion workflow in the context of prompt engineering. The example asks, “Where is Ganymede located in the solar system?” The prompt is sent to the LLM, and the model returns a completion explaining that Ganymede is a moon of Jupiter located within Jupiter’s orbit. The diagram labels the three main parts: prompt, model, and completion. This reinforces that the prompt is not merely a question; it is the full input the model sees, including instructions, examples, formatting requirements, and any relevant context. The completion is the model’s generated response. The slide also mentions the context window, typically a few thousand words. In prompt engineering, the context window is a practical constraint because all useful instructions and supporting information must fit inside it. If the context is too long, some information may need to be summarized, selected, or retrieved dynamically. The example is factual and direct, but the same pattern applies to complex tasks such as classification, summarization, reasoning, coding, and tutoring. The educational lesson is that the quality of an LLM output depends on both the model and the prompt. A well-designed prompt can specify the task, expected answer style, constraints, and examples, increasing the chance that the completion matches the user’s goal.

Key concepts: Prompt engineering, Prompt, Completion, Context window, LLM response generation

metadata.slide_title: Prompting and Prompt Engineering: Prompt, Model, Completion, and Context Window

text:
In-Context Learning: Zero-Shot Inference

This slide introduces zero-shot inference as a form of in-context learning. The prompt asks the LLM to classify the review “I loved this movie!” and provides the label field “Sentiment:” without giving any examples. The model returns the completion “Positive.” Zero-shot means the model is asked to perform the task directly from the instruction and the input, with zero task examples included in the prompt. The model relies on knowledge and patterns learned during pretraining and instruction tuning to understand what sentiment classification means. The diagram shows prompt, model, and completion, emphasizing that the task is performed at inference time without updating model parameters. This is important because in-context learning does not train the model in the traditional gradient-descent sense; instead, the model adapts its behavior based on the context provided in the prompt. The example is simple: the phrase “I loved this movie” clearly expresses positive sentiment, so the model can infer the correct label. Zero-shot prompting is useful when the task is straightforward or when the model already understands the task well. However, it can fail if the instruction is ambiguous, the label format is unclear, or the task is specialized. This motivates one-shot and few-shot prompting, where examples are added to guide the model.

Key concepts: In-context learning, Zero-shot inference, Sentiment classification, Prompt, Completion, No examples

metadata.index_text: Slide 41. In-context learning zero-shot inference. Prompt asks classify review I loved this movie Sentiment. LLM returns Positive without examples.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces zero-shot inference as a form of in-context learning. The prompt asks the LLM to classify the review “I loved this movie!” and provides the label field “Sentiment:” without giving any examples. The model returns the completion “Positive.” Zero-shot means the model is asked to perform the task directly from the instruction and the input, with zero task examples included in the prompt. The model relies on knowledge and patterns learned during pretraining and instruction tuning to understand what sentiment classification means. The diagram shows prompt, model, and completion, emphasizing that the task is performed at inference time without updating model parameters. This is important because in-context learning does not train the model in the traditional gradient-descent sense; instead, the model adapts its behavior based on the context provided in the prompt. The example is simple: the phrase “I loved this movie” clearly expresses positive sentiment, so the model can infer the correct label. Zero-shot prompting is useful when the task is straightforward or when the model already understands the task well. However, it can fail if the instruction is ambiguous, the label format is unclear, or the task is specialized. This motivates one-shot and few-shot prompting, where examples are added to guide the model.

text:
Zero-Shot Inference Failure Example

This slide shows that zero-shot inference can sometimes fail or produce an irrelevant completion. The prompt again asks the LLM to classify “I loved this movie!” with the field “Sentiment:” but the completion shown is strange: “eived a very nice book review,” rather than a clean sentiment label. This illustrates an important limitation of prompting. Even when a task seems simple, a model may misunderstand the expected output format, continue the text in an unintended way, or generate content that does not match the requested label. The slide is likely demonstrating why prompt design matters. If the prompt is too minimal, the model may not reliably know whether it should output only “Positive/Negative,” write a sentence, complete an unfinished phrase, or generate something else. In practice, we can reduce this problem by making the instruction clearer, specifying allowed labels, giving examples, or requiring a strict output format. For instance, a better prompt might say: “Classify the sentiment of the review as exactly one of: Positive, Negative, or Neutral. Review: I loved this movie. Sentiment:” This slide therefore teaches that zero-shot prompting is convenient but not always robust. The absence of examples and format constraints can lead to unexpected completions, especially when the model interprets the prompt as a continuation rather than a classification task.

Key concepts: Zero-shot inference, Prompt ambiguity, Output format, Prompt failure, In-context learning limitations

metadata.index_text: Slide 42. Zero-shot inference failure. Prompt asks for sentiment but completion is irrelevant or malformed. Demonstrates that minimal prompts can fail without clear format or examples.

Sentiment:” This slide therefore teaches that zero-shot prompting is convenient but not always robust. The absence of examples and format constraints can lead to unexpected completions, especially when the model interprets the prompt as a continuation rather than a classification task.

text:
In-Context Learning: One-Shot Inference

This slide explains one-shot inference, where the prompt includes one example before asking the model to solve a new case. The prompt first shows “Classify this review: I loved this movie! Sentiment: Positive.” This example teaches the model both the task and the expected answer format. Then the prompt gives a new review: “I don’t like this chair. Sentiment:” The model completes it with “Negative.” One-shot prompting is a form of in-context learning because the model uses the example inside the prompt as guidance at inference time. No model weights are updated; the model simply conditions on the example and imitates the demonstrated pattern. Compared with zero-shot prompting, one-shot prompting can improve reliability because it clarifies the label set, the formatting, and the intended mapping from review text to sentiment. The example also shows how the model can transfer from one domain to another: the demonstration is about a movie, while the test case is about a chair. The task remains sentiment classification, so the model applies the same concept. The educational takeaway is that a single well-chosen example can significantly improve model behavior, especially when the task output should follow a specific structure. However, one example may not cover all edge cases, which is why few-shot prompting can be even more robust.

Key concepts: One-shot inference, In-context learning, Demonstration example, Sentiment classification, Prompt format

metadata.index_text: Slide 43. In-context learning one-shot inference. Prompt includes one labeled example I loved this movie Sentiment Positive then asks I don't like this chair. LLM returns Negative.

Then the prompt gives a new review: “I don’t like this chair. Sentiment:” The model completes it with “Negative.” One-shot prompting is a form of in-context learning because the model uses the example inside the prompt as guidance at inference time. No model weights are updated; the model simply conditions on the example and imitates the demonstrated pattern. The task remains sentiment classification, so the model applies the same concept. The educational takeaway is that a single well-chosen example can significantly improve model behavior, especially when the task output should follow a specific structure. However, one example may not cover all edge cases, which is why few-shot prompting can be even more robust.

text:
In-Context Learning: Few-Shot Inference

This slide explains few-shot inference, where the prompt includes several labeled examples before the model completes a new example. The prompt shows multiple review-sentiment pairs: “I loved this DVD!” labeled Positive, “I don’t like this chair.” labeled Negative, and then asks the model to classify “This is not great.” The model should infer from the examples that it should output a sentiment label, and because “not great” expresses dissatisfaction, the expected completion is Negative. The slide’s structure illustrates pattern learning inside the prompt. The model sees repeated examples of the input-output format and uses them as a temporary task specification. Few-shot prompting can be more reliable than zero-shot or one-shot prompting because it gives the model multiple examples of the label mapping and format. It can also show variation: positive wording, negative wording, different product types, and subtle expressions. The phrase “few-shot” usually means a small number of examples, often more than one but still small enough to fit in the context window. This method is useful when the model already has general language ability but needs guidance for a specific task format or domain. The limitation is that examples consume context window space, so there is a trade-off between including more demonstrations and leaving room for the actual task input.

Key concepts: Few-shot inference, In-context learning, Examples in prompt, Sentiment labels, Pattern learning, Context window

metadata.index_text: Slide 44. In-context learning few-shot inference. Prompt includes several labeled sentiment examples and asks the model to classify This is not great. Few-shot examples guide format and label choice.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains few-shot inference, where the prompt includes several labeled examples before the model completes a new example. The prompt shows multiple review-sentiment pairs: “I loved this DVD!” labeled Positive, “I don’t like this chair.” labeled Negative, and then asks the model to classify “This is not great.” The model should infer from the examples that it should output a sentiment label, and because “not great” expresses dissatisfaction, the expected completion is Negative. The slide’s structure illustrates pattern learning inside the prompt. The model sees repeated examples of the input-output format and uses them as a temporary task specification. Few-shot prompting can be more reliable than zero-shot or one-shot prompting because it gives the model multiple examples of the label mapping and format. It can also show variation: positive wording, negative wording, different product types, and subtle expressions. The phrase “few-shot” usually means a small number of examples, often more than one but still small enough to fit in the context window. This method is useful when the model already has general language ability but needs guidance for a specific task format or domain. The limitation is that examples consume context window space, so there is a trade-off between including more demonstrations and leaving room for the actual task input.

text:
Summary of In-Context Learning

This slide summarizes zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting using sentiment classification examples. In the zero-shot prompt, the model receives only the task input and label field: “Classify this review: I loved this movie! Sentiment:” The model must infer the task without examples. In the one-shot prompt, one labeled example is provided first, such as “I loved this movie! Sentiment: Positive,” followed by a new review. This gives the model a template for both the task and output style. In the few-shot prompt, several labeled examples are provided, and the slide notes that few-shot can involve more than five or six examples. The diagram also shows the context window as the container that holds the prompt examples and task input. The key idea is that in-context learning uses the prompt as a temporary learning environment. Instead of retraining model weights, we place instructions and examples inside the input context so the model can infer what behavior is expected. The more examples we include, the more guidance the model receives, but the more context space we consume. Therefore, prompt design involves choosing examples that are representative, clear, and concise. This slide helps students compare the three prompting regimes and understand that they form a spectrum from no examples to multiple examples.

Key concepts: In-context learning, Zero-shot, One-shot, Few-shot, Prompt examples, Context window

metadata.index_text: Slide 45. Summary of in-context learning. Zero-shot uses no examples, one-shot uses one labeled example, few-shot uses multiple examples. All examples and task input fit inside the context window.

In the one-shot prompt, one labeled example is provided first, such as “I loved this movie! Sentiment: Positive,” followed by a new review. This gives the model a template for both the task and output style. In the few-shot prompt, several labeled examples are provided, and the slide notes that few-shot can involve more than five or six examples. The diagram also shows the context window as the container that holds the prompt examples and task input. The key idea is that in-context learning uses the prompt as a temporary learning environment. Instead of retraining model weights, we place instructions and examples inside the input context so the model can infer what behavior is expected. The more examples we include, the more guidance the model receives, but the more context space we consume. Therefore, prompt design involves choosing examples that are representative, clear, and concise. This slide helps students compare the three prompting regimes and understand that they form a spectrum from no examples to multiple examples.

text:
Generative Configuration Parameters for Inference

This slide is a section divider introducing inference-time configuration parameters for generative models. After explaining prompting and in-context learning, the lecture now turns to settings that control how the model generates text after it has produced a probability distribution over tokens. These parameters do not usually change the model’s learned weights; instead, they affect decoding behavior during generation. Important parameters include maximum new tokens, greedy versus sampling strategies, top-k sampling, top-p sampling, and temperature. These settings influence output length, randomness, diversity, determinism, and creativity. For example, a low-temperature greedy setting may produce predictable answers, while higher temperature and sampling may produce more varied outputs. The slide prepares students to understand that LLM behavior is not determined only by the prompt and the model; generation configuration also plays a major role. Two users can give the same prompt to the same model and get different outputs if their decoding settings differ.

Key concepts: Inference parameters, Generation configuration, Decoding, Sampling, Temperature, Max new tokens

metadata.index_text: Slide 46. Generative configuration parameters for inference. Section divider introducing decoding settings that control output length randomness diversity and generation behavior.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide is a section divider introducing inference-time configuration parameters for generative models. After explaining prompting and in-context learning, the lecture now turns to settings that control how the model generates text after it has produced a probability distribution over tokens. These parameters do not usually change the model’s learned weights; instead, they affect decoding behavior during generation. Important parameters include maximum new tokens, greedy versus sampling strategies, top-k sampling, top-p sampling, and temperature. These settings influence output length, randomness, diversity, determinism, and creativity. For example, a low-temperature greedy setting may produce predictable answers, while higher temperature and sampling may produce more varied outputs. The slide prepares students to understand that LLM behavior is not determined only by the prompt and the model; generation configuration also plays a major role. Two users can give the same prompt to the same model and get different outputs if their decoding settings differ.

text:
Generative Configuration: Inference Parameters Interface

This slide shows a typical user interface for configuring text generation. The interface contains a prompt box labeled “Enter your prompt here,” a submit button, and several inference configuration parameters. The parameters shown are sample top K, sample top P, temperature, and max new tokens. Sample top K controls how many of the highest-probability tokens are considered during sampling. If top K is 25, the model samples only from the 25 most likely next tokens rather than the entire vocabulary. Sample top P controls nucleus sampling, where the model samples from the smallest set of high-probability tokens whose cumulative probability reaches a threshold p. Here it is shown as 1, which usually means no restriction from top-p because the cumulative probability can include the full distribution. Temperature controls how sharp or flat the probability distribution becomes before sampling. A temperature of 0.8 is slightly conservative but still allows some variation. Max new tokens is set to 200, meaning generation should stop after producing at most 200 new tokens unless another stopping condition occurs earlier. The slide teaches that inference configuration is part of practical LLM usage. The model produces token probabilities, but these parameters determine how the final text is selected from those probabilities.

Key concepts: Top-k, Top-p, Temperature, Max new tokens, Inference configuration, Sampling controls

metadata.index_text: Slide 47. Generative configuration inference parameters. Interface shows prompt input submit button sample top K 25 sample top P 1 temperature 0.8 and max new tokens 200.

The interface contains a prompt box labeled “Enter your prompt here,” a submit button, and several inference configuration parameters. The parameters shown are sample top K, sample top P, temperature, and max new tokens. Sample top K controls how many of the highest-probability tokens are considered during sampling. If top K is 25, the model samples only from the 25 most likely next tokens rather than the entire vocabulary. Sample top P controls nucleus sampling, where the model samples from the smallest set of high-probability tokens whose cumulative probability reaches a threshold p. Here it is shown as 1, which usually means no restriction from top-p because the cumulative probability can include the full distribution. Temperature controls how sharp or flat the probability distribution becomes before sampling. A temperature of 0.8 is slightly conservative but still allows some variation. Max new tokens is set to 200, meaning generation should stop after producing at most 200 new tokens unless another stopping condition occurs earlier. The slide teaches that inference configuration is part of practical LLM usage. The model produces token probabilities, but these parameters determine how the final text is selected from those probabilities.

text:
Generative Configuration: Max New Tokens

This slide explains the max_new_tokens parameter. The diagram shows a softmax output leading to different possible generation limits: max_new_tokens = 100, 200, or 150, and also a stop token. Max new tokens specifies the maximum number of tokens the model is allowed to generate after the prompt. A token may be a word, subword, punctuation mark, or piece of text depending on the tokenizer. This parameter controls output length and computational cost. If max_new_tokens is too small, the model may stop before completing its answer, producing an incomplete explanation or cut-off sentence. If it is too large, the model may generate unnecessarily long text, repeat itself, or consume more time and memory. The stop token represents another way generation can end: the model may produce a special end-of-sequence token or encounter a user-defined stopping pattern. In that case, generation can stop before reaching the maximum token limit. The slide’s main intuition is that max_new_tokens is a ceiling, not necessarily the exact output length. For example, setting it to 200 allows up to 200 generated tokens, but the model may stop earlier if it completes the response. This parameter is especially important in applications where output length must be controlled, such as chatbots, summarizers, APIs with cost limits, or structured JSON generation.

Key concepts: Max new tokens, Output length, Stop token, Generation limit, Token budget

metadata.index_text: Slide 48. Max new tokens. Controls the maximum number of tokens generated after the prompt. Examples show 100 200 and 150 token limits and stop token ending generation.

This parameter controls output length and computational cost. If max_new_tokens is too small, the model may stop before completing its answer, producing an incomplete explanation or cut-off sentence. In that case, generation can stop before reaching the maximum token limit. The slide’s main intuition is that max_new_tokens is a ceiling, not necessarily the exact output length. text:
Generative Configuration: Greedy vs Random Sampling

This slide compares greedy decoding with random weighted sampling. Both methods start from the model’s softmax output, which assigns probabilities to possible next tokens. The example distribution lists tokens such as cake with probability 0.20, donut with 0.10, banana with 0.02, and apple with 0.01. In greedy decoding, the model always selects the token with the highest probability. In the example, greedy decoding would choose “cake” because it has the largest probability. This makes output more deterministic and often more stable, but it can also make text repetitive or less creative. Random weighted sampling works differently: it samples a token randomly according to the probability distribution. A token with probability 0.20 has a 20% chance of being selected, but it is not guaranteed. The slide notes that even though “cake” has a 20% chance, “banana” was actually selected. This demonstrates that lower-probability tokens can still appear under sampling, which increases diversity. The key trade-off is reliability versus variety. Greedy decoding is predictable and chooses the model’s top preference at each step, while sampling can explore alternatives and produce more varied text. Understanding this difference is essential because many other decoding strategies, such as top-k, top-p, and temperature, modify how random sampling behaves.

Key concepts: Greedy decoding, Random sampling, Softmax probabilities, Token probability, Generation diversity, Determinism

metadata.slide_title: Generative Configuration: Greedy vs Random Sampling

In greedy decoding, the model always selects the token with the highest probability. In the example, greedy decoding would choose “cake” because it has the largest probability. Random weighted sampling works differently: it samples a token randomly according to the probability distribution. A token with probability 0.20 has a 20% chance of being selected, but it is not guaranteed. The slide notes that even though “cake” has a 20% chance, “banana” was actually selected. text:
Generative Configuration: Greedy vs Random Sampling Revisited

This slide repeats the comparison between greedy decoding and random weighted sampling, reinforcing the difference between deterministic and probabilistic token selection. The same softmax output distribution is shown with tokens like cake, donut, banana, and apple and their probabilities. In greedy decoding, the highest-probability token is always selected, so the output follows the most likely local path at every generation step. This can be useful for tasks requiring consistency, such as factual extraction or deterministic formatting, but it may reduce diversity. In random weighted sampling, the model treats the probability distribution as a sampling distribution. Higher-probability tokens are more likely, but lower-probability tokens remain possible. The slide again emphasizes that although “cake” has a 20% chance, “banana” may be selected in a particular sample. This repetition is useful because decoding strategy strongly affects model behavior. A student should understand that the model does not directly output words in a single fixed way; it outputs probabilities, and the decoding algorithm decides how to choose among them. The same model and prompt can therefore produce different completions depending on whether greedy decoding or sampling is used. This idea prepares the student for top-k and top-p sampling, which restrict the set of tokens before sampling.

Key concepts: Greedy decoding, Weighted sampling, Probability distribution, Token selection, Deterministic generation, Stochastic generation

metadata.slide_title: Generative Configuration: Greedy vs Random Sampling Revisited
metadata.index_text: Slide 50. Greedy vs random sampling repeated. Greedy always chooses highest probability word. Random weighted sampling can choose any token according to probabilities, creating varied outputs.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide repeats the comparison between greedy decoding and random weighted sampling, reinforcing the difference between deterministic and probabilistic token selection. The same softmax output distribution is shown with tokens like cake, donut, banana, and apple and their probabilities. In greedy decoding, the highest-probability token is always selected, so the output follows the most likely local path at every generation step. This can be useful for tasks requiring consistency, such as factual extraction or deterministic formatting, but it may reduce diversity. In random weighted sampling, the model treats the probability distribution as a sampling distribution. Higher-probability tokens are more likely, but lower-probability tokens remain possible. The slide again emphasizes that although “cake” has a 20% chance, “banana” may be selected in a particular sample. This repetition is useful because decoding strategy strongly affects model behavior. A student should understand that the model does not directly output words in a single fixed way; it outputs probabilities, and the decoding algorithm decides how to choose among them. The same model and prompt can therefore produce different completions depending on whether greedy decoding or sampling is used. This idea prepares the student for top-k and top-p sampling, which restrict the set of tokens before sampling.

text:
Generative Configuration: Top-k and Top-p

This slide introduces top-k and top-p sampling as two methods for controlling random generation. The interface again shows sample top K set to 25, sample top P set to 1, temperature set to 0.8, and max new tokens set to 200. Top-k and top-p are used after the model produces token probabilities. Their purpose is to restrict or shape the candidate token set before sampling, preventing the model from sampling from extremely unlikely tokens that might produce incoherent output. Top-k sampling keeps only the k highest-probability tokens. If k is 25, only the 25 most likely next tokens are considered, and all other vocabulary tokens are ignored for that step. Top-p sampling, also called nucleus sampling, keeps the smallest group of highest-probability tokens whose cumulative probability reaches p. If p is low, only a small high-confidence set is used; if p is 1, the full distribution may be allowed. These methods are important because pure random sampling over the entire vocabulary can sometimes select very unlikely tokens, while greedy decoding can be too rigid. Top-k and top-p offer middle ground: they preserve randomness and diversity but keep generation focused on plausible tokens. The slide prepares students for the next two slides, which explain each method visually.

Key concepts: Top-k sampling, Top-p sampling, Nucleus sampling, Sampling control, Inference parameters, Candidate tokens

Top-k sampling keeps only the k highest-probability tokens. If k is 25, only the 25 most likely next tokens are considered, and all other vocabulary tokens are ignored for that step. Top-p sampling, also called nucleus sampling, keeps the smallest group of highest-probability tokens whose cumulative probability reaches p. If p is low, only a small high-confidence set is used; if p is 1, the full distribution may be allowed. text:
Generative Configuration: Top-k Sampling

This slide explains top-k sampling using the same token probability distribution. The softmax output includes words such as cake, donut, banana, and apple with different probabilities. The slide defines top-k as selecting an output from the top k results after applying a random weighted strategy using the probabilities. In the example, k = 3, so only the three highest-probability tokens are kept as candidates. If the top three are cake, donut, and banana, then apple and the rest of the vocabulary are excluded from sampling for that step. The model then randomly samples among the remaining top-k tokens according to their probabilities, usually after renormalizing them so the remaining probabilities sum to 1. This gives a balance between greedy decoding and unrestricted sampling. Unlike greedy decoding, top-k sampling can choose something other than the single most probable token, which increases variation. Unlike unrestricted random sampling, it avoids very low-probability tokens that may be nonsensical. The choice of k matters. A small k makes generation more focused and deterministic, while a larger k allows more diversity but may introduce less likely words. This slide teaches that top-k is a simple rank-based filter: keep the k most likely tokens, then sample from them.

Key concepts: Top-k sampling, k value, Candidate filtering, Random weighted sampling, Token probabilities

metadata.index_text: Slide 52. Top-k sampling. With k=3, select from the top three highest probability tokens after softmax using random weighted sampling. Lower-ranked tokens are excluded.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains top-k sampling using the same token probability distribution. The softmax output includes words such as cake, donut, banana, and apple with different probabilities. The slide defines top-k as selecting an output from the top k results after applying a random weighted strategy using the probabilities. In the example, k = 3, so only the three highest-probability tokens are kept as candidates. If the top three are cake, donut, and banana, then apple and the rest of the vocabulary are excluded from sampling for that step. The model then randomly samples among the remaining top-k tokens according to their probabilities, usually after renormalizing them so the remaining probabilities sum to 1. This gives a balance between greedy decoding and unrestricted sampling. Unlike greedy decoding, top-k sampling can choose something other than the single most probable token, which increases variation. Unlike unrestricted random sampling, it avoids very low-probability tokens that may be nonsensical. The choice of k matters. A small k makes generation more focused and deterministic, while a larger k allows more diversity but may introduce less likely words. This slide teaches that top-k is a simple rank-based filter: keep the k most likely tokens, then sample from them.

text:
Generative Configuration: Top-p Sampling

This slide explains top-p sampling, also called nucleus sampling. Instead of keeping a fixed number of tokens like top-k, top-p keeps the top-ranked consecutive tokens whose cumulative probability is less than or equal to a probability threshold p. The example shows p = 0.30. The token probabilities include cake at 0.20, donut at 0.10, banana at 0.02, apple at 0.01, and so on. Starting from the highest probability token, cake contributes 0.20. Adding donut brings the cumulative probability to 0.30. Because this reaches the threshold, the sampling set contains cake and donut, while banana, apple, and the rest are excluded for that step. The model then samples from the retained set according to their probabilities. The advantage of top-p is that the number of candidate tokens adapts to the shape of the probability distribution. If the model is very confident and the top tokens carry most probability mass, the candidate set is small. If the distribution is flatter, more tokens may be included to reach p. This makes top-p more flexible than top-k. The slide teaches that top-p controls diversity by probability mass rather than by a fixed count of tokens.

Key concepts: Top-p sampling, Nucleus sampling, Cumulative probability, Probability threshold, Adaptive candidate set

metadata.index_text: Slide 53. Top-p sampling. Select top-ranked consecutive tokens by probability until cumulative probability reaches p. Example p=0.30 includes cake 0.20 and donut 0.10.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains top-p sampling, also called nucleus sampling. The example shows p = 0.30. The token probabilities include cake at 0.20, donut at 0.10, banana at 0.02, apple at 0.01, and so on. Starting from the highest probability token, cake contributes 0.20. Adding donut brings the cumulative probability to 0.30. Because this reaches the threshold, the sampling set contains cake and donut, while banana, apple, and the rest are excluded for that step. The model then samples from the retained set according to their probabilities. The advantage of top-p is that the number of candidate tokens adapts to the shape of the probability distribution. If the model is very confident and the top tokens carry most probability mass, the candidate set is small. If the distribution is flatter, more tokens may be included to reach p. This makes top-p more flexible than top-k. The slide teaches that top-p controls diversity by probability mass rather than by a fixed count of tokens.

text:
Generative Configuration: Temperature

This slide introduces temperature as another inference configuration parameter. The interface shows temperature set to 0.8 along with top K, top P, max new tokens, prompt input, and submit button. Temperature controls the shape of the probability distribution used for sampling. It does not change the model’s underlying knowledge, but it changes how strongly the model prefers high-probability tokens over lower-probability ones during decoding. A lower temperature makes the distribution sharper, meaning the highest-probability tokens become even more dominant. This usually produces more deterministic, focused, and conservative outputs. A higher temperature makes the distribution flatter, increasing the probability of less likely tokens and producing more diverse or creative outputs. A temperature around 1 often means the original distribution is used, while values below 1 reduce randomness and values above 1 increase randomness. The slide acts as an introduction before the next slide visually compares cooler and higher temperature distributions. For practical use, temperature is important because different tasks need different levels of creativity. Factual question answering, data extraction, and JSON formatting often benefit from low temperature. Brainstorming, creative writing, and ideation may benefit from higher temperature.

Key concepts: Temperature, Sampling, Randomness, Probability distribution, Inference control, Creativity

metadata.index_text: Slide 54. Temperature. Interface highlights temperature setting with top K top P and max new tokens. Temperature controls sharpness or flatness of token probabilities during generation.
