text:
Sample Computational Requirements

This slide uses Mixtral 8x7B to illustrate the computational difference between loading a sparse MoE model and running it. The model contains shared parameters such as embeddings, attention, router, and LM head, plus a large set of expert parameters. In the Load Model column, the listed components include embeddings, attention, router, experts, and LM head; the sparse parameter total is shown as 46.7B. This means many parameters must exist in memory when the model is loaded. In the Run Model column, however, the expert parameter count is smaller because only selected experts are active during a forward pass. The slide states "Use 2 experts at a time" and shows active parameters as 12.8B. The key lesson is that MoE models can have a large total parameter count but a much smaller active parameter count per token. This provides high capacity without requiring every parameter to be computed for every token, though memory is still needed to store the full model.

Key concepts: Mixtral 8x7B, Sparse parameters, Active parameters, Shared parameters, Expert parameters, MoE compute, Two active experts

text:
Lecture References

This final slide lists the main references used to prepare the lecture. The first reference is Hands-On Large Language Models by Jay Alammar and Maarten Grootendorst, a resource that explains modern LLM concepts with practical and visual explanations. The second reference is DeepLearning.AI's How Transformer LLMs Work course, which focuses on the internal mechanisms of transformer-based large language models. The presence of references is important academically because it tells students where the lecture content is grounded and where they can continue studying. It also shows that the lecture combines textbook-style explanation with structured online course material. For a student reviewing the lecture independently, these references are useful follow-up resources for topics such as tokenization, transformer blocks, attention, decoding, caching, modern model architectures, and mixture-of-experts systems.

text:
Pre-training and Scaling Laws

This opening slide introduces the lecture topic: pre-training and scaling laws for large language models. The title signals that the lecture will move beyond using an existing LLM and discuss how large models are initially trained from raw text, how their size and training data affect performance, and why compute becomes a central limitation. The visual of a human head connected to digital components, a CPU, gears, documents, and a monitor suggests the connection between human-like language behavior and computational infrastructure. The key message is that modern generative AI is not only about model architecture, but also about the engineering decisions that make training possible: data filtering, model size, numerical precision, distributed GPUs, and compute-efficient scaling.

Key concepts: pre-training, scaling laws, large language models, compute, model training

The visual of a human head connected to digital components, a CPU, gears, documents, and a monitor suggests the connection between human-like language behavior and computational infrastructure. The key message is that modern generative AI is not only about model architecture, but also about the engineering decisions that make training possible: data filtering, model size, numerical precision, distributed GPUs, and compute-efficient scaling.

text:
Generative AI Project Lifecycle

This slide places pre-training inside the broader generative AI project lifecycle. The diagram is organized as a pipeline with four major phases: Scope, Select, Adapt and align model, and Application integration. In the Scope phase, the project begins by defining the use case, because the task determines what kind of model, data, and evaluation will be needed. In the Select phase, the practitioner chooses whether to use an existing model or pre-train a model from scratch. The Adapt and align phase includes prompt engineering, fine-tuning, alignment with human feedback, and evaluation, showing that using an LLM usually requires several refinement steps. Finally, Application integration covers optimizing and deploying the model for inference, then augmenting it to build LLM-powered applications. The slide emphasizes that pre-training is only one decision in a full system-building process.

Key concepts: project lifecycle, scope, model selection, fine-tuning, deployment, application integration

Finally, Application integration covers optimizing and deploying the model for inference, then augmenting it to build LLM-powered applications. The slide emphasizes that pre-training is only one decision in a full system-building process.

text:
Generative AI Project Lifecycle: Model Selection Focus

This slide repeats the generative AI project lifecycle to highlight the model-selection stage. The visual structure again shows a left-to-right workflow: define the use case, choose an existing model or pre-train your own, adapt and align the model, evaluate it, deploy it, and build applications around it. The important emphasis here is the transition from problem definition to model choice. A project may not always need a custom pre-trained model; often, an existing foundation model can be adapted through prompting, fine-tuning, or alignment. However, if the use case has special domain language, privacy constraints, uncommon modalities, or very high performance requirements, pre-training or continued pre-training may become relevant. The slide prepares the student to think of pre-training as a strategic choice, not a default step.

Key concepts: model selection, foundation model, pre-training decision, adaptation, evaluation

metadata.slide_title: Generative AI Project Lifecycle: Model Selection Focus

A project may not always need a custom pre-trained model; often, an existing foundation model can be adapted through prompting, fine-tuning, or alignment. The slide prepares the student to think of pre-training as a strategic choice, not a default step.

text:
Considerations for Choosing a Model

This slide introduces the main decision between using a pretrained foundation model and training a custom LLM. The left side refers to a pretrained LLM or foundation model, which is a general-purpose model already trained on large datasets and made available for reuse. The right side refers to training your own model, which leads to a custom LLM. The diagram suggests a spectrum: using an existing foundation model is usually faster and cheaper, while training a custom model gives more control but requires much more data, compute, and engineering. The main lesson is that model choice depends on constraints such as task specificity, available data, budget, performance needs, and whether the model must understand a specialized domain.

Key concepts: pretrained LLM, foundation model, custom LLM, training from scratch, model choice

metadata.index_text: Slide 4 | Considerations for choosing a model | pretrained foundation model versus custom LLM trained from scratch

The right side refers to training your own model, which leads to a custom LLM. The diagram suggests a spectrum: using an existing foundation model is usually faster and cheaper, while training a custom model gives more control but requires much more data, compute, and engineering. The main lesson is that model choice depends on constraints such as task specificity, available data, budget, performance needs, and whether the model must understand a specialized domain.

text:
Considerations for Choosing a Model: Foundation vs Custom

This slide reinforces the comparison between a foundation model and a custom LLM. A foundation model is already trained and can often be adapted with prompting, retrieval, fine-tuning, or alignment, which makes it practical for many projects. A custom LLM, by contrast, is created by training a model on selected data, giving the developer more control over vocabulary, domain coverage, architecture, and behavior. The visual arrangement shows that the custom path is connected to the phrase 'train your own model', while the foundation path is connected to 'pretrained LLM'. The student should understand that custom training is not simply better; it is more expensive and only justified when existing models cannot meet the requirements.

Key concepts: foundation model, custom model, pretrained model, domain specialization, training cost

text:
Model Architectures and Pre-training Objectives

This transition slide announces the next topic: model architectures and pre-training objectives. In LLMs, architecture describes the structural design of the model, such as encoder-only, decoder-only, or encoder-decoder Transformer designs. The pre-training objective describes the task used to teach the model from raw data before it is adapted to downstream use cases. For example, an encoder-only model may be trained to reconstruct masked words, while a decoder-only model may be trained to predict the next token. This distinction is important because the objective shapes what the model becomes good at: understanding, generation, or transforming one sequence into another.

Key concepts: architecture, pre-training objective, encoder, decoder, Transformer

metadata.index_text: Slide 6 | Model architectures and pre-training objectives | how architecture and training task shape LLM behavior
metadata.slide_paragraph: This transition slide announces the next topic: model architectures and pre-training objectives. In LLMs, architecture describes the structural design of the model, such as encoder-only, decoder-only, or encoder-decoder Transformer designs. The pre-training objective describes the task used to teach the model from raw data before it is adapted to downstream use cases. For example, an encoder-only model may be trained to reconstruct masked words, while a decoder-only model may be trained to predict the next token. This distinction is important because the objective shapes what the model becomes good at: understanding, generation, or transforming one sequence into another.

text:
LLM Pre-training at a High Level

This slide shows the high-level pre-training pipeline. On the left, massive unstructured text data is collected at GB, TB, or PB scale. Before the data reaches the model, it passes through a data quality filter, and the slide states that only about 1–3% of the original tokens may remain. This highlights that data cleaning is a major part of LLM training: low-quality, duplicated, unsafe, or irrelevant text must be removed. The filtered text is then used to train the LLM on GPUs. On the right, the vocabulary table shows token strings, token IDs, and embedding or vector representations. This means raw text is not fed to the model as characters directly; it is tokenized into units, mapped to numeric IDs, and represented as dense vectors that the neural network can process.

Key concepts: pre-training pipeline, data quality filtering, tokens, vocabulary, embeddings, GPU training

This highlights that data cleaning is a major part of LLM training: low-quality, duplicated, unsafe, or irrelevant text must be removed. The filtered text is then used to train the LLM on GPUs. On the right, the vocabulary table shows token strings, token IDs, and embedding or vector representations. This means raw text is not fed to the model as characters directly; it is tokenized into units, mapped to numeric IDs, and represented as dense vectors that the neural network can process.

text:
Transformers

This slide summarizes the three major Transformer families used in language modeling. Encoder-only models take an input and produce output representations, making them suitable for understanding tasks. Decoder-only models take an input sequence and generate output tokens autoregressively, making them common for generative LLMs. Encoder-decoder models include both components: the encoder reads and represents the input sequence, while the decoder generates a related output sequence. The diagram visually separates these designs into three columns, showing which components are present in each architecture. The key point is that all three are Transformer-based, but they are optimized for different pre-training objectives and downstream tasks.

Key concepts: encoder-only, decoder-only, encoder-decoder, Transformer families, language modeling

metadata.index_text: Slide 8 | Transformers | encoder only, decoder only, and encoder decoder architectures
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes the three major Transformer families used in language modeling. Encoder-only models take an input and produce output representations, making them suitable for understanding tasks. Decoder-only models take an input sequence and generate output tokens autoregressively, making them common for generative LLMs. Encoder-decoder models include both components: the encoder reads and represents the input sequence, while the decoder generates a related output sequence. The diagram visually separates these designs into three columns, showing which components are present in each architecture. The key point is that all three are Transformer-based, but they are optimized for different pre-training objectives and downstream tasks.

text:
Autoencoding Models

This slide explains encoder-only LLMs trained with masked language modeling. The original sentence shown is 'The teacher teaches the student.' In masked language modeling, some tokens are hidden or replaced with a mask, and the model learns to reconstruct the missing text. The slide labels the objective as reconstructing text, or denoising, because the model receives a corrupted version of the input and must recover the original. It also emphasizes bidirectional context: the encoder can look at both the words before and after a masked token. This is why autoencoding models are strong for understanding tasks, because each token representation can be informed by the full surrounding sentence.

Key concepts: autoencoding, encoder-only LLM, masked language modeling, denoising, bidirectional context

metadata.index_text: Slide 9 | Autoencoding models | encoder-only LLM, MLM objective, reconstruct masked text, bidirectional context
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains encoder-only LLMs trained with masked language modeling. The original sentence shown is 'The teacher teaches the student.' In masked language modeling, some tokens are hidden or replaced with a mask, and the model learns to reconstruct the missing text. The slide labels the objective as reconstructing text, or denoising, because the model receives a corrupted version of the input and must recover the original. It also emphasizes bidirectional context: the encoder can look at both the words before and after a masked token. This is why autoencoding models are strong for understanding tasks, because each token representation can be informed by the full surrounding sentence.

text:
Autoencoding Models: Use Cases

This slide lists the typical use cases and examples of autoencoding models. Because these models learn rich bidirectional representations of text, they are well suited for tasks that require understanding rather than free-form generation. Sentiment analysis requires recognizing whether text expresses positive or negative opinion. Named entity recognition requires identifying spans such as people, organizations, and locations. Word classification assigns labels to words or tokens based on their context. The examples given are BERT and RoBERTa, both of which are encoder-based representation models. The slide teaches that if the task is mainly classification or extraction, an encoder-style model is often more appropriate than a purely generative decoder model.

Key concepts: sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, word classification, BERT, RoBERTa, representation learning

metadata.index_text: Slide 10 | Autoencoding use cases | sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, word classification, BERT, RoBERTa
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide lists the typical use cases and examples of autoencoding models. Because these models learn rich bidirectional representations of text, they are well suited for tasks that require understanding rather than free-form generation. Sentiment analysis requires recognizing whether text expresses positive or negative opinion. Named entity recognition requires identifying spans such as people, organizations, and locations. Word classification assigns labels to words or tokens based on their context. The examples given are BERT and RoBERTa, both of which are encoder-based representation models. The slide teaches that if the task is mainly classification or extraction, an encoder-style model is often more appropriate than a purely generative decoder model.

text:
Autoregressive Models

This slide explains decoder-only LLMs trained with causal language modeling. The sentence 'The teacher teaches the student' is used to show next-token prediction: given previous tokens such as 'The teacher', the model predicts the next token, such as 'teaches'. The objective is labeled as predicting the next token, and the context is labeled unidirectional because the model is only allowed to use earlier tokens, not future ones. This is essential for generation: when writing text left to right, the model cannot know future words in advance. The diagram on the right reinforces the decoder-only structure, where outputs are generated sequentially. This training objective is the foundation of many modern generative LLMs.

Key concepts: autoregressive modeling, causal language modeling, decoder-only LLM, next-token prediction, unidirectional context

metadata.index_text: Slide 11 | Autoregressive models | CLM objective, decoder-only LLM, predict next token, unidirectional context
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains decoder-only LLMs trained with causal language modeling. The sentence 'The teacher teaches the student' is used to show next-token prediction: given previous tokens such as 'The teacher', the model predicts the next token, such as 'teaches'. The objective is labeled as predicting the next token, and the context is labeled unidirectional because the model is only allowed to use earlier tokens, not future ones. This is essential for generation: when writing text left to right, the model cannot know future words in advance. The diagram on the right reinforces the decoder-only structure, where outputs are generated sequentially. This training objective is the foundation of many modern generative LLMs.

text:
Autoregressive Models: Use Cases

This slide lists the main strengths of autoregressive models. Their natural use case is text generation because they are trained to predict the next token repeatedly, forming complete sentences, paragraphs, answers, or code. The slide also mentions 'other emergent behavior', which refers to capabilities that appear as model size increases, such as following instructions, performing simple reasoning, translating, summarizing, or answering questions, even when those exact tasks were not explicitly programmed as separate modules. The examples given are GPT and BLOOM, both decoder-only style large language models. The key point is that generative behavior arises directly from the next-token prediction objective combined with scale and diverse training data.

Key concepts: text generation, emergent behavior, GPT, BLOOM, decoder-only models

metadata.index_text: Slide 12 | Autoregressive use cases | text generation, emergent behavior, GPT, BLOOM
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide lists the main strengths of autoregressive models. Their natural use case is text generation because they are trained to predict the next token repeatedly, forming complete sentences, paragraphs, answers, or code. The slide also mentions 'other emergent behavior', which refers to capabilities that appear as model size increases, such as following instructions, performing simple reasoning, translating, summarizing, or answering questions, even when those exact tasks were not explicitly programmed as separate modules. The examples given are GPT and BLOOM, both decoder-only style large language models. The key point is that generative behavior arises directly from the next-token prediction objective combined with scale and diverse training data.

text:
Sequence-to-Sequence Models

This slide introduces encoder-decoder models trained with sequence-to-sequence objectives, specifically span corruption. The original text is again based on 'The teacher teaches the student.' Instead of masking a single word, a span of text is replaced with a sentinel token, shown as a special placeholder. The encoder reads the corrupted input, while the decoder reconstructs the missing span. This differs from standard masked language modeling because the model is not just labeling a hidden token in place; it is generating a sequence that fills the removed region. The slide therefore connects encoder-decoder architecture with tasks where an input sequence must be transformed into a new output sequence.

Key concepts: sequence-to-sequence, encoder-decoder LLM, span corruption, sentinel token, reconstruct span

metadata.index_text: Slide 13 | Sequence-to-sequence models | encoder-decoder, span corruption, sentinel token, reconstruct missing span
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces encoder-decoder models trained with sequence-to-sequence objectives, specifically span corruption. The encoder reads the corrupted input, while the decoder reconstructs the missing span. This differs from standard masked language modeling because the model is not just labeling a hidden token in place; it is generating a sequence that fills the removed region. The slide therefore connects encoder-decoder architecture with tasks where an input sequence must be transformed into a new output sequence.

text:
Sequence-to-Sequence Models: Use Cases

This slide lists the tasks where encoder-decoder sequence-to-sequence models are especially useful. Translation is a natural example because the input is a sentence in one language and the output is a sentence in another language. Text summarization also fits this architecture because the model reads a longer input document and generates a shorter output. Question answering can be formulated similarly: the input may include a question and context, and the output is the answer. The example models are T5 and BART, both of which are known for text-to-text framing. The key message is that encoder-decoder models are suitable when the task requires both strong input understanding and controlled output generation.

Key concepts: translation, summarization, question answering, T5, BART, encoder-decoder

metadata.index_text: Slide 14 | Sequence-to-sequence use cases | translation, summarization, question answering, T5, BART

Question answering can be formulated similarly: the input may include a question and context, and the output is the answer. The example models are T5 and BART, both of which are known for text-to-text framing. The key message is that encoder-decoder models are suitable when the task requires both strong input understanding and controlled output generation.

text:
Model Architectures and Pre-training Objectives Summary

This slide compares three architecture-objective combinations in one place. Autoencoding uses an encoder-only LLM and a masked language modeling objective: the input contains a mask, and the target is the original text. Decoder-only autoregressive modeling uses causal language modeling: given earlier tokens such as 'The teacher', the model predicts the next token, such as 'teaches'. Sequence-to-sequence modeling uses an encoder-decoder architecture with span corruption: a span is removed and replaced by a placeholder, and the model generates the missing span. The slide is important because it shows that architecture and training objective are linked. A model becomes useful for particular tasks because the pre-training objective teaches it a particular kind of language behavior.

Key concepts: MLM, CLM, span corruption, encoder-only, decoder-only, encoder-decoder

metadata.slide_title: Model Architectures and Pre-training Objectives Summary
metadata.index_text: Slide 15 | Architecture and pre-training objective summary | autoencoding MLM, autoregressive CLM, seq-to-seq span corruption

Sequence-to-sequence modeling uses an encoder-decoder architecture with span corruption: a span is removed and replaced by a placeholder, and the model generates the missing span. The slide is important because it shows that architecture and training objective are linked. A model becomes useful for particular tasks because the pre-training objective teaches it a particular kind of language behavior.

text:
The Significance of Scale: Task Ability

This slide contrasts BERT-base with 110 million parameters and BLOOM with 176 billion parameters to illustrate the significance of scale. The visual emphasis is that both are language models, but they exist at very different sizes. A larger parameter count generally means the model has more capacity to store patterns, represent linguistic structures, and support more complex behaviors. The phrase 'task ability' suggests that scale can change what a model is capable of doing, not merely improve a score slightly. The slide prepares the discussion of scaling laws by showing that model size is one of the central variables behind modern LLM capability.

Key concepts: scale, parameter count, BERT-base, BLOOM, task ability

metadata.slide_title: The Significance of Scale: Task Ability
metadata.index_text: Slide 16 | Significance of scale | BERT 110M versus BLOOM 176B and task capability

text:
Model Size vs. Time

This slide shows the historical growth of model size over time. The timeline begins around 2018 with BERT-L at 340 million parameters, then moves to GPT-2 at 1.5 billion, GPT-3 at 175 billion, and PaLM at 540 billion by 2022. The listed drivers of this growth are the introduction of the Transformer, access to massive datasets, and more powerful compute resources. The slide teaches that modern LLM progress was not caused by a single improvement; it came from a combination of better architectures, much larger training corpora, and hardware capable of performing huge numbers of operations. This historical view motivates why scaling became a research topic.

Key concepts: model size growth, BERT-L, GPT-2, GPT-3, PaLM, Transformer, compute

metadata.index_text: Slide 17 | Model size over time | BERT-L 340M, GPT-2 1.5B, GPT-3 175B, PaLM 540B, growth drivers

The slide teaches that modern LLM progress was not caused by a single improvement; it came from a combination of better architectures, much larger training corpora, and hardware capable of performing huge numbers of operations. This historical view motivates why scaling became a research topic.

text:
Model Size vs. Time: Toward Trillions

This slide visualizes model-size growth with increasingly large circles on a timeline. BERT-L appears as a small circle at 340 million parameters, GPT-2 grows to 1.5 billion, GPT-3 becomes much larger at 175 billion, and PaLM is shown as 540 billion. The arrow labeled 'increase?' points toward an even larger circle labeled 'Trillion(s)', suggesting the question of whether models should continue growing into trillion-parameter scale. The visual encourages students to think critically: if larger models often perform better, is the obvious strategy always to keep increasing parameter count? The later slides answer this by introducing compute budgets, memory constraints, and compute-optimal training.

Key concepts: trillion-parameter models, parameter scaling, timeline, PaLM, GPT-3, model growth

metadata.slide_title: Model Size vs. Time: Toward Trillions

The arrow labeled 'increase?' points toward an even larger circle labeled 'Trillion(s)', suggesting the question of whether models should continue growing into trillion-parameter scale. The visual encourages students to think critically: if larger models often perform better, is the obvious strategy always to keep increasing parameter count? The later slides answer this by introducing compute budgets, memory constraints, and compute-optimal training.

text:
Computational Challenges

This transition slide introduces the computational challenges that arise when models become very large. Once models contain billions or hundreds of billions of parameters, training is no longer a simple matter of running a neural network on one GPU. Memory is needed to store model weights, gradients, optimizer states, and intermediate activations. Compute is needed for forward and backward passes across many tokens. Communication is needed when the training is distributed across multiple GPUs. The slide prepares students for the following quantitative discussion about GPU RAM, precision formats, quantization, and distributed training strategies.

Key concepts: computational challenges, GPU memory, training compute, large models, distributed training

metadata.index_text: Slide 19 | Computational challenges | memory, compute, GPU limits, large-scale LLM training
metadata.slide_paragraph: This transition slide introduces the computational challenges that arise when models become very large. Once models contain billions or hundreds of billions of parameters, training is no longer a simple matter of running a neural network on one GPU. Memory is needed to store model weights, gradients, optimizer states, and intermediate activations. Compute is needed for forward and backward passes across many tokens. Communication is needed when the training is distributed across multiple GPUs. The slide prepares students for the following quantitative discussion about GPU RAM, precision formats, quantization, and distributed training strategies.

text:
Approximate GPU RAM Needed to Store 1B Parameters

This slide calculates the memory required just to store a one-billion-parameter model in 32-bit floating-point precision. A single FP32 parameter uses 4 bytes, so 1 billion parameters require approximately 4 × 10^9 bytes, which is about 4 GB of GPU RAM. The slide emphasizes that this is only storage for the model weights, not the full training memory. The calculation gives students an intuitive baseline: even a 1B-parameter model already consumes several gigabytes before gradients, optimizer states, activations, or batch data are considered. This simple multiplication is the foundation for understanding why large models quickly exceed the capacity of a single GPU.

Key concepts: GPU RAM, 1B parameters, FP32, model storage, memory calculation

metadata.slide_title: Approximate GPU RAM Needed to Store 1B Parameters
metadata.index_text: Slide 20 | GPU RAM for 1B parameters | 1 parameter 4 bytes, 1B parameters about 4GB at FP32
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide calculates the memory required just to store a one-billion-parameter model in 32-bit floating-point precision. A single FP32 parameter uses 4 bytes, so 1 billion parameters require approximately 4 × 10^9 bytes, which is about 4 GB of GPU RAM. The slide emphasizes that this is only storage for the model weights, not the full training memory. The calculation gives students an intuitive baseline: even a 1B-parameter model already consumes several gigabytes before gradients, optimizer states, activations, or batch data are considered. This simple multiplication is the foundation for understanding why large models quickly exceed the capacity of a single GPU.

text:
Additional GPU RAM Needed to Train 1B Parameters

This slide shows that training needs much more memory than inference or simple storage. It states that about 20 extra bytes per parameter may be needed during training. This extra memory comes from components such as optimizer states, gradients, and activations. For example, when using an optimizer like Adam, the training process stores additional moving-average statistics for each parameter. Gradients must also be stored after backpropagation, and activations may be kept to compute gradients. The main lesson is that a model that appears to fit in memory for inference may not fit for training, because training stores several additional tensors for every parameter.

Key concepts: training memory, optimizer states, gradients, activations, bytes per parameter

metadata.slide_title: Additional GPU RAM Needed to Train 1B Parameters
metadata.index_text: Slide 21 | Additional GPU RAM for training | about 20 extra bytes per parameter beyond weights
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows that training needs much more memory than inference or simple storage. It states that about 20 extra bytes per parameter may be needed during training. This extra memory comes from components such as optimizer states, gradients, and activations. For example, when using an optimizer like Adam, the training process stores additional moving-average statistics for each parameter. Gradients must also be stored after backpropagation, and activations may be kept to compute gradients. The main lesson is that a model that appears to fit in memory for inference may not fit for training, because training stores several additional tensors for every parameter.

text:
Approximate GPU RAM Needed to Train 1B Parameters

This slide combines the previous two memory estimates. A one-billion-parameter model requires about 4 GB to store the weights in 32-bit full precision, but approximately 24 GB to train in full precision after adding training-related memory. The difference is important: the memory needed to store a model is not the same as the memory needed to update it. Training requires the model weights plus gradients, optimizer states, and temporary activation memory. Therefore, when planning LLM training, one must estimate training memory, not just parameter storage. The slide provides a concrete rule of thumb for why even moderate-size models require powerful GPUs.

Key concepts: training RAM, full precision, 1B parameters, model weights, optimizer memory

Training requires the model weights plus gradients, optimizer states, and temporary activation memory. text:
Quantization

This slide introduces quantization as a way to represent model numbers using fewer bits. It compares FP32, which is 32-bit floating point, with lower-precision formats such as FP16, BFLOAT16, and INT8. The number line highlights that different formats have different ranges and precision. FP32 can represent extremely large and small values, roughly from negative 3e38 to positive 3e38, while lower-precision formats reduce memory but may reduce numerical detail. The key idea is that neural network parameters are numbers, and choosing how many bits to store each number directly affects memory usage, speed, and sometimes accuracy. Quantization is therefore a practical tool for training and deploying large models.

Key concepts: quantization, FP32, FP16, BFLOAT16, INT8, numerical precision

metadata.index_text: Slide 23 | Quantization | FP32 versus FP16 BFLOAT16 INT8, range, memory, precision
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces quantization as a way to represent model numbers using fewer bits. It compares FP32, which is 32-bit floating point, with lower-precision formats such as FP16, BFLOAT16, and INT8. The number line highlights that different formats have different ranges and precision. FP32 can represent extremely large and small values, roughly from negative 3e38 to positive 3e38, while lower-precision formats reduce memory but may reduce numerical detail. The key idea is that neural network parameters are numbers, and choosing how many bits to store each number directly affects memory usage, speed, and sometimes accuracy. Quantization is therefore a practical tool for training and deploying large models.

text:
Quantization: FP32

This slide explains how FP32 stores a number using 32 bits. The bit layout is divided into 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, and 23 fraction or mantissa bits. The sign bit tells whether the number is positive or negative, the exponent controls the scale or magnitude, and the fraction/mantissa stores the precision of the value. The example stores pi, 3.141592, and shows that the FP32 representation approximates it as 3.1415920257568359375, while the real value is 3.1415926535897932384. This illustrates an important principle: floating-point numbers are approximations. FP32 offers a wide range and relatively high precision, but it still cannot represent every real number exactly.

Key concepts: FP32, sign bit, exponent, mantissa, floating-point approximation, pi representation

metadata.index_text: Slide 24 | Quantization FP32 | 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, 23 fraction bits, storing pi approximately
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains how FP32 stores a number using 32 bits. The bit layout is divided into 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, and 23 fraction or mantissa bits. The sign bit tells whether the number is positive or negative, the exponent controls the scale or magnitude, and the fraction/mantissa stores the precision of the value. The example stores pi, 3.141592, and shows that the FP32 representation approximates it as 3.1415920257568359375, while the real value is 3.1415926535897932384. This illustrates an important principle: floating-point numbers are approximations. FP32 offers a wide range and relatively high precision, but it still cannot represent every real number exactly.

text:
Quantization: FP16

This slide compares FP32 with FP16 using the example of storing pi. FP16 uses only 16 bits: 1 sign bit, 5 exponent bits, and 10 fraction bits. Because it has fewer bits than FP32, it needs only 2 bytes instead of 4 bytes per value. However, the reduction also limits the representable range and precision. The slide shows FP16 representing pi approximately as 3.140625, which is less precise than the FP32 approximation. It also shows the FP16 range from about -65504 to 65504, much smaller than FP32's extremely wide range. The key trade-off is memory savings versus numerical accuracy and range.

Key concepts: FP16, half precision, memory reduction, precision loss, range limitation

metadata.index_text: Slide 25 | Quantization FP16 | 2 byte half precision, 1 sign 5 exponent 10 fraction, pi rounded to 3.140625
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide compares FP32 with FP16 using the example of storing pi. FP16 uses only 16 bits: 1 sign bit, 5 exponent bits, and 10 fraction bits. Because it has fewer bits than FP32, it needs only 2 bytes instead of 4 bytes per value. However, the reduction also limits the representable range and precision. The slide shows FP16 representing pi approximately as 3.140625, which is less precise than the FP32 approximation. It also shows the FP16 range from about -65504 to 65504, much smaller than FP32's extremely wide range. The key trade-off is memory savings versus numerical accuracy and range.

text:
Quantization: BFLOAT16

This slide explains BFLOAT16, also called BF16, and compares it with FP32. BFLOAT16 uses 16 bits like FP16, so it also requires 2 bytes per value, but it keeps the same 8 exponent bits as FP32 and uses only 7 fraction bits. Because it preserves the exponent size, BFLOAT16 keeps a range close to FP32, roughly around plus or minus 3e38. However, because it has fewer fraction bits, it has lower precision. The slide describes BF16 as a truncated FP32, meaning it keeps the broad scale of FP32 while dropping many precision bits. This is why BFLOAT16 is popular in deep learning: it often provides stable training with lower memory usage.

Key concepts: BFLOAT16, BF16, truncated FP32, exponent range, reduced precision, training stability

metadata.index_text: Slide 26 | Quantization BFLOAT16 | 1 sign 8 exponent 7 fraction, FP32-like range with less precision
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains BFLOAT16, also called BF16, and compares it with FP32. BFLOAT16 uses 16 bits like FP16, so it also requires 2 bytes per value, but it keeps the same 8 exponent bits as FP32 and uses only 7 fraction bits. Because it preserves the exponent size, BFLOAT16 keeps a range close to FP32, roughly around plus or minus 3e38. However, because it has fewer fraction bits, it has lower precision. The slide describes BF16 as a truncated FP32, meaning it keeps the broad scale of FP32 while dropping many precision bits. This is why BFLOAT16 is popular in deep learning: it often provides stable training with lower memory usage.

text:
Quantization: INT8

This slide presents INT8 quantization, where each value is stored using only 8 bits, requiring 1 byte of memory. The slide shows INT8 representing pi as the integer 3, demonstrating that integer formats are much less precise for raw real-valued quantities unless a scaling method is used. INT8 has a limited signed integer range from -128 to 127, unlike floating-point formats that represent fractional and very large values. The key intuition is that INT8 can greatly reduce memory and accelerate computation, especially during inference, but it requires careful scaling so that real-valued neural network weights and activations can be mapped into integer ranges without losing too much information.

Key concepts: INT8, integer quantization, 1 byte, scaling, inference efficiency, precision loss

metadata.index_text: Slide 27 | Quantization INT8 | 8-bit integer, range -128 to 127, stores pi as 3 without scaling
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide presents INT8 quantization, where each value is stored using only 8 bits, requiring 1 byte of memory. The slide shows INT8 representing pi as the integer 3, demonstrating that integer formats are much less precise for raw real-valued quantities unless a scaling method is used. INT8 has a limited signed integer range from -128 to 127, unlike floating-point formats that represent fractional and very large values. The key intuition is that INT8 can greatly reduce memory and accelerate computation, especially during inference, but it requires careful scaling so that real-valued neural network weights and activations can be mapped into integer ranges without losing too much information.

text:
Calculating Max and Min FP32, FP16, BFLOAT16, INT8

This slide gives a detailed table for the maximum and minimum values representable by several numerical formats. For FP32, the table shows 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, and 23 fraction bits, leading to a maximum around 3.4028235E+38 and a corresponding negative minimum. FP16 uses 5 exponent bits and 10 fraction bits, giving a much smaller maximum of 65504. BFLOAT16 uses 8 exponent bits and 7 fraction bits, giving a range close to FP32 but with lower precision. INT8 is different: it uses one sign-related bit and seven value bits in a signed integer representation, giving values from -128 to 127. The table connects bit allocation directly to numerical range.

Key concepts: max/min values, floating-point range, FP32, FP16, BFLOAT16, INT8

metadata.slide_title: Calculating Max and Min FP32, FP16, BFLOAT16, INT8
metadata.index_text: Slide 28 | Max and min precision formats | FP32 FP16 BFLOAT16 INT8 bit layouts and representable ranges

FP16 uses 5 exponent bits and 10 fraction bits, giving a much smaller maximum of 65504. BFLOAT16 uses 8 exponent bits and 7 fraction bits, giving a range close to FP32 but with lower precision. INT8 is different: it uses one sign-related bit and seven value bits in a signed integer representation, giving values from -128 to 127. The table connects bit allocation directly to numerical range.

text:
Quantization Summary

This slide summarizes why quantization matters for LLMs. Quantization reduces the memory required to store and train models by projecting original 32-bit floating-point values into lower-precision spaces. The table compares FP32, FP16, BFLOAT16, and INT8 by bits, exponent size, fraction size, and memory per value. FP32 uses 4 bytes, FP16 and BFLOAT16 use 2 bytes, and INT8 uses 1 byte. The slide also mentions quantization-aware training, where the model learns scaling factors during training so it can operate better under quantized representations. BFLOAT16 is highlighted as a popular choice because it preserves a wide exponent range while saving memory.

Key concepts: quantization summary, memory savings, quantization-aware training, BFLOAT16, precision formats

metadata.index_text: Slide 29 | Quantization summary | lower precision memory savings, QAT, FP32 FP16 BFLOAT16 INT8 comparison
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes why quantization matters for LLMs. Quantization reduces the memory required to store and train models by projecting original 32-bit floating-point values into lower-precision spaces. The table compares FP32, FP16, BFLOAT16, and INT8 by bits, exponent size, fraction size, and memory per value. FP32 uses 4 bytes, FP16 and BFLOAT16 use 2 bytes, and INT8 uses 1 byte. The slide also mentions quantization-aware training, where the model learns scaling factors during training so it can operate better under quantized representations. BFLOAT16 is highlighted as a popular choice because it preserves a wide exponent range while saving memory.

text:
Approximate GPU RAM Needed to Store 1B Parameters with Quantization

This slide revisits the 1B-parameter memory calculation under different precision choices. A full-precision FP32 model needs about 4 GB to store 1 billion parameters. If the model is stored in 16-bit precision, the memory drops to about 2 GB. If it is stored in 8-bit precision, the memory drops to about 1 GB. The visual clearly shows why quantization is so important for practical LLM deployment: reducing the number of bytes per parameter can make a model fit on cheaper or smaller hardware. The trade-off is that lower precision must be managed carefully to avoid unacceptable degradation in model quality.

Key concepts: model storage, quantized model, FP32 memory, 16-bit precision, 8-bit precision, 1B parameters

metadata.slide_title: Approximate GPU RAM Needed to Store 1B Parameters with Quantization
metadata.index_text: Slide 30 | GPU RAM to store 1B parameters | 4GB FP32, 2GB 16-bit, 1GB 8-bit
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide revisits the 1B-parameter memory calculation under different precision choices. A full-precision FP32 model needs about 4 GB to store 1 billion parameters. If the model is stored in 16-bit precision, the memory drops to about 2 GB. If it is stored in 8-bit precision, the memory drops to about 1 GB. The visual clearly shows why quantization is so important for practical LLM deployment: reducing the number of bytes per parameter can make a model fit on cheaper or smaller hardware. The trade-off is that lower precision must be managed carefully to avoid unacceptable degradation in model quality.

text:
GPU RAM Needed to Train Larger Models

This slide scales the memory problem from one billion parameters to very large models. It shows that a 175B-parameter model may require around 4,200 GB of full-precision GPU RAM for training, while a 500B-parameter model may require around 12,000 GB. These numbers emphasize that training frontier-scale LLMs is far beyond the capacity of a single GPU. The memory includes more than weights; it also reflects training overhead such as gradients and optimizer states. The key lesson is that as model size grows, training becomes a distributed systems problem, not only a machine learning problem.

Key concepts: large model training, 175B parameters, 500B parameters, GPU RAM, training memory

metadata.slide_title: GPU RAM Needed to Train Larger Models
metadata.index_text: Slide 31 | GPU RAM for larger models | 175B needs about 4200GB, 500B needs about 12000GB at FP32 training
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide scales the memory problem from one billion parameters to very large models. It shows that a 175B-parameter model may require around 4,200 GB of full-precision GPU RAM for training, while a 500B-parameter model may require around 12,000 GB. These numbers emphasize that training frontier-scale LLMs is far beyond the capacity of a single GPU. The memory includes more than weights; it also reflects training overhead such as gradients and optimizer states. The key lesson is that as model size grows, training becomes a distributed systems problem, not only a machine learning problem.

text:
GPU RAM Needed to Train Larger Models: Need for Multi-GPU Training

This slide makes the practical conclusion from the previous memory estimates: as model sizes get larger, the model must be split across multiple GPUs for training. The slide visually relates massive memory requirements to the need for distributed computation. A single GPU cannot hold all weights, gradients, optimizer states, and activations for a 175B or 500B parameter model in full precision. Therefore, engineers use strategies such as data parallelism, model parallelism, sharding, and memory offloading. The slide prepares students for distributed compute strategies by showing that they are not optional optimizations; they are necessary for training very large models.

Key concepts: multi-GPU training, model sharding, distributed compute, memory limits, large-scale training

metadata.slide_title: GPU RAM Needed to Train Larger Models: Need for Multi-GPU Training
metadata.index_text: Slide 32 | Need to split model across GPUs | larger models exceed single GPU memory
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide makes the practical conclusion from the previous memory estimates: as model sizes get larger, the model must be split across multiple GPUs for training. The slide visually relates massive memory requirements to the need for distributed computation. A single GPU cannot hold all weights, gradients, optimizer states, and activations for a 175B or 500B parameter model in full precision. Therefore, engineers use strategies such as data parallelism, model parallelism, sharding, and memory offloading. The slide prepares students for distributed compute strategies by showing that they are not optional optimizations; they are necessary for training very large models.

text:
Efficient Multi-GPU Compute Strategies

This transition slide introduces efficient multi-GPU compute strategies. The purpose of these methods is to make training possible when the model, data, or training state cannot be handled efficiently on one GPU. Multi-GPU strategies must solve two problems at once: distributing work so training is faster, and distributing memory so the model fits. The following slides focus on approaches such as Distributed Data Parallel and Fully Sharded Data Parallel, which differ in what they replicate and what they shard. The key idea is that large LLM training depends heavily on systems-level design.

Key concepts: multi-GPU compute, distributed training, parallelism, DDP, FSDP, memory efficiency

metadata.index_text: Slide 33 | Efficient multi-GPU compute strategies | distributed training methods for large LLMs
metadata.slide_paragraph: This transition slide introduces efficient multi-GPU compute strategies. The purpose of these methods is to make training possible when the model, data, or training state cannot be handled efficiently on one GPU. Multi-GPU strategies must solve two problems at once: distributing work so training is faster, and distributing memory so the model fits. The following slides focus on approaches such as Distributed Data Parallel and Fully Sharded Data Parallel, which differ in what they replicate and what they shard. The key idea is that large LLM training depends heavily on systems-level design.

text:
When to Use Distributed Compute

This slide distinguishes two reasons to use distributed compute. In one case, the model is too big for a single GPU, so the model itself or its training state must be split across devices. In another case, the model fits on one GPU, but training data can be processed in parallel to speed up training. The diagram shows two LLM blocks, indicating that distributed compute can be about memory capacity or about throughput. This is an important distinction: data parallelism primarily accelerates training by using multiple data batches, while sharding and model parallel methods address memory limitations. Choosing the right strategy depends on whether the bottleneck is model size, data volume, or training speed.

Key concepts: distributed compute, model too big, data parallelism, throughput, memory bottleneck

metadata.index_text: Slide 34 | When to use distributed compute | model too big for one GPU or data parallel training
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide distinguishes two reasons to use distributed compute. In one case, the model is too big for a single GPU, so the model itself or its training state must be split across devices. In another case, the model fits on one GPU, but training data can be processed in parallel to speed up training. The diagram shows two LLM blocks, indicating that distributed compute can be about memory capacity or about throughput. This is an important distinction: data parallelism primarily accelerates training by using multiple data batches, while sharding and model parallel methods address memory limitations. Choosing the right strategy depends on whether the bottleneck is model size, data volume, or training speed.

text:
Distributed Data Parallel (DDP)

This slide illustrates Distributed Data Parallel training. A dataloader sends different mini-batches to several GPUs, labeled GPU 0 through GPU 3. Each GPU holds a full copy of the LLM and independently performs a forward and backward pass on its assigned data. After backpropagation, the gradients from all GPUs are synchronized so every model replica receives the same averaged update. Then each GPU updates its local copy of the model. The main advantage is speed: several batches are processed at the same time. The limitation is memory: because every GPU stores a full model copy plus training states, DDP does not solve the problem of a model being too large to fit on one GPU.

Key concepts: Distributed Data Parallel, DDP, gradient synchronization, data parallelism, model replicas

metadata.index_text: Slide 35 | Distributed Data Parallel | dataloader splits batches, each GPU runs forward backward, gradients synchronized, model updated
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide illustrates Distributed Data Parallel training. A dataloader sends different mini-batches to several GPUs, labeled GPU 0 through GPU 3. Each GPU holds a full copy of the LLM and independently performs a forward and backward pass on its assigned data. After backpropagation, the gradients from all GPUs are synchronized so every model replica receives the same averaged update. Then each GPU updates its local copy of the model. The main advantage is speed: several batches are processed at the same time. The limitation is memory: because every GPU stores a full model copy plus training states, DDP does not solve the problem of a model being too large to fit on one GPU.

text:
Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP)

This slide introduces Fully Sharded Data Parallel, motivated by the ZeRO paper. The phrase 'zero data overlap between GPUs' means that instead of every GPU storing a complete copy of training states, parts of the model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states can be distributed across GPUs. FSDP is designed to reduce memory redundancy, allowing larger models to be trained with the same hardware. The slide references ZeRO and PyTorch FSDP work, connecting the practical technique to research on memory optimization for trillion-parameter training. The main idea is that sharding reduces per-GPU memory by making each GPU responsible for only part of the full training state.

Key concepts: FSDP, ZeRO, sharding, memory optimization, training states

text:
Recap: Additional GPU RAM Needed for Training

This slide gives a memory breakdown per parameter during training. The table shows model parameters or weights requiring 4 bytes per parameter in FP32. Adam optimizer states require about 8 bytes per parameter because Adam keeps two state tensors. Gradients add another 4 bytes per parameter. Activations and temporary memory may add around 8 bytes per parameter as a high-end estimate. The total is summarized as 4 bytes for the parameter itself plus about 20 extra bytes per parameter for training overhead. This table explains why training memory is much larger than inference memory: training keeps multiple related tensors for each learnable weight.

Key concepts: training memory breakdown, Adam optimizer, gradients, activations, bytes per parameter

text:
Memory Usage in DDP

This slide shows the memory behavior of Distributed Data Parallel. The key bullet states that there is one full copy of the model and training parameters on each GPU. The diagram uses colored memory blocks for parameters, gradients, and optimizer states, and shows that the same full stack is repeated across GPU 0 through GPU N−1. This repetition is why DDP is simple and effective for speed, but inefficient for memory. If a model cannot fit on one GPU, DDP alone will not fix that, because each GPU still needs the complete model and training state. The student should understand DDP as replication-based parallelism.

Key concepts: DDP memory, replication, parameters, gradients, optimizer states, GPU redundancy

metadata.index_text: Slide 38 | Memory usage in DDP | full copy of parameters gradients optimizer states on each GPU
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows the memory behavior of Distributed Data Parallel. The key bullet states that there is one full copy of the model and training parameters on each GPU. The diagram uses colored memory blocks for parameters, gradients, and optimizer states, and shows that the same full stack is repeated across GPU 0 through GPU N−1. This repetition is why DDP is simple and effective for speed, but inefficient for memory. If a model cannot fit on one GPU, DDP alone will not fix that, because each GPU still needs the complete model and training state. The student should understand DDP as replication-based parallelism.

text:
Zero Redundancy Optimizer (ZeRO)

This slide introduces ZeRO as a strategy to reduce memory by distributing, or sharding, the model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across GPUs. The diagram contrasts a baseline where each GPU stores repeated memory blocks with sharded layouts where each GPU stores only a subset. A model shard is defined as a subset of parameters assigned to each GPU. The key intuition is that redundancy wastes memory: if four GPUs all store the same optimizer states, the total cluster memory is not being used efficiently. ZeRO reduces this redundancy so larger models can be trained without requiring every GPU to hold the full training state.

Key concepts: ZeRO, zero redundancy, model shard, optimizer sharding, gradient sharding, parameter sharding

metadata.index_text: Slide 39 | ZeRO optimizer | sharding parameters gradients and optimizer states across GPUs to reduce memory redundancy
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces ZeRO as a strategy to reduce memory by distributing, or sharding, the model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across GPUs. The diagram contrasts a baseline where each GPU stores repeated memory blocks with sharded layouts where each GPU stores only a subset. A model shard is defined as a subset of parameters assigned to each GPU. The key intuition is that redundancy wastes memory: if four GPUs all store the same optimizer states, the total cluster memory is not being used efficiently. ZeRO reduces this redundancy so larger models can be trained without requiring every GPU to hold the full training state.

text:
Zero Redundancy Optimizer Stages

This slide expands ZeRO into Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3. The diagram shows progressively more aggressive sharding across GPUs. Stage 1 shards optimizer states, reducing the memory used by optimizer statistics. Stage 2 also shards gradients, further reducing duplicated memory after backpropagation. Stage 3 shards parameters as well, giving the largest memory savings but requiring more communication because GPUs must gather needed weights during computation. The main lesson is that memory efficiency increases across stages, but communication and implementation complexity also increase. ZeRO provides a structured way to trade communication cost for memory savings.

Key concepts: ZeRO Stage 1, ZeRO Stage 2, ZeRO Stage 3, optimizer states, gradient sharding, parameter sharding

metadata.index_text: Slide 40 | ZeRO stages | Stage 1 optimizer states, Stage 2 gradients, Stage 3 parameters sharded across GPUs
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide expands ZeRO into Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3. The diagram shows progressively more aggressive sharding across GPUs. Stage 1 shards optimizer states, reducing the memory used by optimizer statistics. Stage 2 also shards gradients, further reducing duplicated memory after backpropagation. Stage 3 shards parameters as well, giving the largest memory savings but requiring more communication because GPUs must gather needed weights during computation. The main lesson is that memory efficiency increases across stages, but communication and implementation complexity also increase. ZeRO provides a structured way to trade communication cost for memory savings.

text:
Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) Workflow

This slide again shows the DDP workflow, emphasizing the order of operations. The dataloader distributes data to GPU 0, GPU 1, GPU 2, and GPU 3. Each GPU runs a forward and backward pass using its local full model copy. Then gradients are synchronized across devices. After synchronization, each GPU updates its own model copy, keeping all replicas identical. This repeated diagram helps students remember that DDP is data-parallel rather than memory-sharded: the data changes from GPU to GPU, but the model is replicated. It is excellent when the model fits on each GPU and the goal is faster training over more data.

Key concepts: DDP workflow, forward pass, backward pass, synchronize gradients, model update

metadata.index_text: Slide 41 | DDP workflow | dataloader, GPU replicas, forward backward pass, gradient sync, update model
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide again shows the DDP workflow, emphasizing the order of operations. The dataloader distributes data to GPU 0, GPU 1, GPU 2, and GPU 3. Each GPU runs a forward and backward pass using its local full model copy. Then gradients are synchronized across devices. After synchronization, each GPU updates its own model copy, keeping all replicas identical. This repeated diagram helps students remember that DDP is data-parallel rather than memory-sharded: the data changes from GPU to GPU, but the model is replicated. It is excellent when the model fits on each GPU and the goal is faster training over more data.

text:
Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) with GPU Memory Focus

This slide presents DDP again but with the LLM icons removed from the GPU boxes, making the workflow more abstract. The same sequence is visible: data is distributed, each GPU performs forward and backward computation, gradients are synchronized, and models are updated. The abstraction helps focus on the parallel process rather than model internals. The main concept remains that DDP parallelizes over data: every GPU computes gradients on a different mini-batch, then all gradients are combined so the final update is equivalent to training with a larger batch. This strategy improves throughput but keeps memory duplication.

Key concepts: data parallel training, GPU workers, gradient averaging, large batch, DDP abstraction

text:
Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP) Workflow

This slide introduces the FSDP workflow using the same broad layout as DDP: dataloader, GPUs, forward/backward passes, gradient synchronization, and model updates. The key difference, implied by the FSDP title, is that the model and training states are not fully replicated on each GPU. Instead, they are sharded, so each GPU stores only a portion of the full state. During computation, the necessary pieces are gathered when needed, then released or re-sharded afterward. This enables training larger models than DDP under the same memory budget. The slide prepares for the more detailed FSDP diagrams that show weight gathering explicitly.

Key concepts: FSDP workflow, sharded states, memory reduction, distributed training, gradient synchronization

metadata.index_text: Slide 43 | FSDP workflow | similar training loop to DDP but with model states sharded across GPUs
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the FSDP workflow using the same broad layout as DDP: dataloader, GPUs, forward/backward passes, gradient synchronization, and model updates. The key difference, implied by the FSDP title, is that the model and training states are not fully replicated on each GPU. Instead, they are sharded, so each GPU stores only a portion of the full state. During computation, the necessary pieces are gathered when needed, then released or re-sharded afterward. This enables training larger models than DDP under the same memory budget. The slide prepares for the more detailed FSDP diagrams that show weight gathering explicitly.

text:
Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP) Training Steps

This slide continues the FSDP workflow and emphasizes that forward and backward computation still occurs on each GPU, followed by gradient synchronization and model updates. The difference from DDP is not the existence of forward/backward passes, but how model weights and training states are stored and retrieved. In FSDP, GPUs may need to communicate to assemble the required weights for a layer before computation, then shard them again to save memory. The visual reinforces that FSDP is still a form of data parallelism, but with full sharding added to reduce per-device memory.

Key concepts: FSDP training, forward pass, backward pass, sharded weights, synchronization

metadata.slide_title: Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP) Training Steps
metadata.index_text: Slide 44 | FSDP training steps | forward and backward passes with sharded model states and synchronization
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide continues the FSDP workflow and emphasizes that forward and backward computation still occurs on each GPU, followed by gradient synchronization and model updates. The difference from DDP is not the existence of forward/backward passes, but how model weights and training states are stored and retrieved. In FSDP, GPUs may need to communicate to assemble the required weights for a layer before computation, then shard them again to save memory. The visual reinforces that FSDP is still a form of data parallelism, but with full sharding added to reduce per-device memory.

text:
Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP): Getting Weights

This slide shows a more detailed FSDP process. On the left, each GPU stores only small colored shards rather than the full model state. Before the forward pass, the system performs a 'Get weights' operation, meaning the GPUs gather the necessary parameter shards to compute the next part of the model. After the forward pass, another get-weights stage appears before the backward pass, because gradients require the relevant weights and activations. Finally, gradients are synchronized and the model is updated. The pink arrows highlight the communication points. The key idea is that FSDP saves memory by storing shards, but it pays extra communication cost to temporarily assemble weights when needed.

Key concepts: weight gathering, parameter shards, FSDP communication, forward pass, backward pass, memory-communication tradeoff

metadata.slide_title: Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP): Getting Weights
metadata.index_text: Slide 45 | FSDP get weights | parameter shards gathered for forward and backward passes, then gradients synchronized

After the forward pass, another get-weights stage appears before the backward pass, because gradients require the relevant weights and activations. Finally, gradients are synchronized and the model is updated. The pink arrows highlight the communication points. The key idea is that FSDP saves memory by storing shards, but it pays extra communication cost to temporarily assemble weights when needed.

text:
Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP): Synchronizing Gradients

This slide highlights the gradient synchronization stage in FSDP. After weights are gathered and each GPU completes its forward and backward passes, gradients must be coordinated so that the sharded model updates remain consistent. The highlighted 'Synchronize gradients' block shows that even though memory is sharded, training still requires communication between GPUs. The diagram then leads to update-model blocks for each GPU. The important lesson is that FSDP reduces memory usage but does not eliminate synchronization; it changes when and what GPUs communicate. Students should see FSDP as a balance between memory savings and communication overhead.

Key concepts: gradient synchronization, FSDP, communication overhead, model updates, sharded training

The highlighted 'Synchronize gradients' block shows that even though memory is sharded, training still requires communication between GPUs. The diagram then leads to update-model blocks for each GPU. The important lesson is that FSDP reduces memory usage but does not eliminate synchronization; it changes when and what GPUs communicate. Students should see FSDP as a balance between memory savings and communication overhead.

text:
Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP): Sharding Options

This slide summarizes practical FSDP features. It states that FSDP helps reduce overall GPU memory utilization and supports offloading to CPU if needed. CPU offloading means some states can be stored in CPU memory instead of GPU memory, trading speed for capacity. The slide also mentions configuring the level of sharding via a sharding factor. The bottom visual compares full replication, full sharding, and hybrid sharding. Full replication resembles DDP and uses more memory. Full sharding maximizes memory savings. Hybrid sharding sits between them, grouping GPUs so some replication and some sharding occur. The best choice depends on model size, GPU count, and communication bandwidth.

Key concepts: FSDP options, CPU offloading, sharding factor, full sharding, hybrid sharding, replication

The bottom visual compares full replication, full sharding, and hybrid sharding. Full replication resembles DDP and uses more memory. Full sharding maximizes memory savings. Hybrid sharding sits between them, grouping GPUs so some replication and some sharding occur. The best choice depends on model size, GPU count, and communication bandwidth.

text:
Scaling Laws and Compute-Optimal Models

This transition slide introduces scaling laws and compute-optimal models. Scaling laws describe empirical relationships between model performance and factors such as model size, dataset size, and training compute. Compute-optimal modeling asks how to spend a fixed compute budget most effectively: should we train a larger model, train on more data, or balance both? This topic is central because bigger models are expensive, and simply increasing parameter count may not be the best use of resources. The next slides examine how performance changes with scale and why recent work argues for balancing parameters and training tokens.

Key concepts: scaling laws, compute-optimal models, model size, dataset size, training compute

metadata.index_text: Slide 48 | Scaling laws and compute optimal models | performance versus model size data size and compute
metadata.slide_paragraph: This transition slide introduces scaling laws and compute-optimal models. Scaling laws describe empirical relationships between model performance and factors such as model size, dataset size, and training compute. Compute-optimal modeling asks how to spend a fixed compute budget most effectively: should we train a larger model, train on more data, or balance both? This topic is central because bigger models are expensive, and simply increasing parameter count may not be the best use of resources. The next slides examine how performance changes with scale and why recent work argues for balancing parameters and training tokens.

text:
Scaling Choices for Pre-training

This slide frames pre-training as an optimization problem. The goal is to maximize model performance, or equivalently minimize loss. The constraint is the compute budget, including GPUs, training time, and financial cost. Within that budget, there are two major scaling choices: dataset size, measured as the number of tokens used for training, and model size, measured as the number of parameters. The diagram shows these three quantities as a connected system: compute budget supports the choices of data and model size, and those choices affect performance. The key lesson is that good pre-training is not just 'make the model bigger'; it is choosing a balanced combination of data, parameters, and compute.

Key concepts: pre-training choices, compute budget, dataset size, model size, loss minimization

metadata.index_text: Slide 49 | Scaling choices | maximize performance under compute budget by choosing tokens and parameters
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide frames pre-training as an optimization problem. The goal is to maximize model performance, or equivalently minimize loss. The constraint is the compute budget, including GPUs, training time, and financial cost. Within that budget, there are two major scaling choices: dataset size, measured as the number of tokens used for training, and model size, measured as the number of parameters. The diagram shows these three quantities as a connected system: compute budget supports the choices of data and model size, and those choices affect performance. The key lesson is that good pre-training is not just 'make the model bigger'; it is choosing a balanced combination of data, parameters, and compute.

text:
Compute Budget for Training LLMs

This slide defines the unit 'petaflop/s-day'. A petaflop per second is one quadrillion floating-point operations per second, written as 1,000,000,000,000,000 operations per second. A petaflop/s-day means sustaining that compute rate for one full day. The slide connects this unit to NVIDIA V100 GPUs and notes that one petaflop/s-day corresponds to those chips running at full efficiency for 24 hours. The purpose of the unit is to give a common way to compare the training cost of different LLMs. Instead of saying only how many GPUs were used, PF-days combine compute rate and time into a single measure.

Key concepts: compute budget, petaflop/s-day, FLOPs, NVIDIA V100, training cost

metadata.index_text: Slide 50 | Compute budget | petaflop per second day definition, one quadrillion operations per second for a day
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide defines the unit 'petaflop/s-day'. A petaflop per second is one quadrillion floating-point operations per second, written as 1,000,000,000,000,000 operations per second. A petaflop/s-day means sustaining that compute rate for one full day. The slide connects this unit to NVIDIA V100 GPUs and notes that one petaflop/s-day corresponds to those chips running at full efficiency for 24 hours. The purpose of the unit is to give a common way to compare the training cost of different LLMs. Instead of saying only how many GPUs were used, PF-days combine compute rate and time into a single measure.

text:
Compute Budget for Training LLMs: GPU Examples

This slide visualizes petaflop/s-days using rows of NVIDIA V100 and A100 GPUs. The diagram shows that different GPU types can supply the same total compute with different numbers of chips because their individual performance differs. The bracketed groups indicate that a certain collection of GPUs running at full efficiency for 24 hours is equivalent to one petaflop/s-day. The main idea is that compute budget combines hardware capability and training duration. Faster GPUs or more GPUs can produce the same compute in less time, while fewer or slower GPUs require longer training.

Key concepts: GPU compute, V100, A100, PF-days, hardware efficiency, training duration

text:
Number of Petaflop/s-Days to Pre-train Various LLMs

This slide shows a bar chart comparing the training compute required for several LLM families, including BERT/RoBERTa, T5, and GPT-3. The y-axis is training petaflop/s-days on a logarithmic scale, meaning each step represents a large multiplicative increase. Smaller BERT and RoBERTa models require much less compute than larger T5 and GPT-3 models. Within GPT-3, the bars grow from small to medium to large to XL and then to billions of parameters, with the 175B model requiring the most compute. The chart makes clear that pre-training cost rises dramatically with model size, which motivates compute-efficient scaling and careful budget allocation.

Key concepts: training compute, PF-days, BERT, T5, GPT-3, log scale

text:
Compute Budget vs. Model Performance

This slide connects compute budget to model performance using test loss. The left diagram shows compute budget balancing dataset size and model size. The right plot places test loss on the vertical axis and compute on the horizontal axis. The downward trend indicates that as compute increases, test loss tends to decrease, meaning performance improves. The line is shown on a log-style scale and comes from Kaplan et al.'s scaling laws. The key intuition is that more compute allows either more data, a larger model, longer training, or some combination of these. However, the improvement follows a smooth trend with diminishing returns, so each additional compute increase gives smaller relative gains.

Key concepts: compute vs performance, test loss, Kaplan scaling laws, dataset size, model size, diminishing returns

However, the improvement follows a smooth trend with diminishing returns, so each additional compute increase gives smaller relative gains.

text:
Dataset Size and Model Size vs. Performance

This slide emphasizes that compute resources are constrained by hardware, project timeline, and financial budget. The visual triangle places compute budget, dataset size, and model size as interacting variables. The snowflake symbols suggest that some variables may be fixed or limited in an experiment. If compute is fixed, increasing model size may require reducing training tokens, and increasing dataset size may require using a smaller model or training fewer steps. The key message is that scaling choices are trade-offs. A practical team must decide how to allocate limited compute between parameter count and data quantity rather than assuming both can grow freely.

Key concepts: compute constraints, hardware, project timeline, financial budget, dataset size, model size

text:
Dataset Size and Model Size vs. Performance: Scaling Trends

This slide shows two plots: test loss decreases as dataset size increases, and test loss also decreases as the number of parameters increases. The frozen compute-budget icon suggests that these relationships are studied under constraints. The highlighted labels for dataset size and model size show that both are important levers. The downward lines indicate that more data and more parameters can both improve performance, but the improvement is not unlimited; it follows a power-law-like trend with diminishing returns. The central lesson is that performance depends on scaling both the data and the model, and focusing on only one can become inefficient.

Key concepts: dataset scaling, parameter scaling, test loss, power law, diminishing returns, compute budget
