text:
Chinchilla Paper

This slide introduces the Chinchilla paper by Hoffmann and colleagues, titled 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models.' The visible abstract text highlights the central result: many large language models were significantly under-trained, meaning they had many parameters but were not trained on enough tokens for their compute budget. The highlighted Chinchilla result compares a 70B-parameter model trained on much more data with larger models such as Gopher. The key claim is that for a fixed compute budget, model size and number of training tokens should be scaled together. The slide marks an important shift from simply building larger models toward building compute-optimal models.

Key concepts: Chinchilla, Hoffmann et al. 2022, compute-optimal training, under-trained models, training tokens

metadata.index_text: Slide 56 | Chinchilla paper | Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models, smaller model with more tokens can outperform larger under-trained models
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the Chinchilla paper by Hoffmann and colleagues, titled 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models.' The visible abstract text highlights the central result: many large language models were significantly under-trained, meaning they had many parameters but were not trained on enough tokens for their compute budget. The highlighted Chinchilla result compares a 70B-parameter model trained on much more data with larger models such as Gopher. The key claim is that for a fixed compute budget, model size and number of training tokens should be scaled together. The slide marks an important shift from simply building larger models toward building compute-optimal models.

text:
Compute-Optimal Models

This slide explains the core idea of compute-optimal training. Under a fixed compute budget, one can choose a very large model with relatively fewer training tokens, but this may produce an over-parameterized and under-trained model. Alternatively, one can choose a smaller model and train it on more data. The bullet points state that very large models may be over-parameterized and under-trained, and that smaller models trained on more data could perform as well as large models. The diagram contrasts model size and dataset size under the same compute budget. The lesson is that the best model is not necessarily the largest; it is the model whose parameter count and training tokens are balanced for the available compute.

Key concepts: compute-optimal model, over-parameterized, under-trained, fixed compute budget, data-parameter balance

text:
Chinchilla Scaling Laws for Model and Dataset Size

This slide presents the Chinchilla scaling rule that compute-optimal training data size is roughly 20 times the number of parameters. The table compares Chinchilla, LLaMA-65B, GPT-3, OPT-175B, and BLOOM. Chinchilla has 70B parameters and about 1.4T actual tokens, matching the compute-optimal estimate. LLaMA-65B has 65B parameters and about 1.4T actual tokens, also close to the estimate. By contrast, GPT-3 and similar 175B models would require around 3.5T tokens under the 20× rule, but their actual token counts are much smaller, such as 300B or 180B. The red box highlights under-training relative to the Chinchilla recommendation.

Key concepts: Chinchilla scaling, 20 tokens per parameter, LLaMA, GPT-3, OPT, BLOOM, training tokens

text:
Model Size vs. Time: Decrease After Scaling Debate

This slide revisits the model-size timeline and adds later models such as LLaMA 65B and BloombergGPT 50B. Earlier slides asked whether model sizes would keep increasing toward trillions. Here, the added smaller circles suggest a possible decrease or correction after the Chinchilla insight. Instead of always increasing parameters, researchers began training smaller models with more tokens and better data. BloombergGPT 50B is highlighted, and LLaMA 65B is shown near it, indicating that competitive models can be smaller than PaLM 540B or GPT-3 175B if trained more efficiently. The slide emphasizes a shift from parameter maximalism to compute-optimal design.

Key concepts: model size trend, LLaMA 65B, BloombergGPT 50B, compute optimality, Chinchilla insight

text:
Performance of LLMs

This slide summarizes the three main factors that determine LLM performance: model size N, dataset size D, and compute C measured in PF-days. Model size refers to the number of parameters excluding embeddings, dataset size refers to the amount of pretraining data, and compute refers to the operations used for training. The slide defines a petaflop/s-day as performing 10^15 neural network operations per second for one day, roughly 10^20 operations per day. It also notes that an A100 GPU is around 312 TFLOPs, approximately 3e14 FLOPs, or about 2.7e19 FLOPs per day. The visible formula suggests a parameter-count approximation involving model width and number of layers, reinforcing that architecture dimensions determine model size.

Key concepts: LLM performance factors, model size N, dataset size D, compute C, PF-days, A100 FLOPs

metadata.index_text: Slide 60 | Performance of LLMs | model size, data size, compute, PF-day definition, A100 FLOPs, tokens trained on

text:
LLM Architecture Parameter Scaling

This slide breaks down how Transformer parameters scale by component. The self-attention Q, K, and V projections each use d_model by d_model matrices, giving three matrices and roughly 3 × d_model squared parameters. The attention output projection adds another d_model squared matrix. The feed-forward network has two large linear layers: one expands from d_model to about 4d_model, and the other projects back, contributing roughly 8 × d_model squared parameters together. Layer normalization and biases are marked negligible compared with these large matrices. The table shows why total parameter count scales mainly with d_model squared and the number of layers: wider models become much larger very quickly.

Key concepts: Transformer parameters, self-attention, QKV projections, feed-forward network, d_model squared, model width

text:
Two Example Models

This slide compares GPT-3 from 2020 and LLaMA from 2023. GPT-3 is shown with a vocabulary size of 50,257, context length 2048, 175B parameters, and training on 300B tokens. LLaMA is shown with a vocabulary size of 32,000, context length 2048, 65B parameters, and training on about 1–1.4T tokens. The training-cost notes estimate GPT-3 as requiring thousands to ten-thousands of V100 GPUs, about one month of training, and millions of dollars. For LLaMA 65B, the slide lists 2,048 A100 GPUs, 21 days of training, and about $5M. The comparison highlights that LLaMA used fewer parameters but many more tokens, reflecting the compute-optimal trend.

Key concepts: GPT-3, LLaMA, vocabulary size, context length, training tokens, training cost

text:
One GPU for One Day: Scaling Trade-off Thought Experiment

This slide presents a practical thought experiment: if you can use one GPU for one day, should you train a 5 million parameter language model on 100 books, a 500 million parameter model on one book, or a 100k parameter model on 5k books? The question forces students to think about balancing model size and dataset size under a fixed compute budget. A very large model with too little data may overfit or be under-trained. A tiny model with lots of data may lack capacity. A middle option may be more balanced, depending on the task and compute. The slide prepares the intuition behind scaling laws: performance is determined by a relationship between parameters, tokens, and compute, not by any single factor alone.

Key concepts: compute budget, model-data trade-off, one GPU, under-training, capacity, scaling intuition

text:
Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models: Kaplan et al., 2020

This slide introduces the Kaplan et al. 2020 paper 'Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models.' The slide shows the paper title and authors, including Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, Tom Brown, Alec Radford, Dario Amodei, and others. The paper is central to the lecture because it empirically studied how language model loss changes as model size, dataset size, and compute are scaled. It proposed smooth power-law relationships that made it possible to predict performance trends from scale. The slide functions as a reference point: the next slides summarize the findings and then later compare them with the Chinchilla/Hoffmann view.

Key concepts: Kaplan et al. 2020, scaling laws, language model loss, model size, dataset size, compute

metadata.slide_title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models: Kaplan et al., 2020
metadata.index_text: Slide 64 | Kaplan et al. 2020 | Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models paper and authors
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the Kaplan et al. 2020 paper 'Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models.' The slide shows the paper title and authors, including Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, Tom Brown, Alec Radford, Dario Amodei, and others. The paper is central to the lecture because it empirically studied how language model loss changes as model size, dataset size, and compute are scaled. It proposed smooth power-law relationships that made it possible to predict performance trends from scale. The slide functions as a reference point: the next slides summarize the findings and then later compare them with the Chinchilla/Hoffmann view.

text:
Summary of Scaling Laws: Three Power-Law Trends

This slide shows three plots from the Kaplan scaling-law analysis. The first plot relates test loss to compute measured in PF-days, showing that more compute reduces loss. The second plot relates loss to dataset size in tokens, showing that more training data also reduces loss. The third plot relates loss to non-embedding parameters, showing that larger models reduce loss. The figure caption states that language modeling performance improves smoothly as model size, dataset size, and compute increase, and that all three must be scaled together for optimal performance. The key concept is the power-law relationship: improvements are predictable and smooth, but they also exhibit diminishing returns.

Key concepts: power law, test loss, compute, dataset size, parameters, smooth scaling

metadata.slide_title: Summary of Scaling Laws: Three Power-Law Trends
metadata.index_text: Slide 65 | Summary of scaling laws | plots of test loss versus compute, dataset size, and parameters
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows three plots from the Kaplan scaling-law analysis. The first plot relates test loss to compute measured in PF-days, showing that more compute reduces loss. The second plot relates loss to dataset size in tokens, showing that more training data also reduces loss. The third plot relates loss to non-embedding parameters, showing that larger models reduce loss. The figure caption states that language modeling performance improves smoothly as model size, dataset size, and compute increase, and that all three must be scaled together for optimal performance. The key concept is the power-law relationship: improvements are predictable and smooth, but they also exhibit diminishing returns.

text:
Summary of Scaling Laws: Equations and Critical Values

This slide presents a table of Kaplan-style scaling relationships. For model size, the loss is written as a power-law function of N with exponent 0.076 and a critical value around 8.8 × 10^13 parameters; a 10× increase gives about 17% lower loss. For dataset size, the exponent is 0.095 with a critical value around 5.4 × 10^13 tokens; a 10× increase gives about 20% lower loss. For compute, the exponent is 0.050 with a critical value around 3.1 × 10^8 PF-days; a 10× increase gives about 11% lower loss. The interpretation column emphasizes diminishing returns and the need to balance model and data.

Key concepts: scaling equations, critical value, loss improvement, model size exponent, dataset size exponent, compute exponent

metadata.slide_title: Summary of Scaling Laws: Equations and Critical Values
metadata.index_text: Slide 66 | Scaling laws table | L(N), L(D), L(Cmin), exponents 0.076 0.095 0.050, critical values and loss improvement
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide presents a table of Kaplan-style scaling relationships. For model size, the loss is written as a power-law function of N with exponent 0.076 and a critical value around 8.8 × 10^13 parameters; a 10× increase gives about 17% lower loss. For dataset size, the exponent is 0.095 with a critical value around 5.4 × 10^13 tokens; a 10× increase gives about 20% lower loss. For compute, the exponent is 0.050 with a critical value around 3.1 × 10^8 PF-days; a 10× increase gives about 11% lower loss. The interpretation column emphasizes diminishing returns and the need to balance model and data.

text:
Summary of Scaling Laws: Formal Statements

This slide shows formal mathematical statements from the scaling laws paper. It describes how, for models trained with a limited number of parameters and sufficiently large datasets, loss follows a power-law with model size. It also describes how, for large models trained with limited datasets, loss follows a power-law with dataset size. Finally, it states that when training is compute-limited and model size and dataset size are chosen optimally, loss follows a power-law with compute. The equations are important because they turn empirical observations into predictive relationships. The student should understand that these are not exact physical laws; they are fitted empirical laws that describe trends over a range of experiments.

Key concepts: formal scaling laws, loss prediction, model-limited regime, data-limited regime, compute-limited regime

metadata.slide_title: Summary of Scaling Laws: Formal Statements

Finally, it states that when training is compute-limited and model size and dataset size are chosen optimally, loss follows a power-law with compute. The equations are important because they turn empirical observations into predictive relationships. The student should understand that these are not exact physical laws; they are fitted empirical laws that describe trends over a range of experiments.

text:
Summary of Scaling Laws: Interpretation Table

This slide repeats the scaling-law table in a clearer summary form. It organizes the findings by scaling type: model size, dataset size, and compute. Each row includes the equation, critical value, approximate loss improvement for a 10× increase, and interpretation. The model-size row says larger models lower loss but with diminishing returns. The dataset-size row says more training data lowers loss, also with diminishing returns. The compute row says more compute lowers loss only if model and data are balanced. The table is useful because it directly links mathematical formulas to practical training decisions: where should extra resources be spent? The answer depends on whether the current model is parameter-limited, data-limited, or compute-limited.

Key concepts: scaling interpretation, diminishing returns, resource allocation, balanced scaling, loss reduction

text:
Some Observations

This slide summarizes key observations from scaling-law research. Performance depends strongly on scale: model parameters, data size, and compute used for training. It depends more weakly on model shape, such as exact depth or width, within the studied range. The slide states that performance versus scale can be modeled with power laws, meaning that predictable curves describe how loss changes as resources increase. It also emphasizes that performance improves most when model size and dataset size are scaled together; increasing one while keeping the other fixed leads to diminishing returns. Finally, larger models are described as more sample efficient because they can reach the same loss with fewer steps or data points than smaller models.

Key concepts: scale dependence, power laws, model shape, sample efficiency, balanced scaling, diminishing returns

metadata.index_text: Slide 69 | Scaling observations | performance depends on scale, weakly on shape, scale model and data together, larger models sample efficient
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes key observations from scaling-law research. Performance depends strongly on scale: model parameters, data size, and compute used for training. It depends more weakly on model shape, such as exact depth or width, within the studied range. The slide states that performance versus scale can be modeled with power laws, meaning that predictable curves describe how loss changes as resources increase. It also emphasizes that performance improves most when model size and dataset size are scaled together; increasing one while keeping the other fixed leads to diminishing returns. Finally, larger models are described as more sample efficient because they can reach the same loss with fewer steps or data points than smaller models.

text:
Model Size Effect

This slide focuses on how model size affects training loss. The plots show training curves for models of different sizes, with larger models improving faster and reaching lower loss than smaller models when enough compute and data are available. A highlighted note states that larger models require fewer samples to reach the same performance, which is the idea of sample efficiency. Another note indicates that the optimal model size grows smoothly with the loss target and compute budget. The key message is that increasing model size is powerful, but only when the model receives enough training data and compute to use that capacity effectively.

Key concepts: model size effect, sample efficiency, training curves, loss target, compute budget

metadata.index_text: Slide 70 | Model size effect | larger models need fewer samples and can reach lower loss when trained enough
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide focuses on how model size affects training loss. The plots show training curves for models of different sizes, with larger models improving faster and reaching lower loss than smaller models when enough compute and data are available. A highlighted note states that larger models require fewer samples to reach the same performance, which is the idea of sample efficiency. Another note indicates that the optimal model size grows smoothly with the loss target and compute budget. The key message is that increasing model size is powerful, but only when the model receives enough training data and compute to use that capacity effectively.

text:
Additional Comparisons

This slide provides additional Kaplan-style comparisons. The left plot is labeled loss versus model and dataset size, showing how validation loss changes when both data and parameters vary. The right plot compares loss versus model size and training steps, with curves that show how training duration interacts with model size. The figure caption emphasizes that early stopping a larger model can be more compute-efficient than fully training a smaller model in some settings. The slide reinforces that model performance cannot be understood from parameter count alone. Training steps, dataset size, and compute allocation all affect the final loss.

Key concepts: model-data comparison, training steps, validation loss, early stopping, compute efficiency

metadata.index_text: Slide 71 | Additional comparisons | loss versus model and dataset size, loss versus model size and training steps
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide provides additional Kaplan-style comparisons. The left plot is labeled loss versus model and dataset size, showing how validation loss changes when both data and parameters vary. The right plot compares loss versus model size and training steps, with curves that show how training duration interacts with model size. The figure caption emphasizes that early stopping a larger model can be more compute-efficient than fully training a smaller model in some settings. The slide reinforces that model performance cannot be understood from parameter count alone. Training steps, dataset size, and compute allocation all affect the final loss.

text:
Compute Effect

This slide explains compute per sample using the equation: compute per sample equals batch size multiplied by number of steps multiplied by parameters, divided by dataset size. The example underneath substitutes batch size 100, number of steps 10, and a parameter count written as a power of ten, divided by dataset size. The plot above shows validation loss across different compute budgets and training settings, illustrating that larger compute enables better performance when allocated well. The equation helps students see that compute is not only hardware; it depends on training configuration. Larger batches, more steps, and more parameters all increase compute, while dataset size changes how that compute is distributed per sample.

Key concepts: compute per sample, batch size, training steps, parameters, dataset size, validation loss

metadata.index_text: Slide 72 | Compute effect | compute per sample equation batch size times steps times parameters divided by dataset size
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains compute per sample using the equation: compute per sample equals batch size multiplied by number of steps multiplied by parameters, divided by dataset size. The example underneath substitutes batch size 100, number of steps 10, and a parameter count written as a power of ten, divided by dataset size. The plot above shows validation loss across different compute budgets and training settings, illustrating that larger compute enables better performance when allocated well. The equation helps students see that compute is not only hardware; it depends on training configuration. Larger batches, more steps, and more parameters all increase compute, while dataset size changes how that compute is distributed per sample.

text:
FLOPs per Token

This slide presents a table estimating parameter counts and forward-pass FLOPs per token for Transformer components. The rows include embedding, attention QKV projections, attention mask computation, attention output projection, feedforward layers, and de-embedding. For example, QKV attention parameters scale with the number of layers, model dimension, and attention dimension, while feedforward parameters scale with model dimension and feedforward width. The total non-embedding parameter formula is shown as N = 2 d_model n_layer (2 d_attn + d_ff). The forward compute is summarized as C_forward = 2N + 2 n_layer n_ctx d_attn. The table omits smaller terms such as biases, nonlinearities, and layer normalization, focusing on dominant costs.

Key concepts: FLOPs per token, Transformer compute, QKV, feedforward, non-embedding parameters, forward pass

metadata.index_text: Slide 73 | FLOPs per token | table of parameter counts and compute for embeddings attention feedforward and de-embedding
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide presents a table estimating parameter counts and forward-pass FLOPs per token for Transformer components. The rows include embedding, attention QKV projections, attention mask computation, attention output projection, feedforward layers, and de-embedding. For example, QKV attention parameters scale with the number of layers, model dimension, and attention dimension, while feedforward parameters scale with model dimension and feedforward width. The total non-embedding parameter formula is shown as N = 2 d_model n_layer (2 d_attn + d_ff). The forward compute is summarized as C_forward = 2N + 2 n_layer n_ctx d_attn. The table omits smaller terms such as biases, nonlinearities, and layer normalization, focusing on dominant costs.

text:
FLOPs per Token: Variable Definitions

This slide defines the symbols used in the FLOPs-per-token calculation. n_vocab is the vocabulary size, meaning the number of token types the model can represent. n_ctx is the context length or sequence length. d_model is the model width, also called embedding or hidden size. d_attn is the attention projection size, often approximately equal to d_model. d_ff is the feedforward or MLP inner width, often about four times d_model. n_layer is the number of Transformer layers or blocks. These definitions are essential because compute and parameter formulas become meaningful only when each variable is understood.

Key concepts: n_vocab, n_ctx, d_model, d_attn, d_ff, n_layer

metadata.index_text: Slide 74 | FLOPs variable definitions | vocabulary size, context length, model width, attention size, feedforward width, layers
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide defines the symbols used in the FLOPs-per-token calculation. n_vocab is the vocabulary size, meaning the number of token types the model can represent. n_ctx is the context length or sequence length. d_model is the model width, also called embedding or hidden size. d_attn is the attention projection size, often approximately equal to d_model. d_ff is the feedforward or MLP inner width, often about four times d_model. n_layer is the number of Transformer layers or blocks. These definitions are essential because compute and parameter formulas become meaningful only when each variable is understood.

text:
Why 2 FLOPs per Parameter in Forward Pass?

This slide explains the common rule of thumb that each parameter contributes roughly 2 FLOPs in a forward pass. FLOP 1 is multiplication: a unit i multiplies its activation h(i) by a weight w to send a contribution to unit j. FLOP 2 is addition: unit j adds that contribution into its total input a(j). The diagram shows connected units where activations flow through weighted connections. In a linear layer, this multiply-add pattern happens for many weights, so the compute cost is approximately twice the number of parameters involved. This simplification is useful for estimating LLM training cost without counting every minor operation.

Key concepts: FLOPs, multiply-add, forward pass, parameter compute, linear layer

metadata.slide_title: Why 2 FLOPs per Parameter in Forward Pass?
metadata.index_text: Slide 75 | Why 2 FLOPs per parameter | one multiply and one add for each weight contribution in forward pass
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains the common rule of thumb that each parameter contributes roughly 2 FLOPs in a forward pass. FLOP 1 is multiplication: a unit i multiplies its activation h(i) by a weight w to send a contribution to unit j. FLOP 2 is addition: unit j adds that contribution into its total input a(j). The diagram shows connected units where activations flow through weighted connections. In a linear layer, this multiply-add pattern happens for many weights, so the compute cost is approximately twice the number of parameters involved. This simplification is useful for estimating LLM training cost without counting every minor operation.

text:
Issues with Kaplan Laws

This slide critiques the Kaplan scaling-law results. It states that Kaplan et al. used the same learning-rate schedule for all training runs regardless of how many training tokens or batches were used. The slide explains that the learning-rate schedule should be adjusted based on the number of training steps; otherwise, it can impair performance. If some models are not trained with suitable schedules, the measured loss may not reflect the true best performance for that compute budget. Therefore, the resulting scaling laws may be flawed or biased. This sets up the motivation for later work, especially the Hoffmann/Chinchilla paper, which revised conclusions about how to allocate compute.

Key concepts: Kaplan limitations, learning rate schedule, training steps, scaling law critique, performance bias

metadata.index_text: Slide 76 | Issues with Kaplan laws | same learning rate schedule for all runs may flaw scaling conclusions
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide critiques the Kaplan scaling-law results. It states that Kaplan et al. used the same learning-rate schedule for all training runs regardless of how many training tokens or batches were used. The slide explains that the learning-rate schedule should be adjusted based on the number of training steps; otherwise, it can impair performance. If some models are not trained with suitable schedules, the measured loss may not reflect the true best performance for that compute budget. Therefore, the resulting scaling laws may be flawed or biased. This sets up the motivation for later work, especially the Hoffmann/Chinchilla paper, which revised conclusions about how to allocate compute.

text:
Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models: Hoffmann et al., 2022

This slide introduces the Hoffmann et al. 2022 paper 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models,' also associated with the Chinchilla result. The slide shows the paper title and authors, marking it as the alternative or correction to the earlier Kaplan conclusions. The key contribution of this paper is that compute-optimal training requires increasing the amount of training data much more than earlier practice suggested. Instead of using a very large model trained on relatively fewer tokens, the paper argues that smaller models trained on many more tokens can achieve better performance under the same compute budget. This slide prepares students for the Kaplan versus Hoffmann comparison.

Key concepts: Hoffmann et al. 2022, Chinchilla, compute-optimal LLMs, training tokens, Kaplan comparison

metadata.slide_title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models: Hoffmann et al., 2022
metadata.index_text: Slide 77 | Hoffmann et al 2022 | Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models, Chinchilla scaling revision
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the Hoffmann et al. 2022 paper 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models,' also associated with the Chinchilla result. The slide shows the paper title and authors, marking it as the alternative or correction to the earlier Kaplan conclusions. The key contribution of this paper is that compute-optimal training requires increasing the amount of training data much more than earlier practice suggested. Instead of using a very large model trained on relatively fewer tokens, the paper argues that smaller models trained on many more tokens can achieve better performance under the same compute budget. This slide prepares students for the Kaplan versus Hoffmann comparison.

text:
Kaplan vs. Hoffmann

This slide directly compares the two scaling recommendations. Kaplan et al. 2020 suggested that when compute increases, one should prioritize increasing model size more than data size. The slide states that with a 10× compute increase, Kaplan would increase model size by 5× and data size by 2×; with a 100× compute increase, model size by 25× and data by 4×. Hoffmann et al. 2022 argued instead that model size and data size should increase at the same rate. With a 10× compute increase, both should grow by about 3.1×; with a 100× compute increase, both should grow by 10×. The key difference is parameter-heavy scaling versus balanced parameter-token scaling.

Key concepts: Kaplan vs Hoffmann, compute allocation, model scaling, data scaling, balanced growth

metadata.index_text: Slide 78 | Kaplan versus Hoffmann | Kaplan prioritizes model size, Hoffmann scales model and data at same rate
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide directly compares the two scaling recommendations. Kaplan et al. 2020 suggested that when compute increases, one should prioritize increasing model size more than data size. The slide states that with a 10× compute increase, Kaplan would increase model size by 5× and data size by 2×; with a 100× compute increase, model size by 25× and data by 4×. Hoffmann et al. 2022 argued instead that model size and data size should increase at the same rate. With a 10× compute increase, both should grow by about 3.1×; with a 100× compute increase, both should grow by 10×. The key difference is parameter-heavy scaling versus balanced parameter-token scaling.

text:
How to Allocate Compute Budget

This slide shows a table comparing models by parameter count and training tokens. It lists LaMDA at 137B parameters and 168B tokens, GPT-3 at 175B and 300B tokens, Jurassic at 178B and 300B tokens, Gopher at 280B and 300B tokens, MT-NLG at 530B and 270B tokens, and Chinchilla at 70B and 1.4T tokens. The contrast is striking: Chinchilla is much smaller than many listed models but uses far more training tokens. The slide illustrates the practical implication of compute-optimal training: many earlier large models had high parameter counts but comparatively low token counts, while Chinchilla reallocates compute toward more data.

Key concepts: compute allocation, training tokens, LaMDA, GPT-3, Gopher, MT-NLG, Chinchilla

metadata.index_text: Slide 79 | Allocate compute budget | table comparing model parameters and training tokens, Chinchilla 70B with 1.4T tokens
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows a table comparing models by parameter count and training tokens. It lists LaMDA at 137B parameters and 168B tokens, GPT-3 at 175B and 300B tokens, Jurassic at 178B and 300B tokens, Gopher at 280B and 300B tokens, MT-NLG at 530B and 270B tokens, and Chinchilla at 70B and 1.4T tokens. The contrast is striking: Chinchilla is much smaller than many listed models but uses far more training tokens. The slide illustrates the practical implication of compute-optimal training: many earlier large models had high parameter counts but comparatively low token counts, while Chinchilla reallocates compute toward more data.

text:
How to Allocate Compute Budget: Tokens per Parameter

This slide gives the final rule of thumb from Chinchilla: to train a compute-optimal model, use about 20 training tokens for every parameter. The table shows ratios for Chinchilla, LLaMA, Phi-3, and Llama 3, illustrating that modern models often use hundreds or thousands of tokens per parameter, especially smaller but heavily trained models. The TLDR at the bottom says Chinchilla recommends around 20 tokens per parameter, but modern models may use approximately 20 times or more tokens per parameter. The key lesson is that data quantity has become central: effective LLM design is no longer only about choosing a large parameter count, but about matching parameters with enough tokens.

Key concepts: tokens per parameter, Chinchilla rule, 20 tokens per parameter, LLaMA, Phi-3, Llama 3, data-centric training

text:
Lecture References

This closing slide lists the main references used for the lecture. The first is the DeepLearning.AI course 'Generative AI with Large Language Models,' which provides broad educational coverage of LLM development. The second is Kaplan et al. 2020, 'Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models,' which introduced influential empirical scaling relationships for loss, data, model size, and compute. The third is Hoffmann et al. 2022, 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models,' which revised compute allocation recommendations through the Chinchilla result. The fourth is a lecture on scaling laws of LLMs by Prof. Pawan Goyal from NPTEL IIT Kharagpur. These references show that the lecture combines course material, research papers, and academic lectures.

Key concepts: lecture references, DeepLearning.AI, Kaplan 2020, Hoffmann 2022, Chinchilla, scaling laws

text:
Fine-tuning, Instruction Tuning and PERF

This opening slide introduces the lecture topic: adapting large language models after pre-training. The title emphasizes fine-tuning and instruction tuning, which are techniques used to make a general-purpose model better at following tasks such as classification, summarization, translation, dialogue summarization, and domain-specific response generation. The visual on the left shows a human head connected to digital symbols such as a CPU, gears, a monitor, and cloud-like circuitry, reinforcing the idea that generative AI combines computation, data, and human-like language behavior. Although the title writes PERF, the content of the lecture later focuses on PEFT, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, which is a practical way to adapt large models without updating every parameter. This slide therefore sets the direction of the lecture: moving from prompting alone toward training-based adaptation and efficient model customization.

Key concepts: fine-tuning, instruction tuning, LLM adaptation, PEFT, generative AI lifecycle

The visual on the left shows a human head connected to digital symbols such as a CPU, gears, a monitor, and cloud-like circuitry, reinforcing the idea that generative AI combines computation, data, and human-like language behavior. text:
Fine-tuning with instruction prompts

This slide acts as a section divider for the first major part of the lecture. The phrase fine-tuning with instruction prompts means that the model is not only trained on raw text, but on examples written as instructions and expected responses. Instead of showing the model only a sentence and a label, we can show it a full prompt such as 'Classify this review' and the desired completion such as 'Positive'. This makes the training format closer to how users interact with instruction-following LLMs. The slide signals that the lecture will explain how prompts, completions, labels, and task examples are converted into a training dataset that improves the model's ability to follow similar instructions later.

Key concepts: instruction prompts, prompt-completion pairs, instruction-following, supervised fine-tuning

The phrase fine-tuning with instruction prompts means that the model is not only trained on raw text, but on examples written as instructions and expected responses. Instead of showing the model only a sentence and a label, we can show it a full prompt such as 'Classify this review' and the desired completion such as 'Positive'. This makes the training format closer to how users interact with instruction-following LLMs. The slide signals that the lecture will explain how prompts, completions, labels, and task examples are converted into a training dataset that improves the model's ability to follow similar instructions later.

text:
GenAI project lifecycle

The slide presents the Generative AI project lifecycle as a sequence of stages. It begins with Scope, where the problem or use case is defined, then Select, where an existing model is chosen or a new one is pretrained. The next major block is Adapt and align model, which includes prompt engineering, fine-tuning, alignment with human feedback, and evaluation. The final block is Application integration, where the model is optimized and deployed for inference, then augmented and used to build LLM-powered applications. The placement of fine-tuning inside the adaptation stage is important: it shows that fine-tuning is not the starting point of a project, but a method used after the problem and model have been selected, when prompting alone is not enough or when stronger task-specific behavior is required.

Key concepts: GenAI lifecycle, scope, model selection, adaptation, fine-tuning, deployment

It begins with Scope, where the problem or use case is defined, then Select, where an existing model is chosen or a new one is pretrained. The next major block is Adapt and align model, which includes prompt engineering, fine-tuning, alignment with human feedback, and evaluation. The final block is Application integration, where the model is optimized and deployed for inference, then augmented and used to build LLM-powered applications. The placement of fine-tuning inside the adaptation stage is important: it shows that fine-tuning is not the starting point of a project, but a method used after the problem and model have been selected, when prompting alone is not enough or when stronger task-specific behavior is required.

text:
GenAI project lifecycle - fine-tuning focus

This slide repeats the project lifecycle to keep attention on where fine-tuning fits. A generative AI system is usually built through a pipeline: first define the problem, then choose a model, then adapt the model, evaluate it, optimize it, and integrate it into an application. Fine-tuning belongs to the Adapt and align model stage because it changes the model's behavior using training examples. It is different from prompt engineering, which changes only the input prompt at inference time, and different from deployment optimization, which focuses on speed, memory, and cost. The slide helps students understand that fine-tuning should be justified by the use case, dataset, and evaluation results rather than applied automatically.

Key concepts: fine-tuning stage, adapt and align, evaluation, model deployment, application integration

Fine-tuning belongs to the Adapt and align model stage because it changes the model's behavior using training examples. It is different from prompt engineering, which changes only the input prompt at inference time, and different from deployment optimization, which focuses on speed, memory, and cost. The slide helps students understand that fine-tuning should be justified by the use case, dataset, and evaluation results rather than applied automatically.

text:
Fine-tuning an LLM with instruction prompts

This section title introduces the idea that an LLM can be improved by training it on instruction-style examples. In an instruction dataset, every sample usually contains a user request, possibly some input text, and an expected output. For example, a prompt may ask the model to classify a review, summarize a dialogue, or translate a sentence, while the completion provides the correct answer. Fine-tuning on this format teaches the model the mapping between instructions and outputs. The key idea is that the model is not memorizing one prompt; it is learning a reusable behavior pattern, such as recognizing that a sentiment prompt should end with a sentiment label.

Key concepts: instruction fine-tuning, task-specific behavior, training examples, prompt format

Fine-tuning on this format teaches the model the mapping between instructions and outputs. The key idea is that the model is not memorizing one prompt; it is learning a reusable behavior pattern, such as recognizing that a sentiment prompt should end with a sentiment label.

text:
In-context learning (ICL) - zero-shot inference

This slide shows a basic zero-shot inference example. The prompt asks the LLM to classify the review 'I loved this DVD!' and leaves the sentiment field blank. The model produces the completion 'Positive' without being given an example in the prompt. This is called zero-shot because the model must rely on its pretrained knowledge and instruction-following ability rather than demonstrations inside the current context window. The diagram separates the process into three parts: the prompt provided by the user, the model that processes it, and the completion generated by the model. The example is simple, but it illustrates a central property of modern LLMs: they can often infer the task from natural-language instructions alone.

Key concepts: zero-shot inference, in-context learning, sentiment classification, prompt, completion

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 6; title: In-context learning (ICL) - zero-shot inference; key_concepts: zero-shot inference, in-context learning, sentiment classification, prompt, completion; extracted_text: In-context learning (ICL) - zero shot inference Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Sentiment: LLM Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Sentiment: Positive Prompt Completion Model 6
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows a basic zero-shot inference example. The prompt asks the LLM to classify the review 'I loved this DVD!' and leaves the sentiment field blank. The model produces the completion 'Positive' without being given an example in the prompt. This is called zero-shot because the model must rely on its pretrained knowledge and instruction-following ability rather than demonstrations inside the current context window. The diagram separates the process into three parts: the prompt provided by the user, the model that processes it, and the completion generated by the model. The example is simple, but it illustrates a central property of modern LLMs: they can often infer the task from natural-language instructions alone.

text:
In-context learning failure in zero-shot inference

This slide shows that zero-shot prompting does not always produce the desired output. The prompt again asks for the sentiment of 'I loved this DVD!', but the displayed completion is an incorrect and incoherent continuation: 'eived a very nice book review'. The point is that without examples or fine-tuning, the model may misunderstand the expected output format, generate irrelevant text, or continue the prompt as ordinary text rather than producing a label. This is especially likely for smaller or weaker models, ambiguous prompts, or tasks where the model has not been strongly instruction-tuned. The slide motivates why relying only on zero-shot prompting may be insufficient for production systems or specialized classroom/project tasks.

Key concepts: zero-shot limitation, incorrect completion, format control, prompt ambiguity, need for fine-tuning

text:
In-context learning (ICL) - one/few-shot inference

This slide shows one-shot or few-shot inference, where the prompt includes at least one solved example before the new query. The first example says 'I loved this DVD!' has sentiment Positive. Then the model is asked to classify 'I don’t like this chair.' Because the prompt demonstrates the desired input-output pattern, the LLM can infer that it should return the sentiment label Negative. This is in-context learning because the model's parameters are not updated; the model only uses the examples inside the current prompt as temporary guidance. The slide highlights why examples are useful: they define the task, the output format, and the style of response expected from the model.

Key concepts: one-shot inference, few-shot inference, demonstrations, temporary task learning, sentiment labels

The first example says 'I loved this DVD!' has sentiment Positive. Then the model is asked to classify 'I don’t like this chair.' Because the prompt demonstrates the desired input-output pattern, the LLM can infer that it should return the sentiment label Negative. This is in-context learning because the model's parameters are not updated; the model only uses the examples inside the current prompt as temporary guidance. The slide highlights why examples are useful: they define the task, the output format, and the style of response expected from the model.

text:
Limitations of in-context learning

This slide explains why in-context learning is not always enough. The left side shows a context window filled with several labeled sentiment examples: positive and negative reviews followed by a new unlabeled review, 'Who would use this product?' The right side states two important limitations. First, in-context learning may not work well for smaller models because they may not reliably infer the task from examples. Second, examples consume space in the context window, leaving less room for the actual user input, retrieved documents, or long conversations. The slide concludes that when repeated examples are needed to get acceptable behavior, fine-tuning may be a better strategy because the task pattern is learned into the model rather than repeated in every prompt.

Key concepts: ICL limitations, context window, small models, few-shot examples, fine-tuning motivation

text:
LLM fine-tuning at a high level - pre-training

This slide contrasts pre-training with later fine-tuning by first showing the pre-training stage. A pre-trained LLM is built from very large quantities of unstructured text, often measured in gigabytes, terabytes, or petabytes. The repeated TEXT boxes represent raw textual data that may come from books, webpages, articles, code, and other large corpora. During pre-training, the model learns general language patterns, world knowledge, grammar, reasoning traces, and statistical relationships between tokens. The important point is that pre-training creates a broad foundation model, but it does not necessarily make the model excellent at a specific downstream task or a particular instruction format.

Key concepts: pre-training, unstructured text, foundation model, large corpus, general language learning

text:
LLM fine-tuning at a high level - labeled task examples

This slide introduces fine-tuning as a second training stage after pre-training. Instead of using only unstructured text, fine-tuning uses labeled examples for a specific task or set of tasks. The dataset entries are represented as TEXT plus LABEL pairs, meaning each input has a target answer. A pre-trained LLM enters the fine-tuning process and becomes a fine-tuned LLM with improved performance on the target task. The diagram emphasizes that fine-tuning is supervised: the model compares its predicted output to the correct label and updates its parameters accordingly. This is how a general language model becomes more specialized for tasks such as sentiment classification, summarization, or support-response generation.

Key concepts: supervised fine-tuning, labeled examples, task-specific dataset, fine-tuned LLM, model specialization

text:
LLM fine-tuning at a high level - prompt-completion pairs

This slide refines the fine-tuning dataset format by showing PROMPT and COMPLETION pairs. In instruction tuning, the prompt contains the task instruction and input, while the completion contains the desired model response. This is more natural for LLMs than a simple feature-label table because it matches the way users interact with models at inference time. The diagram shows a pre-trained LLM being fine-tuned on task-specific examples and producing improved performance. The key intuition is that the model learns not only the correct answers, but also how to interpret task instructions, maintain output style, and follow expected response formats.

Key concepts: prompt-completion pair, instruction tuning, task examples, improved performance, output format learning

text:
Using prompts to fine-tune LLMs with instruction - classification example

This slide demonstrates that each training pair can include an explicit instruction. The examples use sentiment classification: one prompt asks about 'I loved this DVD!' and the completion is Positive; another asks about 'I don’t like this chair.' and the completion is Negative. The diagram shows these examples used to convert a pre-trained LLM into a fine-tuned LLM. The purpose of including the instruction inside every example is to teach the model what kind of task is being requested, not just which text maps to which label. This format is especially useful when the same model will be asked to perform many tasks through natural-language instructions.

Key concepts: instruction dataset, classification prompt, sentiment completion, fine-tuned model, natural-language supervision

text:
Using prompts to fine-tune LLMs with instruction - multiple task templates

This slide shows that instruction fine-tuning can include different kinds of tasks, not only sentiment classification. Example prompts include summarizing a text and translating a sentence, each paired with its expected completion. The training set is still represented as prompt-completion pairs, but the instructions vary. This teaches the model to recognize different task types from natural language and respond appropriately. The slide is important because it separates ordinary single-task fine-tuning from broader instruction tuning: a model trained on many instruction templates can become more general and helpful across different user requests.

Key concepts: multi-task prompts, summarization, translation, instruction templates, generalization

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows that instruction fine-tuning can include different kinds of tasks, not only sentiment classification. Example prompts include summarizing a text and translating a sentence, each paired with its expected completion. The training set is still represented as prompt-completion pairs, but the instructions vary. This teaches the model to recognize different task types from natural language and respond appropriately. The slide is important because it separates ordinary single-task fine-tuning from broader instruction tuning: a model trained on many instruction templates can become more general and helpful across different user requests.

text:
Using prompts to fine-tune LLMs with instruction - full fine-tuning

This slide states that full fine-tuning updates all parameters of the model. The visual shows prompt-completion examples entering the fine-tuning process, a pre-trained LLM becoming a fine-tuned LLM, and improved performance as the goal. Updating all parameters means the entire neural network is allowed to change in response to the training data. This can produce strong task performance, but it is computationally expensive and may change the model's general behavior. Later slides introduce PEFT as a more efficient alternative that updates only a small set of parameters while keeping most of the base model frozen.

Key concepts: full fine-tuning, all parameters, training cost, task performance, model update

Updating all parameters means the entire neural network is allowed to change in response to the training data. text:
Sample prompt instruction templates

This slide provides concrete examples of instruction templates from PromptSource. The first template is for classification or sentiment analysis: it inserts a product review into a prompt and asks the model to predict the associated rating from possible choices. The second template is for text generation: it asks the model to generate a star-rated review about a product title, using placeholders such as star_rating, product_title, and review_body. The third template is for summarization: it asks for a short sentence describing a product review. The highlighted parts show the instruction words that tell the model what to do. The slide teaches that prompt design is not random; templates systematically define the input variables, task wording, and expected output field.

Key concepts: PromptSource, instruction template, classification, text generation, summarization, Jinja placeholders

The second template is for text generation: it asks the model to generate a star-rated review about a product title, using placeholders such as star_rating, product_title, and review_body. The third template is for summarization: it asks for a short sentence describing a product review. The highlighted parts show the instruction words that tell the model what to do. The slide teaches that prompt design is not random; templates systematically define the input variables, task wording, and expected output field.

text:
LLM fine-tuning process - dataset splits

This slide outlines the full fine-tuning workflow using a prepared instruction dataset. The dataset is divided into training, validation, and test splits. The training split is used to update the model's parameters; the validation split is used during development to monitor performance and tune decisions such as training duration; the test split is kept for final evaluation after the model has been trained. This separation prevents the model from being evaluated only on examples it has already seen. The slide also reinforces that prompt-completion pairs are the basic training unit, and that a rigorous fine-tuning experiment must include proper data splitting.

Key concepts: training split, validation split, test split, instruction dataset, evaluation protocol

The training split is used to update the model's parameters; the validation split is used during development to monitor performance and tune decisions such as training duration; the test split is kept for final evaluation after the model has been trained. This separation prevents the model from being evaluated only on examples it has already seen. The slide also reinforces that prompt-completion pairs are the basic training unit, and that a rigorous fine-tuning experiment must include proper data splitting.

text:
LLM fine-tuning process - prediction versus label

This slide shows how fine-tuning compares the model's completion to the correct label. The prompt asks the model to classify 'I loved this DVD!' with the sentiment field blank. The pre-trained model produces an incorrect completion, Neutral, while the label in the prepared dataset is Positive. This mismatch is the training signal. The model is not simply told that it is wrong in natural language; instead, a loss function measures how far its predicted probability distribution is from the correct target. Fine-tuning uses many such examples to gradually make correct completions more likely for similar prompts.

Key concepts: model completion, label, training signal, prediction error, sentiment classification

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows how fine-tuning compares the model's completion to the correct label. The prompt asks the model to classify 'I loved this DVD!' with the sentiment field blank. The pre-trained model produces an incorrect completion, Neutral, while the label in the prepared dataset is Positive. This mismatch is the training signal. The model is not simply told that it is wrong in natural language; instead, a loss function measures how far its predicted probability distribution is from the correct target. Fine-tuning uses many such examples to gradually make correct completions more likely for similar prompts.

text:
LLM fine-tuning process - cross-entropy loss

This slide adds the loss function to the fine-tuning process. The prompt is shown, the model produces a completion, and the correct label is available. Cross-entropy loss measures how much probability the model assigned to the correct answer. If the correct label is Positive but the model predicts Neutral with high confidence, the loss is large. During training, gradients are computed from this loss and used to adjust the model parameters so that the correct token or label becomes more likely next time. This is the mathematical mechanism behind supervised fine-tuning: repeated loss minimization over prompt-completion examples.

Key concepts: cross-entropy, loss function, gradient update, supervised learning, correct label probability

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 19; title: LLM fine-tuning process - cross-entropy loss; key_concepts: cross-entropy, loss function, gradient update, supervised learning, correct label probability; extracted_text: LLM fine-tuning process Pre-trained LLM Model Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Cross-entropy loss measures how much probability the model assigned to the correct answer. If the correct label is Positive but the model predicts Neutral with high confidence, the loss is large. During training, gradients are computed from this loss and used to adjust the model parameters so that the correct token or label becomes more likely next time. This is the mathematical mechanism behind supervised fine-tuning: repeated loss minimization over prompt-completion examples.

text:
LLM fine-tuning process - validation accuracy

This slide highlights validation accuracy as a metric used during training. The training split updates the model, but the validation split is used to check how well the model performs on examples that were not used for parameter updates. Validation accuracy can reveal overfitting: if training performance improves but validation performance stops improving or decreases, the model may be memorizing training examples rather than learning a generalizable task pattern. The slide therefore connects dataset splitting to model selection, early stopping, and responsible experimental design. Fine-tuning is not only about training; it is also about monitoring whether training improves generalization.

Key concepts: validation accuracy, overfitting, generalization, model monitoring, training dataset

text:
LLM fine-tuning process - test accuracy

This slide highlights test accuracy as the final held-out evaluation measure. After the model is trained and any development decisions are made using validation results, the test set is used to estimate performance on unseen examples. The test set should not guide repeated tuning, otherwise it stops being an unbiased evaluation. In the context of instruction fine-tuning, test accuracy tells us whether the model can follow the task instruction correctly on new prompts. This slide completes the experimental workflow: train on training examples, tune or monitor on validation examples, then report final results on test examples.

Key concepts: test accuracy, held-out evaluation, unseen examples, final performance, experimental integrity

text:
LLM fine-tuning process - from pre-trained to instruct model

This slide summarizes the outcome of instruction fine-tuning. A pre-trained LLM becomes an instruct LLM after training on instruction-style examples. The difference is behavioral: the pre-trained model has broad language knowledge, while the instruct model is better at interpreting user requests and producing useful responses in the requested format. The slide uses a simple before-and-after diagram to emphasize that instruction tuning changes the model's interaction style. This is why many deployed models are not only pretrained models; they have also undergone instruction tuning and often additional alignment stages.

Key concepts: pre-trained LLM, instruct LLM, instruction following, behavioral adaptation, model alignment

The slide uses a simple before-and-after diagram to emphasize that instruction tuning changes the model's interaction style. This is why many deployed models are not only pretrained models; they have also undergone instruction tuning and often additional alignment stages.

text:
Fine-tuning on a single task

This section divider introduces single-task fine-tuning. In this setting, the training dataset focuses on one specific capability, such as summarization, sentiment classification, or named entity recognition. Single-task fine-tuning can be effective when the application has a narrow objective and a consistent output format. However, because the model repeatedly sees one task style, it may become very specialized. The following slides explain both the benefit and the risk of this approach, especially the possibility of catastrophic forgetting when performance on other tasks decreases.

Key concepts: single-task fine-tuning, task specialization, narrow objective, catastrophic forgetting

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 23; title: Fine-tuning on a single task; key_concepts: single-task fine-tuning, task specialization, narrow objective, catastrophic forgetting; extracted_text: Fine-tuning on a single task 23
metadata.slide_paragraph: This section divider introduces single-task fine-tuning. In this setting, the training dataset focuses on one specific capability, such as summarization, sentiment classification, or named entity recognition. Single-task fine-tuning can be effective when the application has a narrow objective and a consistent output format. However, because the model repeatedly sees one task style, it may become very specialized. The following slides explain both the benefit and the risk of this approach, especially the possibility of catastrophic forgetting when performance on other tasks decreases.

text:
Fine-tuning on a single task - summarization example

This slide shows an example of single-task fine-tuning for summarization. A pre-trained LLM is trained on a dataset where every example asks the model to summarize some text and provides an expected completion. The slide notes that often only 500 to 1000 examples may be needed to fine-tune a single task. This is because the base model already understands language; fine-tuning mainly teaches it a specific behavior, format, or domain. The limitation is that the model sees one repeated instruction type, so it becomes optimized for that task. This is useful for a focused application such as dialogue summarization, but it may not preserve broad general-purpose ability.

Key concepts: summarization fine-tuning, single-task dataset, 500-1000 examples, instruct model, task-specific performance

The limitation is that the model sees one repeated instruction type, so it becomes optimized for that task. This is useful for a focused application such as dialogue summarization, but it may not preserve broad general-purpose ability.

text:
Catastrophic forgetting - before fine-tuning

This slide begins the explanation of catastrophic forgetting by showing the model before fine-tuning. The sentiment prompt asks about 'I loved this DVD!', but the model generates an irrelevant completion rather than the label Positive. This illustrates the weakness that motivates task-specific fine-tuning: the model may not naturally produce the desired output format. The phrase 'before fine-tuning' is important because the slide establishes a baseline. In the next slide, fine-tuning improves this specific task, but later slides show the trade-off: improving one task can sometimes damage performance on other tasks.

Key concepts: catastrophic forgetting, baseline behavior, before fine-tuning, sentiment task, incorrect output

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 25; title: Catastrophic forgetting - before fine-tuning; key_concepts: catastrophic forgetting, baseline behavior, before fine-tuning, sentiment task, incorrect output; extracted_text: Catastrophic forgetting ● Fine-tuning can significantly increase the performance of a model on a specific task… Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Sentiment: LLM Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Sentiment: eived a very nice book review Before fine-tuning Prompt Completion Model 25
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide begins the explanation of catastrophic forgetting by showing the model before fine-tuning. The sentiment prompt asks about 'I loved this DVD!', but the model generates an irrelevant completion rather than the label Positive. This illustrates the weakness that motivates task-specific fine-tuning: the model may not naturally produce the desired output format. The phrase 'before fine-tuning' is important because the slide establishes a baseline. In the next slide, fine-tuning improves this specific task, but later slides show the trade-off: improving one task can sometimes damage performance on other tasks.

text:
Catastrophic forgetting - task improvement after fine-tuning

This slide shows the direct benefit of fine-tuning. After being trained on sentiment examples, the same model now outputs POSITIVE for the prompt 'I loved this DVD! Sentiment:'. This means fine-tuning successfully taught the model the expected task and output format. The slide highlights why fine-tuning is attractive: it can transform unreliable prompting behavior into consistent task performance. However, the word 'specific' in the slide matters. The improvement is measured on the target task, and it does not guarantee that all other abilities of the model remain unchanged.

Key concepts: task improvement, after fine-tuning, sentiment classification, output format, supervised adaptation

Sentiment: LLM Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Sentiment: POSITIVE After fine-tuning Prompt Completion Model 26
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows the direct benefit of fine-tuning. After being trained on sentiment examples, the same model now outputs POSITIVE for the prompt 'I loved this DVD! Sentiment:'. This means fine-tuning successfully taught the model the expected task and output format. The slide highlights why fine-tuning is attractive: it can transform unreliable prompting behavior into consistent task performance. However, the word 'specific' in the slide matters. The improvement is measured on the target task, and it does not guarantee that all other abilities of the model remain unchanged.

text:
Catastrophic forgetting - general ability before fine-tuning

This slide shifts from the fine-tuned sentiment task to a different task: answering a question from a short context. Before fine-tuning, the model reads 'Charlie the cat roamed the garden at night' and correctly answers that the cat's name is Charlie. This shows that the base model had useful general reading-comprehension ability. The slide is setting up the contrast: a model can be good at a broad task before fine-tuning, but if it is aggressively fine-tuned on a narrow task, its general behavior may become distorted. Catastrophic forgetting refers to this loss of previously learned abilities.

Key concepts: reading comprehension, general ability, before fine-tuning, question answering, catastrophic forgetting setup

metadata.slide_title: Catastrophic forgetting - general ability before fine-tuning
metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 27; title: Catastrophic forgetting - general ability before fine-tuning; key_concepts: reading comprehension, general ability, before fine-tuning, question answering, catastrophic forgetting setup; extracted_text: Catastrophic forgetting ● …but can lead to reduction in ability on other tasks What is the name of the cat? Charlie the cat roamed the garden at night. LLM What is the name of the cat? Charlie the cat roamed the garden at night. Charlie Before fine-tuning Prompt Completion Model 27
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shifts from the fine-tuned sentiment task to a different task: answering a question from a short context. Before fine-tuning, the model reads 'Charlie the cat roamed the garden at night' and correctly answers that the cat's name is Charlie. This shows that the base model had useful general reading-comprehension ability. The slide is setting up the contrast: a model can be good at a broad task before fine-tuning, but if it is aggressively fine-tuned on a narrow task, its general behavior may become distorted. Catastrophic forgetting refers to this loss of previously learned abilities.

text:
Catastrophic forgetting - ability reduction after fine-tuning

This slide demonstrates catastrophic forgetting in an exaggerated but clear way. After fine-tuning heavily on sentiment classification, the model is asked a question-answering task about a cat named Charlie. Instead of answering 'Charlie', it outputs 'The garden was positive.' The model has transferred the sentiment-label behavior into an unrelated task, which shows that its original ability has been harmed or its output style has become overly biased toward the fine-tuning task. The key lesson is that fine-tuning can improve one target capability while reducing performance elsewhere, especially if the dataset is narrow, the training is too aggressive, or too many parameters are updated.

Key concepts: catastrophic forgetting, negative transfer, over-specialization, question answering failure, task interference

metadata.slide_title: Catastrophic forgetting - ability reduction after fine-tuning

Charlie the cat roamed the garden at night. LLM What is the name of the cat? Charlie the cat roamed the garden at night. The garden was positive. After fine-tuning Prompt Completion Model 28
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide demonstrates catastrophic forgetting in an exaggerated but clear way. After fine-tuning heavily on sentiment classification, the model is asked a question-answering task about a cat named Charlie. Instead of answering 'Charlie', it outputs 'The garden was positive.' The model has transferred the sentiment-label behavior into an unrelated task, which shows that its original ability has been harmed or its output style has become overly biased toward the fine-tuning task. The key lesson is that fine-tuning can improve one target capability while reducing performance elsewhere, especially if the dataset is narrow, the training is too aggressive, or too many parameters are updated.

text:
How to avoid catastrophic forgetting

This slide lists strategies for dealing with catastrophic forgetting. The first point says that sometimes you may not need to avoid it: if the model is intended only for one narrow task, losing unrelated abilities may not matter. The second strategy is multi-task fine-tuning, where the model trains on many task types at once so it preserves broader instruction-following behavior. The third strategy is parameter-efficient fine-tuning, or PEFT, which updates only a small portion of the model or adds small trainable modules while keeping most original weights frozen. These approaches reduce the risk that a narrow dataset will overwrite the model's general knowledge and capabilities.

Key concepts: avoid forgetting, multi-task fine-tuning, PEFT, frozen weights, task balance

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 29; title: How to avoid catastrophic forgetting; key_concepts: avoid forgetting, multi-task fine-tuning, PEFT, frozen weights, task balance; extracted_text: How to avoid catastrophic forgetting ● First note that you might not have to! ● Fine-tune on multiple tasks at the same time ● Consider Parameter Efficient Fine-tuning (PEFT) 29
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide lists strategies for dealing with catastrophic forgetting. The first point says that sometimes you may not need to avoid it: if the model is intended only for one narrow task, losing unrelated abilities may not matter. The second strategy is multi-task fine-tuning, where the model trains on many task types at once so it preserves broader instruction-following behavior. The third strategy is parameter-efficient fine-tuning, or PEFT, which updates only a small portion of the model or adds small trainable modules while keeping most original weights frozen. These approaches reduce the risk that a narrow dataset will overwrite the model's general knowledge and capabilities.

text:
Multi-task, instruction fine-tuning

This section divider introduces multi-task instruction fine-tuning. The main idea is to train a model on many instruction types rather than only one. For example, the dataset might include summarization, translation, classification, question answering, code generation, and information extraction. By seeing many tasks, the model learns a more general skill: follow the instruction and produce the requested type of output. This approach is closely related to how general instruct models are created, and it can reduce the risk of the model becoming too specialized in a single output pattern.

Key concepts: multi-task instruction tuning, generalization, instruction diversity, task mixture

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 30; title: Multi-task, instruction fine-tuning; key_concepts: multi-task instruction tuning, generalization, instruction diversity, task mixture; extracted_text: Multi-task, instruction fine-tuning 30
metadata.slide_paragraph: This section divider introduces multi-task instruction fine-tuning. The main idea is to train a model on many instruction types rather than only one. For example, the dataset might include summarization, translation, classification, question answering, code generation, and information extraction. By seeing many tasks, the model learns a more general skill: follow the instruction and produce the requested type of output. This approach is closely related to how general instruct models are created, and it can reduce the risk of the model becoming too specialized in a single output pattern.

text:
Multi-task instruction fine-tuning - many tasks

This slide visualizes a pre-trained LLM being instruction fine-tuned on many tasks. The examples include 'Summarize the following text', 'Rate this review', 'Translate into Python code', and 'Identify the places'. Each instruction has an example text and completion. The purpose is to expose the model to many task formats so it learns the general concept of following instructions, not just one mapping such as review-to-sentiment. The diagram implies that the training data must be diverse in both input content and desired output type. A strong instruction-tuned model becomes more useful because it can respond to a wider range of user prompts.

Key concepts: multi-task dataset, summarization, review rating, code translation, place identification

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 31; title: Multi-task instruction fine-tuning - many tasks; key_concepts: multi-task dataset, summarization, review rating, code translation, place identification; extracted_text: Multi-task, instruction fine-tuning Pre-trained LLM Model Instruction fine-tune on many tasks Summarize the following text: Rate this review: Translate into Python code: Identify the places: [EXAMPLE TEXT] [EXAMPLE COMPLETION] … 31
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide visualizes a pre-trained LLM being instruction fine-tuned on many tasks. The examples include 'Summarize the following text', 'Rate this review', 'Translate into Python code', and 'Identify the places'. Each instruction has an example text and completion. The purpose is to expose the model to many task formats so it learns the general concept of following instructions, not just one mapping such as review-to-sentiment. The diagram implies that the training data must be diverse in both input content and desired output type. A strong instruction-tuned model becomes more useful because it can respond to a wider range of user prompts.

text:
Multi-task instruction fine-tuning - instruct LLM output

This slide completes the multi-task fine-tuning diagram by showing the output as an instruct LLM. The slide notes that many examples of each task are needed for training. This is important because multi-task training is broader and requires enough coverage for every task category; otherwise, the model may learn some tasks well and others poorly. The final instruct LLM should be better at recognizing varied instructions and adapting its response style. The trade-off is data and compute: multi-task instruction tuning generally needs larger and more carefully balanced datasets than single-task fine-tuning.

Key concepts: instruct LLM, many examples, task coverage, balanced training, instruction generalization

metadata.slide_title: Multi-task instruction fine-tuning - instruct LLM output
