metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide completes the multi-task fine-tuning diagram by showing the output as an instruct LLM. The slide notes that many examples of each task are needed for training. This is important because multi-task training is broader and requires enough coverage for every task category; otherwise, the model may learn some tasks well and others poorly. The final instruct LLM should be better at recognizing varied instructions and adapting its response style. The trade-off is data and compute: multi-task instruction tuning generally needs larger and more carefully balanced datasets than single-task fine-tuning.

text:
Instruction fine-tuning with FLAN

This slide introduces FLAN as a family of instruction fine-tuning datasets or procedures. It states that FLAN models refer to a specific set of instructions used to perform instruction fine-tuning. The dessert metaphor says FLAN is like the 'metaphorical dessert' after the 'main course' of pretraining: pretraining provides the main language knowledge, while FLAN-style instruction tuning makes the model more pleasant and useful for following tasks. The slide does not present equations, but it communicates an important workflow: large-scale pretraining comes first, then instruction fine-tuning improves usability and task responsiveness.

Key concepts: FLAN, instruction fine-tuning, pretraining, Fine-tuned Language Net, instruct models

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 33; title: Instruction fine-tuning with FLAN; key_concepts: FLAN, instruction fine-tuning, pretraining, Fine-tuned Language Net, instruct models; extracted_text: ● FLAN models refer to a specific set of instructions used to perform instruction fine-tuning Instruction fine-tuning with FLAN “The metaphorical dessert to the main course of pretraining” FLAN 33
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces FLAN as a family of instruction fine-tuning datasets or procedures. It states that FLAN models refer to a specific set of instructions used to perform instruction fine-tuning. The dessert metaphor says FLAN is like the 'metaphorical dessert' after the 'main course' of pretraining: pretraining provides the main language knowledge, while FLAN-style instruction tuning makes the model more pleasant and useful for following tasks. The slide does not present equations, but it communicates an important workflow: large-scale pretraining comes first, then instruction fine-tuning improves usability and task responsiveness.

text:
Instruction fine-tuning with FLAN - model examples

This slide shows how FLAN instruction tuning can be applied to different base models. T5 becomes FLAN-T5 after being instruction-tuned with FLAN-style tasks, and PaLM becomes FLAN-PaLM. The arrows indicate transformation: the base model remains the starting point, but its behavior is changed by training on a broad instruction mixture. The visual is useful because it clarifies that FLAN is not only one model architecture; it is a tuning approach or dataset collection that can be used to create instruction-following versions of existing models. The result is usually stronger zero-shot and few-shot performance across many tasks.

Key concepts: FLAN-T5, FLAN-PaLM, base model, instruction mixture, zero-shot improvement

metadata.slide_title: Instruction fine-tuning with FLAN - model examples

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows how FLAN instruction tuning can be applied to different base models. T5 becomes FLAN-T5 after being instruction-tuned with FLAN-style tasks, and PaLM becomes FLAN-PaLM. The arrows indicate transformation: the base model remains the starting point, but its behavior is changed by training on a broad instruction mixture. The visual is useful because it clarifies that FLAN is not only one model architecture; it is a tuning approach or dataset collection that can be used to create instruction-following versions of existing models. The result is usually stronger zero-shot and few-shot performance across many tasks.

text:
FLAN-T5: Fine-tuned version of pre-trained T5 model

This slide summarizes the scale and diversity of tasks used in FLAN-T5-style instruction tuning. It lists several task collections: T0-SF with 55 datasets, 14 categories, and 193 tasks; Muffin with 69 datasets, 27 categories, and 80 tasks; Chain-of-Thought reasoning with 9 datasets and 9 tasks; and Natural Instructions with 372 datasets, 108 categories, and 1554 tasks. The examples include commonsense reasoning, question generation, closed-book QA, code instruction generation, summarization, natural language inference, named entity recognition, and toxic language detection. The message is that FLAN-T5 is general-purpose because it was exposed to many instruction forms, not because it was trained on one narrow task.

Key concepts: FLAN-T5, T5, Natural Instructions, Chain-of-Thought, task diversity, general-purpose instruct model

metadata.slide_title: FLAN-T5: Fine-tuned version of pre-trained T5 model

text:
SAMSum: A dialogue dataset

This slide introduces SAMSum as a dialogue summarization dataset used in prompt-based fine-tuning. The screenshot shows a dataset interface with columns for dialogue and summary. Each example contains a short conversation and a human-written summary. This makes it suitable for training a model to read multi-speaker dialogue and produce concise summaries. The slide connects a real dataset to the earlier concept of prompt-completion pairs: the dialogue can be placed inside a prompt, and the summary becomes the expected completion. SAMSum is useful because dialogue summarization requires identifying speakers, intentions, and important events rather than simply shortening a paragraph.

Key concepts: SAMSum, dialogue summarization, training dataset, dialogue column, summary column

text:
Sample FLAN-T5 prompt templates - SAMSum variants

This slide shows several prompt templates for the same SAMSum summarization task. Each template inserts the dialogue into a slightly different instruction, such as 'Briefly summarize that dialogue', 'Write a short summary', 'What is a summary of this dialogue?', or 'What were the main points in that conversation?'. The target output remains the same summary. The key idea is prompt diversity: if the model sees many ways of asking for the same task, it becomes less dependent on one exact wording. This improves robustness because users may phrase their requests differently at inference time.

Key concepts: prompt templates, SAMSum, summarization instructions, prompt diversity, robustness

metadata.slide_title: Sample FLAN-T5 prompt templates - SAMSum variants

The target output remains the same summary. The key idea is prompt diversity: if the model sees many ways of asking for the same task, it becomes less dependent on one exact wording. This improves robustness because users may phrase their requests differently at inference time.

text:
Sample FLAN-T5 prompt templates - complete template list

This slide continues the SAMSum template examples and shows the prompt-completion pairs in code-like form. Each tuple contains an input template and the target placeholder summary. The slide demonstrates that instruction datasets can be generated systematically from templates rather than manually written one by one. This allows the same dataset example to support multiple natural-language instructions. The template list also illustrates how small wording changes can teach the model that many expressions, such as 'main points' or 'what was going on', all correspond to the summarization task. This is one reason instruction-tuned models generalize better to user phrasing.

Key concepts: template tuples, instruction variation, summary target, template-based dataset generation, prompt robustness

text:
Improving FLAN-T5's summarization capabilities - support chat

This slide begins a practical example involving a support chatbot conversation. The visible dialogue shows ShopBot greeting a customer, the customer saying they need to return jeans, the bot asking when the item was bought, and the customer replying 'Two weeks ago.' This resembles a customer-service transcript. The purpose is to show a domain where summarization is useful: instead of reading the whole conversation, a model should identify the user's need and the action required. The slide sets up the idea that a general FLAN-T5 model may already summarize, but additional domain-specific fine-tuning can make it better for support-dialogue summaries.

Key concepts: support dialogue, ShopBot, return request, conversation summarization, domain-specific fine-tuning

text:
Improving FLAN-T5's summarization capabilities - action-oriented summary

This slide adds the goal of the support-dialogue example: summarize conversations to identify actions to take. This is more specific than generic summarization. A customer-support summary should preserve operational details, such as the product being returned, the purchase time, and what the agent should do next. The conversation itself is short, but it demonstrates why domain-specific summarization matters: in business applications, a good summary is not only fluent; it must help a human or automated system decide the next step. The slide motivates further fine-tuning on a relevant dialogue dataset.

Key concepts: action extraction, support workflow, dialogue summary, customer service, task-specific objective

text:
Improving FLAN-T5's summarization capabilities - DialogSum dataset

This slide shows the idea of further fine-tuning FLAN-T5 with a domain-specific instruction dataset called DialogSum. The screenshot displays a dataset page with examples containing dialogue and summary fields. Compared with using only general instruction tuning, domain-specific fine-tuning exposes the model to the style, structure, and information needs of dialogue summarization. The word 'further' is important: FLAN-T5 is already instruction-tuned, but it can still be specialized for a narrower task. This is a common workflow in applied LLM projects: start from a strong instruct model, then fine-tune on high-quality examples from the target domain.

Key concepts: DialogSum, domain-specific fine-tuning, FLAN-T5, dialogue-summary pairs, specialization

text:
Example support-dialog summarization - prompt

This slide presents a concrete prompt for dialogue summarization. The instruction says 'Summarize the following conversation', followed by a hotel check-in dialogue between Tommy Sandals and Mike. The conversation includes Tommy stating he has a reservation, Mike requesting identification and a credit card, Mike assigning room 507, and the exchange ending politely. This prompt is created from a template, meaning the dialogue is inserted into a fixed instruction format. The expected model behavior is to condense the conversation into the important facts: the reservation, identity check, credit card check, room assignment, and check-in assistance.

Key concepts: dialogue prompt, hotel check-in, template-generated prompt, conversation summarization, important facts

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 42; title: Example support-dialog summarization - prompt; key_concepts: dialogue prompt, hotel check-in, template-generated prompt, conversation summarization, important facts; extracted_text: Example support-dialog summarization Summarize the following conversation. Tommy: Hello. My name is Tommy Sandals, I have a reservation. Mike: May I see some identification, sir, please? Tommy: Sure. Here you go. Mike: Thank you so much. Have you got a credit card, Mr. Sandals? Tommy: I sure do. Mike: Thank you, sir. You'll be in room 507, nonsmoking, queen bed. Tommy: That's great, thank you! Mike: Enjoy your stay! Prompt (created from template) Source: https://huggingface.co/datasets/knkarthick/dialogsum/viewer/knkarthick--dialogsum/ 42

The instruction says 'Summarize the following conversation', followed by a hotel check-in dialogue between Tommy Sandals and Mike. The conversation includes Tommy stating he has a reservation, Mike requesting identification and a credit card, Mike assigning room 507, and the exchange ending politely. This prompt is created from a template, meaning the dialogue is inserted into a fixed instruction format. The expected model behavior is to condense the conversation into the important facts: the reservation, identity check, credit card check, room assignment, and check-in assistance.

text:
Summary before fine-tuning FLAN-T5 with our dataset

This slide compares FLAN-T5's summary before additional fine-tuning with a human baseline. The model summary says Tommy Sandals has a reservation for a room at the Venetian Hotel in Las Vegas. This is adequate because it captures the reservation topic, but it misses some interaction details. The human baseline says Mike asks for identification and credit card and helps Tommy check in, which better reflects the sequence of events. The slide shows why automatic summaries must be evaluated qualitatively as well as numerically: a fluent summary can still omit important task-specific details. Fine-tuning aims to make the model output closer to the human reference.

Key concepts: before fine-tuning, FLAN-T5 summary, human baseline, summary quality, missing details

text:
Summary before fine-tuning - repeated comparison

This slide repeats the before-fine-tuning comparison to reinforce the weakness of the original model output. The prompt contains the same hotel check-in dialogue, the FLAN-T5 summary is acceptable but incomplete, and the human baseline includes more useful details about identification, credit card, and check-in. Repetition here helps students focus on the evaluation criterion: a good domain summary should capture actions and roles, not merely the broad topic. The slide prepares the learner to appreciate the improvement shown after fine-tuning, where the model becomes more aligned with the human style and information priorities.

Key concepts: baseline repetition, summary evaluation, human reference, domain details, fine-tuning motivation

text:
Summary before fine-tuning - adequacy versus alignment

This slide again emphasizes that the original FLAN-T5 summary is not completely wrong, but it does not match the human baseline closely enough. It captures the existence of a hotel reservation but does not mention the check-in process and required documents. This distinction is important in LLM evaluation: outputs can be adequate, partially correct, or fully aligned with the desired reference. Fine-tuning is useful when the model already has the general skill but needs to adapt to a domain-specific notion of what information should be included.

Key concepts: partial correctness, alignment with reference, domain-specific detail, summary adequacy, fine-tuning benefit

This distinction is important in LLM evaluation: outputs can be adequate, partially correct, or fully aligned with the desired reference. Fine-tuning is useful when the model already has the general skill but needs to adapt to a domain-specific notion of what information should be included.

text:
Summary after fine-tuning FLAN-T5 with our dataset

This slide shows the effect of fine-tuning on the dialogue summarization task. After fine-tuning, the model summary says Tommy Sandals has a reservation, checks in by showing his ID and credit card, and Mike helps approve the reservation. This output is closer to the human baseline because it includes the key actions and participants. The slide illustrates the practical value of supervised fine-tuning: the model becomes better at selecting the information the task requires. It also shows that fine-tuning does not necessarily teach a completely new language ability; it can refine an existing ability so the output better matches target examples.

Key concepts: after fine-tuning, improved summary, human baseline match, domain adaptation, supervised improvement

text:
Fine-tuning with your own data

This slide returns to the support-chat example and highlights the idea of using your own data. The visible conversation about returning jeans represents the kind of internal dataset a company or course project might collect. Fine-tuning with your own data means adapting the model to the specific language, workflows, policies, and output expectations of the application domain. For a support bot, this might include summaries that identify return eligibility, missing information, and next actions. The slide prepares the transition from model training to evaluation, because once a model is fine-tuned on custom data, we need objective ways to measure whether it actually improved.

Key concepts: custom data, support bot, domain adaptation, return workflow, fine-tuning dataset

text:
Model evaluation metrics

This section divider introduces the evaluation part of the lecture. After fine-tuning, we need metrics that can quantify whether the model performs better. Unlike ordinary classification, LLM outputs are often free-form text, so evaluation is more complex. A generated summary may use different words from the reference while still being correct, or it may share words with the reference while changing the meaning. The following slides introduce common metrics such as ROUGE and BLEU and explain their strengths and limitations. The slide signals that model development should be evidence-based, not judged only by a few examples.

Key concepts: model evaluation, LLM metrics, ROUGE, BLEU, free-form text evaluation

Unlike ordinary classification, LLM outputs are often free-form text, so evaluation is more complex. A generated summary may use different words from the reference while still being correct, or it may share words with the reference while changing the meaning. The following slides introduce common metrics such as ROUGE and BLEU and explain their strengths and limitations. The slide signals that model development should be evidence-based, not judged only by a few examples.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Challenges: accuracy

This slide begins with the familiar accuracy formula: correct predictions divided by total predictions. Accuracy works well when the model output is a discrete class label, such as Positive or Negative. However, the slide title says evaluation challenges because many LLM tasks do not have one exact answer. For classification, accuracy is straightforward; for summarization, translation, and open-ended generation, deciding whether a prediction is correct is much harder. The slide prepares students to understand why text generation requires metrics that compare overlap, meaning, fluency, and faithfulness rather than only exact-match correctness.

Key concepts: accuracy, correct predictions, total predictions, classification evaluation, generation challenge

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 49; title: LLM Evaluation - Challenges: accuracy; key_concepts: accuracy, correct predictions, total predictions, classification evaluation, generation challenge; extracted_text: LLM Evaluation - Challenges Correct Predictions Accuracy = Total Predictions 49

However, the slide title says evaluation challenges because many LLM tasks do not have one exact answer. For classification, accuracy is straightforward; for summarization, translation, and open-ended generation, deciding whether a prediction is correct is much harder. The slide prepares students to understand why text generation requires metrics that compare overlap, meaning, fluency, and faithfulness rather than only exact-match correctness.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Challenges: semantic equivalence and contradiction

This slide shows two pairs of sentences to demonstrate why exact text matching is insufficient. 'Mike really loves drinking tea' and 'Mike adores sipping tea' are semantically very similar even though many words differ. In contrast, 'Mike does not drink coffee' and 'Mike does drink coffee' are almost identical in words but opposite in meaning because of the word 'not'. This illustrates a core challenge in LLM evaluation: surface overlap does not always equal meaning. Metrics based only on shared n-grams can reward wrong outputs or penalize correct paraphrases. Human judgment or semantic metrics may be needed when meaning matters.

Key concepts: semantic equivalence, contradiction, exact match limitation, paraphrase, negation

In contrast, 'Mike does not drink coffee' and 'Mike does drink coffee' are almost identical in words but opposite in meaning because of the word 'not'. This illustrates a core challenge in LLM evaluation: surface overlap does not always equal meaning. Metrics based only on shared n-grams can reward wrong outputs or penalize correct paraphrases. Human judgment or semantic metrics may be needed when meaning matters.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE and BLEU

This slide introduces two common automatic evaluation metrics. ROUGE is typically used for text summarization and compares a generated summary to one or more reference summaries. BLEU is commonly used for translation and compares generated translations to human translations. Both metrics rely on overlap between generated text and reference text, usually through words or n-grams. The slide helps students distinguish use cases: ROUGE is recall-oriented and summary-focused, while BLEU is precision-oriented and translation-focused. However, both are imperfect because they measure textual overlap more directly than true understanding or factual correctness.

Key concepts: ROUGE, BLEU, summarization, translation, reference comparison

metadata.slide_title: LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE and BLEU

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces two common automatic evaluation metrics. ROUGE is typically used for text summarization and compares a generated summary to one or more reference summaries. BLEU is commonly used for translation and compares generated translations to human translations. Both metrics rely on overlap between generated text and reference text, usually through words or n-grams. The slide helps students distinguish use cases: ROUGE is recall-oriented and summary-focused, while BLEU is precision-oriented and translation-focused. However, both are imperfect because they measure textual overlap more directly than true understanding or factual correctness.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics terminology: n-gram

This slide defines n-grams using the sentence 'The dog lay on the rug as I sipped a cup of tea.' A unigram is a single word, such as 'dog'. A bigram is a sequence of two consecutive words, such as 'the dog' or 'cup of'. The concept generalizes to trigrams, four-grams, and longer sequences. N-grams are fundamental to ROUGE and BLEU because these metrics compare generated outputs and reference texts by counting how many word sequences overlap. Understanding n-grams is necessary before interpreting ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and BLEU scores.

Key concepts: n-gram, unigram, bigram, text overlap, evaluation terminology

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 52; title: LLM Evaluation - Metrics terminology: n-gram; key_concepts: n-gram, unigram, bigram, text overlap, evaluation terminology; extracted_text: LLM Evaluation - Metrics - Terminology n-gram The dog lay on the rug as I sipped a cup of tea. bigram unigram 52
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide defines n-grams using the sentence 'The dog lay on the rug as I sipped a cup of tea.' A unigram is a single word, such as 'dog'. A bigram is a sequence of two consecutive words, such as 'the dog' or 'cup of'. The concept generalizes to trigrams, four-grams, and longer sequences. N-grams are fundamental to ROUGE and BLEU because these metrics compare generated outputs and reference texts by counting how many word sequences overlap. Understanding n-grams is necessary before interpreting ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and BLEU scores.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE-1 example

This slide calculates ROUGE-1 for a generated sentence, 'It is very cold outside', against the reference 'It is cold outside.' ROUGE-1 uses unigram overlap, meaning it counts matching individual words. The generated output contains the extra word 'very', but all four reference words appear in the generated output. Therefore recall is 4/4 = 1.0 because every reference unigram is covered. Precision is 4/5 = 0.8 because four of the five generated unigrams match the reference. The F1 score balances precision and recall and is shown as 0.89. The example demonstrates how ROUGE-1 rewards word-level overlap.

Key concepts: ROUGE-1, unigram overlap, precision, recall, F1 score

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 53; title: LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE-1 example; key_concepts: ROUGE-1, unigram overlap, precision, recall, F1 score; extracted_text: LLM Evaluation - Metrics - ROUGE-1 Generated output: It is very cold outside. Reference (human): It is cold outside. = unigram matches unigrams in reference ROUGE-1 Recall ROUGE-1 Precision: unigram matches unigrams in output = ROUGE-1 F1: precision + recall 2 precision x recall = = 4 4 = 1.0 = 4 5 = 0.8 = 2 0.8 1.8 = 0.89 53
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide calculates ROUGE-1 for a generated sentence, 'It is very cold outside', against the reference 'It is cold outside.' ROUGE-1 uses unigram overlap, meaning it counts matching individual words. The generated output contains the extra word 'very', but all four reference words appear in the generated output. Therefore recall is 4/4 = 1.0 because every reference unigram is covered. Precision is 4/5 = 0.8 because four of the five generated unigrams match the reference. The F1 score balances precision and recall and is shown as 0.89. The example demonstrates how ROUGE-1 rewards word-level overlap.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE-1 limitation with negation

This slide uses 'It is not cold outside' as the generated output and 'It is cold outside' as the reference. The ROUGE-1 calculation can still produce a high overlap score because the words 'It', 'is', 'cold', and 'outside' are shared. However, the generated sentence contains 'not', which reverses the meaning. This is a major limitation of lexical overlap metrics: they may treat a semantically incorrect statement as strong because many words match. The slide teaches that automatic metrics must be interpreted carefully, especially when small words such as 'not' change the meaning completely.

Key concepts: ROUGE limitation, negation, word overlap, semantic error, metric caution

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE-2 setup

This slide introduces ROUGE-2 by showing how sentences are broken into bigrams. The generated sentence 'It is very cold outside' has bigrams such as 'It is', 'is very', 'very cold', and 'cold outside'. The reference 'It is cold outside' has bigrams such as 'It is', 'is cold', and 'cold outside'. ROUGE-2 is stricter than ROUGE-1 because it checks consecutive two-word sequences, not only individual words. This means it captures some local word order. The slide prepares students for the next calculation, where the number of matching bigrams determines precision, recall, and F1.

Key concepts: ROUGE-2, bigram, word order, generated bigrams, reference bigrams

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 55; title: LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE-2 setup; key_concepts: ROUGE-2, bigram, word order, generated bigrams, reference bigrams; extracted_text: LLM Evaluation - Metrics - ROUGE-2 Generated output: It is very cold outside. It is is very very cold cold outside Reference (human): It is cold outside. It is is cold cold outside 55
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces ROUGE-2 by showing how sentences are broken into bigrams. The generated sentence 'It is very cold outside' has bigrams such as 'It is', 'is very', 'very cold', and 'cold outside'. The reference 'It is cold outside' has bigrams such as 'It is', 'is cold', and 'cold outside'. ROUGE-2 is stricter than ROUGE-1 because it checks consecutive two-word sequences, not only individual words. This means it captures some local word order. The slide prepares students for the next calculation, where the number of matching bigrams determines precision, recall, and F1.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE-2 calculation

This slide calculates ROUGE-2 for the same generated and reference sentences. The matching bigrams are 'It is' and 'cold outside'. The reference contains three bigrams, so recall is 2/3 = 0.67. The generated output contains four bigrams, so precision is 2/4 = 0.5. The F1 score combines these values and is shown as about 0.57. This example shows that ROUGE-2 penalizes the inserted word 'very' more than ROUGE-1 because it disrupts the bigram 'is cold'. In general, higher-order ROUGE scores are stricter about local phrase structure.

Key concepts: ROUGE-2 precision, ROUGE-2 recall, bigram matches, F1, local phrase structure

It is is cold cold outside 56
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide calculates ROUGE-2 for the same generated and reference sentences. The matching bigrams are 'It is' and 'cold outside'. The reference contains three bigrams, so recall is 2/3 = 0.67. The generated output contains four bigrams, so precision is 2/4 = 0.5. The F1 score combines these values and is shown as about 0.57. This example shows that ROUGE-2 penalizes the inserted word 'very' more than ROUGE-1 because it disrupts the bigram 'is cold'. In general, higher-order ROUGE scores are stricter about local phrase structure.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE-L and LCS

This slide introduces ROUGE-L, which is based on the longest common subsequence, abbreviated LCS. The generated output is 'It is very cold outside', and the reference is 'It is cold outside.' A subsequence does not need to be contiguous, but the words must appear in the same order. The highlighted common sequence includes 'It is' and 'cold outside', meaning the generated sentence preserves the main ordered structure of the reference while inserting the extra word 'very'. ROUGE-L is useful because it considers sequence order more flexibly than exact n-gram matching.

Key concepts: ROUGE-L, longest common subsequence, LCS, sequence order, summary evaluation

Generated output: It is very cold outside. Longest common subsequence (LCS): It is cold outside 2 57
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces ROUGE-L, which is based on the longest common subsequence, abbreviated LCS. The generated output is 'It is very cold outside', and the reference is 'It is cold outside.' A subsequence does not need to be contiguous, but the words must appear in the same order. The highlighted common sequence includes 'It is' and 'cold outside', meaning the generated sentence preserves the main ordered structure of the reference while inserting the extra word 'very'. ROUGE-L is useful because it considers sequence order more flexibly than exact n-gram matching.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE-L calculation

This slide shows the ROUGE-L precision, recall, and F1 formulas using the length of the longest common subsequence. The LCS between generated and reference text is compared to the number of unigrams in the reference for recall and to the number of unigrams in the generated output for precision. In the displayed calculation, the score is lower than ROUGE-1 because ROUGE-L is sensitive to ordered sequence structure. The exact arithmetic on the slide illustrates the general procedure: find the longest ordered overlap, compute recall and precision, then combine them into F1. This metric is popular for summarization because summaries may preserve meaning with non-identical wording.

Key concepts: ROUGE-L recall, ROUGE-L precision, LCS length, ordered overlap, F1

Reference (human): It is cold outside. LCS(Gen, Ref) unigrams in reference ROUGE-L Recall: ROUGE-L Precision: ROUGE-L F1: LCS(Gen, Ref) unigrams in output precision + recall 2 precision x recall = = = = = = 2 2 4 2 5 0.2 0.9 = 0.44 = 0.4 = 0.5 58
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows the ROUGE-L precision, recall, and F1 formulas using the length of the longest common subsequence. The LCS between generated and reference text is compared to the number of unigrams in the reference for recall and to the number of unigrams in the generated output for precision. In the displayed calculation, the score is lower than ROUGE-1 because ROUGE-L is sensitive to ordered sequence structure. The exact arithmetic on the slide illustrates the general procedure: find the longest ordered overlap, compute recall and precision, then combine them into F1. This metric is popular for summarization because summaries may preserve meaning with non-identical wording.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE-L with definition

This slide repeats the ROUGE-L calculation and explicitly defines LCS as the longest common subsequence. The repetition helps connect the abbreviation to the formula. In practice, ROUGE-L measures how much of the reference appears in the generated text in the correct order, even if some additional words are inserted. This makes it more flexible than exact phrase matching, but it is still based on text overlap rather than deep semantic understanding. The slide reinforces that ROUGE-L can be useful for comparing summaries, but it should not be treated as a complete measure of factual correctness.

Key concepts: LCS definition, ROUGE-L F1, ordered subsequence, summary metric, evaluation limitation

This makes it more flexible than exact phrase matching, but it is still based on text overlap rather than deep semantic understanding. The slide reinforces that ROUGE-L can be useful for comparing summaries, but it should not be treated as a complete measure of factual correctness.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE hacking

This slide shows a failure case called ROUGE hacking. The reference is 'It is cold outside', while the generated output is 'cold cold cold cold'. If a metric only counts word overlap naively, repeating a common reference word could artificially increase the score. This reveals that overlap metrics can be gamed by repetition or by outputting high-frequency reference words. The slide is important because it teaches that evaluation metrics need safeguards and that model outputs should also be inspected for quality, repetition, coherence, and meaning. A high automatic score is not always a good answer.

Key concepts: ROUGE hacking, repetition, metric gaming, word overlap, evaluation robustness

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 60; title: LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE hacking; key_concepts: ROUGE hacking, repetition, metric gaming, word overlap, evaluation robustness; extracted_text: LLM Evaluation - Metrics - ROUGE hacking Reference (human): It is cold outside. Generated output: cold cold cold cold 60
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows a failure case called ROUGE hacking. The reference is 'It is cold outside', while the generated output is 'cold cold cold cold'. If a metric only counts word overlap naively, repeating a common reference word could artificially increase the score. This reveals that overlap metrics can be gamed by repetition or by outputting high-frequency reference words. The slide is important because it teaches that evaluation metrics need safeguards and that model outputs should also be inspected for quality, repetition, coherence, and meaning. A high automatic score is not always a good answer.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE clipping

This slide explains clipping as a correction for ROUGE or BLEU-style overlap counting. The generated output 'cold cold cold cold' repeats one word four times, but the reference contains 'cold' only once. Without clipping, precision may be misleadingly high. Modified precision clips the count of each matched unigram to the maximum count found in the reference, so repeated extra matches are not rewarded. The slide also contrasts this with 'outside cold it is', which contains all reference words but in a different order. Clipping handles repetition, but word order and meaning still require other considerations. This slide shows how evaluation metrics are engineered to reduce obvious loopholes.

Key concepts: clipping, modified precision, repetition penalty, ROUGE, BLEU-style counting

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 61; title: LLM Evaluation - Metrics: ROUGE clipping; key_concepts: clipping, modified precision, repetition penalty, ROUGE, BLEU-style counting; extracted_text: LLM Evaluation - Metrics - ROUGE clipping Reference (human): It is cold outside. Generated output: cold cold cold cold Generated output: outside cold it is unigram matches unigrams in output ROUGE-1 Precision = = 4 4 = 1.0 Modified precision clip(unigram matches) unigrams in output = = 1 4 = 0.25 unigrams in output Modified precision = clip(unigram matches) = 4 4 = 1.0 61
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains clipping as a correction for ROUGE or BLEU-style overlap counting. The generated output 'cold cold cold cold' repeats one word four times, but the reference contains 'cold' only once. Without clipping, precision may be misleadingly high. Modified precision clips the count of each matched unigram to the maximum count found in the reference, so repeated extra matches are not rewarded. The slide also contrasts this with 'outside cold it is', which contains all reference words but in a different order. Clipping handles repetition, but word order and meaning still require other considerations. This slide shows how evaluation metrics are engineered to reduce obvious loopholes.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics recap: ROUGE and BLEU

This slide returns to the high-level comparison between ROUGE and BLEU. ROUGE is mainly used for summarization, where the generated summary is compared with one or more reference summaries. BLEU is mainly used for translation, where generated translations are compared with human translations. Both metrics use n-gram overlap, but they were designed for different tasks and emphasize different aspects of quality. The repetition of this comparison helps students organize the evaluation toolkit: choose a metric based on the task, understand what it measures, and remember what it does not measure, such as factuality, reasoning, or human usefulness.

Key concepts: ROUGE, BLEU, metric selection, summarization evaluation, translation evaluation

BLEU is mainly used for translation, where generated translations are compared with human translations. text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics: BLEU

This slide introduces BLEU as the average precision across a range of n-gram sizes. The reference sentence is 'I am very happy to say that I am drinking a warm cup of tea.' Several generated outputs are compared, and their BLEU scores increase as they become closer to the reference. The perfect match receives BLEU 1.000. Outputs that omit words or alter phrase structure receive lower scores. The example shows that BLEU rewards exact word and phrase overlap with the human translation or reference text. It is useful for translation benchmarking, but like ROUGE, it can penalize valid paraphrases and does not always capture meaning perfectly.

Key concepts: BLEU, n-gram precision, translation metric, reference text, score interpretation

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 63; title: LLM Evaluation - Metrics: BLEU; key_concepts: BLEU, n-gram precision, translation metric, reference text, score interpretation; extracted_text: LLM Evaluation - Metrics - BLEU BLEU metric = Avg(precision across range of n-gram sizes) Reference (human): I am very happy to say that I am drinking a warm cup of tea. Generated output: I am very happy that I am drinking a cup of tea. - BLEU 0.495 I am very happy that I am drinking a warm cup of tea. - BLEU 0.730 I am very happy to say that I am drinking a warm tea. - BLEU 0.798 I am very happy to say that I am drinking a warm cup of tea. - BLEU 1.000 63
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces BLEU as the average precision across a range of n-gram sizes. The reference sentence is 'I am very happy to say that I am drinking a warm cup of tea.' Several generated outputs are compared, and their BLEU scores increase as they become closer to the reference. The perfect match receives BLEU 1.000. Outputs that omit words or alter phrase structure receive lower scores. The example shows that BLEU rewards exact word and phrase overlap with the human translation or reference text. It is useful for translation benchmarking, but like ROUGE, it can penalize valid paraphrases and does not always capture meaning perfectly.

text:
LLM Evaluation - Metrics summary

This slide again summarizes that ROUGE is used mainly for summarization and BLEU mainly for translation. The repeated visual serves as a transition from metric formulas to larger benchmark suites. The important takeaway is that individual metrics are task-specific tools, not universal judgments of intelligence. For fine-tuned LLMs, a good evaluation plan may combine task-specific metrics such as ROUGE or BLEU with human review, classification accuracy, factuality checks, and benchmark results. This is especially important when the model output is open-ended or when errors have practical consequences.

Key concepts: task-specific metrics, automatic evaluation, ROUGE, BLEU, benchmark transition

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide again summarizes that ROUGE is used mainly for summarization and BLEU mainly for translation. The repeated visual serves as a transition from metric formulas to larger benchmark suites. The important takeaway is that individual metrics are task-specific tools, not universal judgments of intelligence. For fine-tuned LLMs, a good evaluation plan may combine task-specific metrics such as ROUGE or BLEU with human review, classification accuracy, factuality checks, and benchmark results. This is especially important when the model output is open-ended or when errors have practical consequences.

text:
Benchmarks

This section divider introduces standardized benchmarks for evaluating language models. Unlike a single task metric such as ROUGE, a benchmark usually contains many datasets or tasks designed to test broader capabilities. Benchmarks help compare models under consistent conditions and track progress over time. The next slides mention GLUE, SuperGLUE, MMLU, BIG-bench, and HELM. Each benchmark has a different focus, from natural language understanding to multitask knowledge and holistic evaluation. The slide signals a move from evaluating one fine-tuned application to assessing general language-model ability.

Key concepts: benchmarks, standardized evaluation, model comparison, LLM capabilities

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 65; title: Benchmarks; key_concepts: benchmarks, standardized evaluation, model comparison, LLM capabilities; extracted_text: Benchmarks 65
metadata.slide_paragraph: This section divider introduces standardized benchmarks for evaluating language models. Each benchmark has a different focus, from natural language understanding to multitask knowledge and holistic evaluation. The slide signals a move from evaluating one fine-tuned application to assessing general language-model ability.

text:
Evaluation benchmarks

This slide displays major benchmark names and logos: GLUE, SuperGLUE, HELM, MMLU, and BIG-bench. GLUE and SuperGLUE focus on natural language understanding tasks. MMLU evaluates broad multitask knowledge across many subjects. BIG-bench and BIG-bench Hard contain challenging tasks intended to test large-model capabilities. HELM takes a more holistic view by measuring not only accuracy but also robustness, fairness, toxicity, and efficiency. The slide teaches that no single benchmark captures everything; different benchmarks reveal different dimensions of model behavior.

Key concepts: GLUE, SuperGLUE, HELM, MMLU, BIG-bench, evaluation suite

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 66; title: Evaluation benchmarks; key_concepts: GLUE, SuperGLUE, HELM, MMLU, BIG-bench, evaluation suite; extracted_text: Evaluation benchmarks 66
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide displays major benchmark names and logos: GLUE, SuperGLUE, HELM, MMLU, and BIG-bench. GLUE and SuperGLUE focus on natural language understanding tasks. MMLU evaluates broad multitask knowledge across many subjects. BIG-bench and BIG-bench Hard contain challenging tasks intended to test large-model capabilities. HELM takes a more holistic view by measuring not only accuracy but also robustness, fairness, toxicity, and efficiency. The slide teaches that no single benchmark captures everything; different benchmarks reveal different dimensions of model behavior.

text:
GLUE

This slide introduces GLUE, the General Language Understanding Evaluation benchmark. The table lists tasks from several categories, including single-sentence tasks, similarity and paraphrase tasks, and inference tasks. Examples include CoLA for grammatical acceptability, SST-2 for sentiment, MRPC and QQP for paraphrase detection, MNLI and QNLI for natural language inference, RTE for textual entailment, and WNLI for coreference-style reasoning. The slide is useful because it shows that language understanding is not one task; it includes classification, sentence-pair reasoning, paraphrase recognition, and entailment. GLUE became a standard way to compare representation models such as BERT.

Key concepts: GLUE, natural language understanding, CoLA, SST-2, MNLI, paraphrase detection

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 67; title: GLUE; key_concepts: GLUE, natural language understanding, CoLA, SST-2, MNLI, paraphrase detection; extracted_text: GLUE The tasks included in SuperGLUE benchmark: Source: Wang et al. 2018, “GLUE: A Multi-Task Benchmark and Analysis Platform for Natural Language Understanding” 67
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces GLUE, the General Language Understanding Evaluation benchmark. The table lists tasks from several categories, including single-sentence tasks, similarity and paraphrase tasks, and inference tasks. Examples include CoLA for grammatical acceptability, SST-2 for sentiment, MRPC and QQP for paraphrase detection, MNLI and QNLI for natural language inference, RTE for textual entailment, and WNLI for coreference-style reasoning. The slide is useful because it shows that language understanding is not one task; it includes classification, sentence-pair reasoning, paraphrase recognition, and entailment. GLUE became a standard way to compare representation models such as BERT.

text:
SuperGLUE

This slide introduces SuperGLUE, a harder benchmark created after models began performing very well on GLUE. The table lists tasks such as BoolQ, CB, COPA, MultiRC, ReCoRD, RTE, WiC, and WSC, with metrics such as accuracy, F1, and exact match. These tasks cover question answering, natural language inference, word sense disambiguation, commonsense reasoning, and coreference. The slide shows that benchmarks evolve as models improve: when earlier tasks become too easy, new benchmarks are designed to be more challenging and better discriminate between systems. SuperGLUE is therefore a stronger test of general-purpose language understanding.

Key concepts: SuperGLUE, BoolQ, COPA, ReCoRD, WiC, harder NLU benchmark

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 68; title: SuperGLUE; key_concepts: SuperGLUE, BoolQ, COPA, ReCoRD, WiC, harder NLU benchmark; extracted_text: SuperGLUE Source: Wang et al. 2019, “SuperGLUE: A Stickier Benchmark for General-Purpose Language Understanding Systems“ The tasks included in SuperGLUE benchmark: 68
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces SuperGLUE, a harder benchmark created after models began performing very well on GLUE. The table lists tasks such as BoolQ, CB, COPA, MultiRC, ReCoRD, RTE, WiC, and WSC, with metrics such as accuracy, F1, and exact match. These tasks cover question answering, natural language inference, word sense disambiguation, commonsense reasoning, and coreference. The slide shows that benchmarks evolve as models improve: when earlier tasks become too easy, new benchmarks are designed to be more challenging and better discriminate between systems. SuperGLUE is therefore a stronger test of general-purpose language understanding.

text:
GLUE and SuperGLUE leaderboards

This slide shows leaderboard pages for GLUE and SuperGLUE. A leaderboard ranks models by benchmark scores, allowing researchers to compare systems on standardized tasks. The disclaimer says metrics may not be up to date and points learners to the official leaderboard websites for the latest results. This is important because model rankings change quickly as new systems are released. The slide also teaches that benchmark numbers should be interpreted in context: a high leaderboard score is useful, but it may not guarantee strong performance on a specific domain task, deployment environment, or user population.

Key concepts: leaderboards, benchmark ranking, model comparison, GLUE website, SuperGLUE website

text:
Benchmarks for massive models - MMLU

This slide introduces MMLU, Massive Multitask Language Understanding, as a benchmark for large models. MMLU evaluates a model across many academic and professional subjects, testing whether it can answer questions that require broad knowledge and reasoning. The slide places MMLU in 2021, indicating its role in the more recent era of evaluating massive models. Unlike GLUE, which focuses heavily on sentence understanding tasks, MMLU is designed to probe breadth of knowledge. It is particularly relevant for LLMs because it measures performance across many domains rather than one narrow task.

Key concepts: MMLU, massive multitask evaluation, knowledge benchmark, large models, 2021

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 70; title: Benchmarks for massive models - MMLU; key_concepts: MMLU, massive multitask evaluation, knowledge benchmark, large models, 2021; extracted_text: Benchmarks for massive models Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) 2021 Source: Hendrycks, 2021. “Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding” 70
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces MMLU, Massive Multitask Language Understanding, as a benchmark for large models. MMLU evaluates a model across many academic and professional subjects, testing whether it can answer questions that require broad knowledge and reasoning. The slide places MMLU in 2021, indicating its role in the more recent era of evaluating massive models. Unlike GLUE, which focuses heavily on sentence understanding tasks, MMLU is designed to probe breadth of knowledge. It is particularly relevant for LLMs because it measures performance across many domains rather than one narrow task.

text:
Benchmarks for massive models - BIG-bench and BIG-bench Hard

This slide expands the benchmark picture by showing MMLU from 2021 and BIG-bench or BIG-bench Hard from 2022. The circles indicate different benchmark families and their relative relationship. BIG-bench contains a large collection of diverse tasks designed to test capabilities that may emerge in large language models. BIG-bench Hard is a subset of especially challenging tasks, and Lite indicates a smaller practical version. The slide highlights that as models scale, evaluation must also scale in diversity and difficulty. Benchmarks for massive models are designed to test reasoning, instruction following, generalization, and robustness beyond traditional NLU datasets.

Key concepts: BIG-bench, BIG-bench Hard, MMLU, massive model evaluation, challenging tasks

text:
Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM)

This slide introduces HELM, a holistic evaluation framework for language models. The metric list includes accuracy, calibration, robustness, fairness, bias, toxicity, and efficiency. The table on the right shows multiple scenarios and models, with check marks indicating which evaluations are performed. The highlighted metrics fairness, bias, and toxicity are especially important because a model can be accurate but still harmful or unfair. HELM therefore goes beyond task performance and asks broader deployment questions: Is the model reliable? Is it robust? Does it behave safely? Is it efficient enough for practical use? This aligns evaluation with real-world responsible AI concerns.

Key concepts: HELM, holistic evaluation, fairness, bias, toxicity, robustness, efficiency

text:
Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM) leaderboard

This slide shows an example HELM results page with core scenarios and accuracy columns for different tasks. The disclaimer again warns that metrics may not be up to date and directs users to the latest HELM site. The slide teaches that holistic evaluation is organized across scenarios, not just one aggregate score. A model may perform well on natural questions but worse on robustness or bias measures. For students, this is an important lesson: choosing a model for an application requires looking at the dimensions that matter for that application, not simply selecting the model with the highest overall benchmark number.

Key concepts: HELM results, core scenarios, accuracy table, model selection, responsible evaluation

text:
Key takeaways

This slide marks a transition into the summary and PEFT part of the lecture. It indicates that the previous concepts - instruction fine-tuning, evaluation metrics, benchmarks, and catastrophic forgetting - are being consolidated before moving to parameter-efficient methods. A key takeaway from the preceding section is that fine-tuning can improve task performance, but it must be evaluated carefully and can be expensive or risky if it updates the whole model. The following slides revisit the fine-tuning process and then explain why PEFT methods are attractive for large LLMs. This slide functions as a pause point for organizing the lecture's main ideas.

Key concepts: key takeaways, fine-tuning summary, evaluation, PEFT transition, catastrophic forgetting

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 74; title: Key takeaways; key_concepts: key takeaways, fine-tuning summary, evaluation, PEFT transition, catastrophic forgetting; extracted_text: Key takeaways 74
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide marks a transition into the summary and PEFT part of the lecture. It indicates that the previous concepts - instruction fine-tuning, evaluation metrics, benchmarks, and catastrophic forgetting - are being consolidated before moving to parameter-efficient methods. A key takeaway from the preceding section is that fine-tuning can improve task performance, but it must be evaluated carefully and can be expensive or risky if it updates the whole model. The following slides revisit the fine-tuning process and then explain why PEFT methods are attractive for large LLMs. This slide functions as a pause point for organizing the lecture's main ideas.

text:
LLM fine-tuning process - key takeaway recap

This slide recaps the fine-tuning process with the prompt, model completion, label, and loss. It shows a training dataset example where the prompt asks for sentiment and the model completion is compared against the label. The loss is shown as Cross, which refers to cross-entropy. The main takeaway is that fine-tuning is a supervised learning loop: provide input, generate output, compare with target, compute loss, and update parameters. The slide prepares the learner for the memory problem of full fine-tuning: if all model weights are trainable, then the training process requires storing weights, gradients, optimizer states, and activations.

Key concepts: fine-tuning recap, prompt, label, cross-entropy, supervised update

metadata.slide_title: LLM fine-tuning process - key takeaway recap

Sentiment: LLM completion: Label: Prompt: LLM fine-tuning Loss: Cross 75
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide recaps the fine-tuning process with the prompt, model completion, label, and loss. It shows a training dataset example where the prompt asks for sentiment and the model completion is compared against the label. The loss is shown as Cross, which refers to cross-entropy. The main takeaway is that fine-tuning is a supervised learning loop: provide input, generate output, compare with target, compute loss, and update parameters. The slide prepares the learner for the memory problem of full fine-tuning: if all model weights are trainable, then the training process requires storing weights, gradients, optimizer states, and activations.

text:
LLM fine-tuning process - updated LLM

This slide shows the same process but emphasizes that the result is an updated LLM. During fine-tuning, each training example contributes to parameter updates through the loss function. After many examples and optimization steps, the model's output probabilities change, making desired completions more likely. The word updated matters because full fine-tuning changes the base model itself. This can be powerful, but it also means that if we want different task-specific versions, we may need to store multiple full model copies unless we use parameter-efficient alternatives.

Key concepts: updated LLM, parameter update, optimization, full fine-tuning, model copy

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 76; title: LLM fine-tuning process - updated LLM; key_concepts: updated LLM, parameter update, optimization, full fine-tuning, model copy; extracted_text: LLM fine-tuning process Updated LLM Model Training dataset Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Sentiment: LLM completion: Label: Prompt: LLM fine-tuning Loss: Cross-Entropy 76
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows the same process but emphasizes that the result is an updated LLM. During fine-tuning, each training example contributes to parameter updates through the loss function. After many examples and optimization steps, the model's output probabilities change, making desired completions more likely. The word updated matters because full fine-tuning changes the base model itself. This can be powerful, but it also means that if we want different task-specific versions, we may need to store multiple full model copies unless we use parameter-efficient alternatives.

text:
LLM fine-tuning process - incorrect completion and correct label

This slide revisits the concrete sentiment example. The model sees the prompt 'Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Sentiment:' and produces Neutral, while the correct label is Positive. This mismatch is exactly what cross-entropy penalizes. Over many examples, the optimizer adjusts the model so that Positive receives higher probability for similar positive reviews. The slide is useful because it connects the abstract training process to one intuitive example. It also reminds students that fine-tuning is not magic: it is ordinary supervised learning applied to LLM outputs formatted as natural-language completions.

Key concepts: incorrect completion, correct label, sentiment prompt, cross-entropy penalty, supervised fine-tuning

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide revisits the concrete sentiment example. The model sees the prompt 'Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Sentiment:' and produces Neutral, while the correct label is Positive. This mismatch is exactly what cross-entropy penalizes. Over many examples, the optimizer adjusts the model so that Positive receives higher probability for similar positive reviews. The slide is useful because it connects the abstract training process to one intuitive example. It also reminds students that fine-tuning is not magic: it is ordinary supervised learning applied to LLM outputs formatted as natural-language completions.

text:
Parameter-efficient Fine-tuning (PEFT)

This section divider introduces PEFT, or parameter-efficient fine-tuning. PEFT methods are designed for large models where full fine-tuning is expensive, memory-intensive, and difficult to store for multiple tasks. Instead of updating every parameter, PEFT updates a small subset of parameters or adds lightweight trainable components. This makes fine-tuning more practical on limited hardware and allows different task adapters to be stored separately. The section will explain why full fine-tuning is challenging, how PEFT reduces memory and storage cost, and how methods such as LoRA and QLoRA work.

Key concepts: PEFT, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, memory efficiency, adapters, LoRA

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 78; title: Parameter-efficient Fine-tuning (PEFT); key_concepts: PEFT, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, memory efficiency, adapters, LoRA; extracted_text: Parameter- efficient Fine-tuning (PEFT) 78
metadata.slide_paragraph: This section divider introduces PEFT, or parameter-efficient fine-tuning. PEFT methods are designed for large models where full fine-tuning is expensive, memory-intensive, and difficult to store for multiple tasks. Instead of updating every parameter, PEFT updates a small subset of parameters or adds lightweight trainable components. This makes fine-tuning more practical on limited hardware and allows different task adapters to be stored separately. The section will explain why full fine-tuning is challenging, how PEFT reduces memory and storage cost, and how methods such as LoRA and QLoRA work.

text:
Full fine-tuning of large LLMs is challenging

This slide explains why full fine-tuning large LLMs is difficult. The diagram shows an LLM and a stack of memory components: trainable weights, optimizer states, gradients, forward activations, and temporary memory. The bracket indicates that training can require 12 to 20 times the memory of the model weights alone. This is because optimization needs more than just the model parameters; it must store intermediate computations for backpropagation and optimizer statistics such as momentum. For large LLMs, this can exceed the memory of a single GPU. The slide motivates PEFT as a way to reduce the number of trainable parameters and therefore reduce training memory.

Key concepts: full fine-tuning, GPU memory, optimizer states, gradients, forward activations, 12-20x memory

metadata.slide_title: Full fine-tuning of large LLMs is challenging
metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 79; title: Full fine-tuning of large LLMs is challenging; key_concepts: full fine-tuning, GPU memory, optimizer states, gradients, forward activations, 12-20x memory; extracted_text: Full fine-tuning of large LLMs is challenging LLM Trainable Weights Optimizer states Gradients Forward Activations Temp memory 12-20x weights 79
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains why full fine-tuning large LLMs is difficult. The diagram shows an LLM and a stack of memory components: trainable weights, optimizer states, gradients, forward activations, and temporary memory. The bracket indicates that training can require 12 to 20 times the memory of the model weights alone. This is because optimization needs more than just the model parameters; it must store intermediate computations for backpropagation and optimizer statistics such as momentum. For large LLMs, this can exceed the memory of a single GPU. The slide motivates PEFT as a way to reduce the number of trainable parameters and therefore reduce training memory.

text:
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) - frozen layers

This slide introduces the basic PEFT idea: most layers of the LLM are frozen, and only a small number of layers or parameters are trainable. The diagram shows the large LLM with a snowflake symbol, indicating frozen weights, and a small trainable region. Freezing means those original weights are not changed during training, which greatly reduces gradient and optimizer memory. The model can still adapt because the trainable components learn task-specific adjustments. This approach is useful when hardware is limited, when many task variants are needed, or when we want to preserve the general knowledge of the base model.

Key concepts: frozen weights, small trainable layers, memory reduction, task adaptation, base model preservation

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 80; title: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) - frozen layers; key_concepts: frozen weights, small trainable layers, memory reduction, task adaptation, base model preservation; extracted_text: Parameter efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) LLM with most layers frozen LLM Small number of trainable layers 80
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the basic PEFT idea: most layers of the LLM are frozen, and only a small number of layers or parameters are trainable. The diagram shows the large LLM with a snowflake symbol, indicating frozen weights, and a small trainable region. Freezing means those original weights are not changed during training, which greatly reduces gradient and optimizer memory. The model can still adapt because the trainable components learn task-specific adjustments. This approach is useful when hardware is limited, when many task variants are needed, or when we want to preserve the general knowledge of the base model.

text:
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) - additional trainable layers

This slide shows another view of PEFT where new trainable layers are added to the LLM while the original model weights stay frozen. The right side separates frozen weights from trainable weights and notes that PEFT is less prone to catastrophic forgetting. This is because the base model's learned knowledge is not overwritten as aggressively as in full fine-tuning. The new trainable modules can specialize the model for a task while leaving the original representation mostly intact. The diagram also implies storage efficiency: for each task, we can store only the small PEFT weights rather than a complete copy of the whole LLM.

Key concepts: additional layers, trainable weights, frozen base model, catastrophic forgetting reduction, adapter storage

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 81; title: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) - additional trainable layers; key_concepts: additional layers, trainable weights, frozen base model, catastrophic forgetting reduction, adapter storage; extracted_text: Parameter efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) Frozen Weights LLM with additional layers for PEFT Other components Trainable weights Less prone to catastrophic forgetting New trainable layers LLM 81
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows another view of PEFT where new trainable layers are added to the LLM while the original model weights stay frozen. The right side separates frozen weights from trainable weights and notes that PEFT is less prone to catastrophic forgetting. This is because the base model's learned knowledge is not overwritten as aggressively as in full fine-tuning. The new trainable modules can specialize the model for a task while leaving the original representation mostly intact. The diagram also implies storage efficiency: for each task, we can store only the small PEFT weights rather than a complete copy of the whole LLM.

text:
Full fine-tuning creates a full copy of the original LLM per task

This slide illustrates the storage problem of full fine-tuning. Starting from one LLM, separate fine-tuning processes for QA, summarization, and generation produce separate full models: QA LLM, Summarize LLM, and Generate LLM. Each full model may require gigabytes of storage. If an organization needs many specialized models, full fine-tuning becomes expensive to store, maintain, and deploy. The slide's key message is that full fine-tuning duplicates the entire model for every task, even though most of the knowledge is shared across tasks. PEFT solves this by reusing one base model and storing small task-specific weights.

Key concepts: model copies, storage cost, task-specific full models, QA fine-tune, summarization fine-tune

metadata.slide_title: Full fine-tuning creates a full copy of the original LLM per task

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide illustrates the storage problem of full fine-tuning. Starting from one LLM, separate fine-tuning processes for QA, summarization, and generation produce separate full models: QA LLM, Summarize LLM, and Generate LLM. Each full model may require gigabytes of storage. If an organization needs many specialized models, full fine-tuning becomes expensive to store, maintain, and deploy. The slide's key message is that full fine-tuning duplicates the entire model for every task, even though most of the knowledge is shared across tasks. PEFT solves this by reusing one base model and storing small task-specific weights.

text:
PEFT fine-tuning saves space and is flexible

This slide contrasts PEFT with full fine-tuning. Instead of storing separate gigabyte-sized full models for QA, summarization, and generation, PEFT stores one large base LLM plus small task-specific PEFT weights measured in megabytes. The diagram shows QA PEFT, Summarize PEFT, and Generate PEFT as lightweight components attached to the same base model. This makes it easier to switch tasks by loading different adapters. It also reduces storage and deployment complexity. The slide highlights why PEFT is especially practical for course projects and real applications where compute and memory are limited.

Key concepts: PEFT weights, adapter switching, storage efficiency, MB-sized adapters, shared base model

metadata.slide_title: PEFT fine-tuning saves space and is flexible

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide contrasts PEFT with full fine-tuning. Instead of storing separate gigabyte-sized full models for QA, summarization, and generation, PEFT stores one large base LLM plus small task-specific PEFT weights measured in megabytes. The diagram shows QA PEFT, Summarize PEFT, and Generate PEFT as lightweight components attached to the same base model. This makes it easier to switch tasks by loading different adapters. It also reduces storage and deployment complexity. The slide highlights why PEFT is especially practical for course projects and real applications where compute and memory are limited.

text:
PEFT trade-offs

This slide lists important trade-offs when choosing a PEFT method: parameter efficiency, memory efficiency, training speed, model performance, and inference costs. Parameter efficiency asks how many trainable parameters are needed. Memory efficiency asks how much GPU RAM is saved. Training speed depends on how much of the model is updated and how much extra computation is introduced. Model performance measures whether PEFT can match full fine-tuning. Inference costs matter because some PEFT methods add extra modules or require merging weights. The slide teaches that PEFT is not one universal answer; the best method depends on hardware, task, dataset size, and performance requirements.

Key concepts: parameter efficiency, memory efficiency, training speed, model performance, inference cost

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 84; title: PEFT trade-offs; key_concepts: parameter efficiency, memory efficiency, training speed, model performance, inference cost; extracted_text: PEFT Trade-offs Parameter Efficiency Memory Efficiency Training Speed Model Performance Inference Costs 84
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide lists important trade-offs when choosing a PEFT method: parameter efficiency, memory efficiency, training speed, model performance, and inference costs. Parameter efficiency asks how many trainable parameters are needed. Memory efficiency asks how much GPU RAM is saved. Training speed depends on how much of the model is updated and how much extra computation is introduced. Model performance measures whether PEFT can match full fine-tuning. Inference costs matter because some PEFT methods add extra modules or require merging weights. The slide teaches that PEFT is not one universal answer; the best method depends on hardware, task, dataset size, and performance requirements.

text:
PEFT methods - selective and reparameterization

This slide categorizes PEFT methods into two major families shown here: selective and reparameterization. Selective methods choose a subset of the original LLM parameters to fine-tune, leaving the rest frozen. Reparameterization methods represent weight updates using a smaller structure, often a low-rank representation, so fewer trainable parameters are needed. The slide cites a guide on PEFT and prepares for the introduction of LoRA, which is a reparameterization method. The important idea is that PEFT can reduce training cost in different ways: either by training fewer existing parameters or by learning compact substitutes for full weight updates.

Key concepts: PEFT methods, selective tuning, reparameterization, low-rank representation, trainable subset
