metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide categorizes PEFT methods into two major families shown here: selective and reparameterization. Selective methods choose a subset of the original LLM parameters to fine-tune, leaving the rest frozen. Reparameterization methods represent weight updates using a smaller structure, often a low-rank representation, so fewer trainable parameters are needed. The slide cites a guide on PEFT and prepares for the introduction of LoRA, which is a reparameterization method. The important idea is that PEFT can reduce training cost in different ways: either by training fewer existing parameters or by learning compact substitutes for full weight updates.

text:
PEFT methods - additive, selective, and reparameterization

This slide expands the PEFT taxonomy. Additive methods add trainable layers or parameters, such as adapters, soft prompts, or prompt tuning. Selective methods fine-tune only selected original parameters. Reparameterization methods, such as LoRA, express weight updates through a low-rank representation. The diagram helps students organize many PEFT names into understandable categories. Additive methods attach new components; selective methods choose parts of the old model; reparameterization methods change how the update is represented. LoRA is highlighted because it becomes the main technical focus of the next section.

Key concepts: additive PEFT, adapters, soft prompts, prompt tuning, LoRA, PEFT taxonomy

metadata.slide_title: PEFT methods - additive, selective, and reparameterization
metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 86; title: PEFT methods - additive, selective, and reparameterization; key_concepts: additive PEFT, adapters, soft prompts, prompt tuning, LoRA, PEFT taxonomy; extracted_text: PEFT methods Add trainable layers or parameters to model Additive Adapters Soft Prompts Prompt Tuning Selective Select subset of initial LLM parameters to fine-tune Reparameterize model weights using a low-rank representation LoRA Reparameterization Source: Lialin et al. 2023, “Scaling Down to Scale Up: A Guide to Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning”, 86
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide expands the PEFT taxonomy. Additive methods add trainable layers or parameters, such as adapters, soft prompts, or prompt tuning. Selective methods fine-tune only selected original parameters. Reparameterization methods, such as LoRA, express weight updates through a low-rank representation. The diagram helps students organize many PEFT names into understandable categories. Additive methods attach new components; selective methods choose parts of the old model; reparameterization methods change how the update is represented. LoRA is highlighted because it becomes the main technical focus of the next section.

text:
Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (LoRA)

This section divider introduces LoRA, Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models. LoRA is a PEFT method that keeps the original model weights frozen and trains small low-rank matrices that represent the update to selected weight matrices. Instead of learning a full large weight update, LoRA learns two smaller matrices whose product approximates that update. This greatly reduces the number of trainable parameters while preserving strong performance. The following slides explain LoRA in the context of Transformer attention layers and show how the low-rank matrices are trained and merged for inference.

Key concepts: LoRA, low-rank adaptation, frozen base weights, rank decomposition, parameter-efficient tuning

metadata.slide_title: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (LoRA)
metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 87; title: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (LoRA); key_concepts: LoRA, low-rank adaptation, frozen base weights, rank decomposition, parameter-efficient tuning; extracted_text: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (LoRA) 87
metadata.slide_paragraph: This section divider introduces LoRA, Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models. LoRA is a PEFT method that keeps the original model weights frozen and trains small low-rank matrices that represent the update to selected weight matrices. Instead of learning a full large weight update, LoRA learns two smaller matrices whose product approximates that update. This greatly reduces the number of trainable parameters while preserving strong performance. The following slides explain LoRA in the context of Transformer attention layers and show how the low-rank matrices are trained and merged for inference.

text:
Transformers recap - encoder-decoder structure

This slide recaps the basic Transformer architecture before introducing LoRA. It shows an encoder and decoder with embeddings at the input side and a softmax output at the end. The example input is French text, 'J'aime l'apprentissage automatique', represented by token IDs such as 2345, 3425, and 3853. The purpose is to remind students that text is first tokenized and embedded, then processed through Transformer blocks, and finally converted into output probabilities. This recap is necessary because LoRA is usually applied inside Transformer weight matrices, especially attention projections, rather than to raw text directly.

Key concepts: Transformer recap, encoder, decoder, embedding, softmax output, token IDs

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 88; title: Transformers recap - encoder-decoder structure; key_concepts: Transformer recap, encoder, decoder, embedding, softmax output, token IDs; extracted_text: Transformers: recap Decoder Encoder Embedding Embedding Softmax output Inputs Output 2345 3425 3853 J'aime l'apprentissage automatique 88
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide recaps the basic Transformer architecture before introducing LoRA. It shows an encoder and decoder with embeddings at the input side and a softmax output at the end. The example input is French text, 'J'aime l'apprentissage automatique', represented by token IDs such as 2345, 3425, and 3853. The purpose is to remind students that text is first tokenized and embedded, then processed through Transformer blocks, and finally converted into output probabilities. This recap is necessary because LoRA is usually applied inside Transformer weight matrices, especially attention projections, rather than to raw text directly.

text:
Transformers recap - attention and feed-forward layers

This slide adds more internal detail to the Transformer recap by showing self-attention and feed-forward networks inside both encoder and decoder. Self-attention lets tokens interact and compute contextual representations. The feed-forward network then transforms each token representation through learned linear layers and nonlinearities. These components contain large weight matrices, which are expensive to fully fine-tune in a big model. The slide prepares students to understand where LoRA can be inserted: rather than changing all Transformer weights, LoRA can target specific matrices within attention or feed-forward layers.

Key concepts: self-attention, feed-forward network, Transformer layers, weight matrices, LoRA insertion point

metadata.slide_title: Transformers recap - attention and feed-forward layers
metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 89; title: Transformers recap - attention and feed-forward layers; key_concepts: self-attention, feed-forward network, Transformer layers, weight matrices, LoRA insertion point; extracted_text: Transformers: recap Encoder Decoder Embedding Embedding Softmax output Inputs Output Feed forward network Feed forward network Self-attention Self-attention 2345 3425 3853 89
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide adds more internal detail to the Transformer recap by showing self-attention and feed-forward networks inside both encoder and decoder. Self-attention lets tokens interact and compute contextual representations. The feed-forward network then transforms each token representation through learned linear layers and nonlinearities. These components contain large weight matrices, which are expensive to fully fine-tune in a big model. The slide prepares students to understand where LoRA can be inserted: rather than changing all Transformer weights, LoRA can target specific matrices within attention or feed-forward layers.

text:
LoRA: Low Rank Adaptation of LLMs - freezing original weights

This slide focuses on a self-attention component where weights are applied to embedding vectors. The first LoRA step is to freeze most of the original LLM weights. In practice, this means the large pre-trained weight matrix stays unchanged during fine-tuning. Freezing is important because it preserves the base model and avoids storing gradients and optimizer states for that full matrix. The task-specific learning will happen through additional low-rank matrices introduced in the next slide. This slide therefore establishes the central PEFT principle: do not retrain the whole model unless necessary.

Key concepts: LoRA step 1, frozen weights, self-attention weights, embedding vectors, base model preservation

metadata.slide_title: LoRA: Low Rank Adaptation of LLMs - freezing original weights
metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 90; title: LoRA: Low Rank Adaptation of LLMs - freezing original weights; key_concepts: LoRA step 1, frozen weights, self-attention weights, embedding vectors, base model preservation; extracted_text: Self-attention Encoder Embedding Weights applied to embedding vectors 1. Freeze most of the original LLM weights. LoRA: Low Rank Adaption of LLMs 90
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide focuses on a self-attention component where weights are applied to embedding vectors. The first LoRA step is to freeze most of the original LLM weights. In practice, this means the large pre-trained weight matrix stays unchanged during fine-tuning. Freezing is important because it preserves the base model and avoids storing gradients and optimizer states for that full matrix. The task-specific learning will happen through additional low-rank matrices introduced in the next slide. This slide therefore establishes the central PEFT principle: do not retrain the whole model unless necessary.

text:
LoRA: injecting rank decomposition matrices

This slide adds steps two and three of LoRA. After freezing most original weights, two rank decomposition matrices are injected. These smaller matrices are trained while the original large matrix stays fixed. The rank r is a small dimension, typically values such as 4, 8, or 64. A smaller rank means fewer trainable parameters and lower memory cost; a larger rank gives the update more capacity but costs more. The plus sign indicates that the learned low-rank update will be added to the original weight transformation. The intuition is that many useful task adaptations can be represented by a low-dimensional update rather than a full matrix.

Key concepts: rank r, low-rank matrices, trainable adapters, LoRA update, parameter reduction

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 91; title: LoRA: injecting rank decomposition matrices; key_concepts: rank r, low-rank matrices, trainable adapters, LoRA update, parameter reduction; extracted_text: Self-attention Encoder Embedding 1. Freeze most of the original LLM weights. 2. Inject 2 rank decomposition matrices 3. Train the weights of the smaller matrices LoRA: Low Rank Adaption of LLMs + Rank r is small dimension, typically 4, 8 … 64 91
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide adds steps two and three of LoRA. After freezing most original weights, two rank decomposition matrices are injected. These smaller matrices are trained while the original large matrix stays fixed. The rank r is a small dimension, typically values such as 4, 8, or 64. A smaller rank means fewer trainable parameters and lower memory cost; a larger rank gives the update more capacity but costs more. The plus sign indicates that the learned low-rank update will be added to the original weight transformation. The intuition is that many useful task adaptations can be represented by a low-dimensional update rather than a full matrix.

text:
LoRA: updating the model for inference

This slide explains how LoRA is used after training. The two low-rank matrices, often called A and B, are multiplied to form the update B x A. This product has the same shape as the original weight matrix, even though A and B individually are much smaller. The product is then added to the original frozen weights before or during inference. Conceptually, the model uses updated weights equal to the base weights plus the LoRA update. This allows the model to behave as if it had been fine-tuned for the task, while only the small LoRA matrices needed to be trained and stored.

Key concepts: B x A, matrix multiplication, weight update, inference merge, LoRA adapters

metadata.slide_title: LoRA: updating the model for inference
metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 92; title: LoRA: updating the model for inference; key_concepts: B x A, matrix multiplication, weight update, inference merge, LoRA adapters; extracted_text: Self-attention Encoder Embedding 1. Freeze most of the original LLM weights. 2. Inject 2 rank decomposition matrices 3. Train the weights of the smaller matrices LoRA: Low Rank Adaption of LLMs + Steps to update model for inference 1. Matrix multiply the low rank matrices B x A + 2. Add to original weights B x A = * B A 92
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains how LoRA is used after training. The two low-rank matrices, often called A and B, are multiplied to form the update B x A. This product has the same shape as the original weight matrix, even though A and B individually are much smaller. The product is then added to the original frozen weights before or during inference. Conceptually, the model uses updated weights equal to the base weights plus the LoRA update. This allows the model to behave as if it had been fine-tuned for the task, while only the small LoRA matrices needed to be trained and stored.

text:
LoRA: updated weights

This slide continues the LoRA inference procedure and explicitly shows the updated weights. The low-rank product B x A is added to the original weights to create a task-adapted version of the transformation. Because the original weights remain available, different LoRA adapters can be applied for different tasks without retraining or duplicating the whole model. The key mathematical idea is additive adaptation: new behavior comes from adding a learned low-rank correction to a frozen pre-trained matrix. This makes LoRA both storage-efficient and operationally flexible.

Key concepts: updated weights, additive adaptation, task-specific correction, adapter merge, frozen pre-trained matrix

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 93; title: LoRA: updated weights; key_concepts: updated weights, additive adaptation, task-specific correction, adapter merge, frozen pre-trained matrix; extracted_text: Self-attention Encoder Embedding 1. Freeze most of the original LLM weights. 2. Inject 2 rank decomposition matrices 3. Train the weights of the smaller matrices LoRA: Low Rank Adaption of LLMs B x A + Steps to update model for inference: 1. Matrix multiply the low rank matrices 2. Add to original weights B x A + Updated weights B x A = * B A 93
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide continues the LoRA inference procedure and explicitly shows the updated weights. The low-rank product B x A is added to the original weights to create a task-adapted version of the transformation. Because the original weights remain available, different LoRA adapters can be applied for different tasks without retraining or duplicating the whole model. The key mathematical idea is additive adaptation: new behavior comes from adding a learned low-rank correction to a frozen pre-trained matrix. This makes LoRA both storage-efficient and operationally flexible.

text:
Concrete example using base Transformer as reference

This slide gives a numerical example of LoRA parameter savings using the base Transformer dimensions from Vaswani et al. The original Transformer weight matrix has dimensions d x k = 512 x 64, giving 32,768 trainable parameters if fully fine-tuned. With LoRA rank r = 8, matrix A has dimensions 8 x 64, which is 512 parameters, and matrix B has dimensions 512 x 8, which is 4,096 parameters. Together, LoRA trains 4,608 parameters instead of 32,768. The slide states this is an 86% reduction in trainable parameters. The example makes the memory benefit concrete: low rank drastically reduces what must be learned.

Key concepts: LoRA parameter count, rank 8, matrix A, matrix B, 86% reduction, Transformer dimensions

metadata.slide_title: Concrete example using base Transformer as reference
metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 94; title: Concrete example using base Transformer as reference; key_concepts: LoRA parameter count, rank 8, matrix A, matrix B, 86% reduction, Transformer dimensions; extracted_text: Concrete example using base Transformer as reference Use the base Transformer model presented by Vaswani et al. 2017: ● Transformer weights have dimensions d x k = 512 x 64 ● So 512 x 64 = 32,768 trainable parameters In LoRA with rank r = 8: ● A has dimensions r x k = 8 x 64 = 512 parameters ● B has dimension d x r = 512 x 8 = 4,096 trainable parameters ● 86% reduction in parameters to train! 94
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide gives a numerical example of LoRA parameter savings using the base Transformer dimensions from Vaswani et al. The original Transformer weight matrix has dimensions d x k = 512 x 64, giving 32,768 trainable parameters if fully fine-tuned. With LoRA rank r = 8, matrix A has dimensions 8 x 64, which is 512 parameters, and matrix B has dimensions 512 x 8, which is 4,096 parameters. Together, LoRA trains 4,608 parameters instead of 32,768. The slide states this is an 86% reduction in trainable parameters. The example makes the memory benefit concrete: low rank drastically reduces what must be learned.

text:
LoRA for different tasks - task A

This slide shows how LoRA supports different task-specific adaptations. The base self-attention weights are shared, while different low-rank decomposition matrices can be trained for different tasks. For Task A, the LoRA matrices are multiplied and added to the frozen base weights before inference. This produces updated weights specialized for Task A. The advantage is that the same LLM can serve many tasks by swapping small adapters. Instead of storing a full model for every task, we store a compact LoRA update for each task and apply it when needed.

Key concepts: Task A adapter, multiple LoRA adapters, shared base model, task-specific weights, adapter swapping

metadata.slide_title: LoRA for different tasks - task A
metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 95; title: LoRA for different tasks - task A; key_concepts: Task A adapter, multiple LoRA adapters, shared base model, task-specific weights, adapter swapping; extracted_text: Self-attention Encoder Embedding Weights applied to embedding vectors LoRA: Low Rank Adaption of LLMs 1. Train different rank decomposition matrices for different tasks 2. Update weights before inference * = Task A + 95
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows how LoRA supports different task-specific adaptations. The base self-attention weights are shared, while different low-rank decomposition matrices can be trained for different tasks. For Task A, the LoRA matrices are multiplied and added to the frozen base weights before inference. This produces updated weights specialized for Task A. The advantage is that the same LLM can serve many tasks by swapping small adapters. Instead of storing a full model for every task, we store a compact LoRA update for each task and apply it when needed.

text:
LoRA for different tasks - task A and task B

This slide extends the previous idea by showing separate LoRA updates for Task A and Task B. Each task has its own low-rank matrices, and each produces a different updated weight matrix when added to the same base model. This illustrates the flexibility of LoRA in multi-application settings. For example, one adapter might specialize the model for summarization, while another specializes it for question answering. The base model remains unchanged; only the selected adapter changes. This design is useful for deployment because adapters are small, modular, and easier to manage than full fine-tuned models.

Key concepts: Task B adapter, adapter modularity, multi-task deployment, base model reuse, LoRA flexibility

metadata.slide_title: LoRA for different tasks - task A and task B

Train different rank decomposition matrices for different tasks 2. Update weights before inference = Task A = Task B + + * * 96
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide extends the previous idea by showing separate LoRA updates for Task A and Task B. Each task has its own low-rank matrices, and each produces a different updated weight matrix when added to the same base model. This illustrates the flexibility of LoRA in multi-application settings. For example, one adapter might specialize the model for summarization, while another specializes it for question answering. The base model remains unchanged; only the selected adapter changes. This design is useful for deployment because adapters are small, modular, and easier to manage than full fine-tuned models.

text:
LoRA for different tasks - updated weights for task B

This slide highlights the updated weights for Task B. The visual shows that Task B has its own low-rank update, separate from Task A. At inference time, applying the Task B update changes the model's behavior toward the Task B objective. The important concept is task isolation: different adapters can encode different behaviors without overwriting each other or the base model. This reduces catastrophic forgetting and makes experimentation easier because a poor adapter can be removed or replaced without damaging the original LLM.

Key concepts: updated weights for task B, task isolation, adapter replacement, catastrophic forgetting reduction, LoRA deployment

Train different rank decomposition matrices for different tasks 2. Update weights before inference = Task A = Task B + + * * 97
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide highlights the updated weights for Task B. The visual shows that Task B has its own low-rank update, separate from Task A. At inference time, applying the Task B update changes the model's behavior toward the Task B objective. The important concept is task isolation: different adapters can encode different behaviors without overwriting each other or the base model. This reduces catastrophic forgetting and makes experimentation easier because a poor adapter can be removed or replaced without damaging the original LLM.

text:
Sample ROUGE metrics for full vs. LoRA fine-tuning - baseline

This slide begins a quantitative comparison for FLAN-T5 dialogue summarization. The base model ROUGE scores are shown for flan_t5_base, including ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, and ROUGE-Lsum. These scores represent the model before instruction fine-tuning or LoRA adaptation for the dialogue summarization task. Establishing a baseline is essential: without the base score, we cannot know whether fine-tuning improved performance. The slide also links the LoRA discussion back to evaluation metrics introduced earlier, showing that PEFT methods should be judged empirically rather than assumed to work.

Key concepts: ROUGE baseline, FLAN-T5, dialogue summarization, LoRA evaluation, base model score

metadata.slide_title: Sample ROUGE metrics for full vs. LoRA fine-tuning - baseline

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide begins a quantitative comparison for FLAN-T5 dialogue summarization. The base model ROUGE scores are shown for flan_t5_base, including ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, and ROUGE-Lsum. These scores represent the model before instruction fine-tuning or LoRA adaptation for the dialogue summarization task. Establishing a baseline is essential: without the base score, we cannot know whether fine-tuning improved performance. The slide also links the LoRA discussion back to evaluation metrics introduced earlier, showing that PEFT methods should be judged empirically rather than assumed to work.

text:
Sample ROUGE metrics for full vs. LoRA fine-tuning - comparison

This slide compares three sets of ROUGE scores: the base FLAN-T5 model, a LoRA fine-tuned model, and a fully fine-tuned model. The base ROUGE-1 is around 0.2334, while the LoRA model improves to around 0.4081, and the full fine-tune reaches around 0.4216. Similar improvements appear for ROUGE-2 and ROUGE-L. The slide notes that LoRA achieves a large gain over the baseline and is only slightly below full fine-tuning, with an indicated small difference of about -3.20% relative to full fine-tuning. The key message is practical: LoRA can recover most of the performance benefit while training far fewer parameters.

Key concepts: LoRA ROUGE, full fine-tuning, baseline comparison, performance gap, parameter efficiency

The slide notes that LoRA achieves a large gain over the baseline and is only slightly below full fine-tuning, with an indicated small difference of about -3.20% relative to full fine-tuning. text:
Choosing the LoRA rank

This slide discusses the choice of LoRA rank r. The table compares ranks such as 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, and higher values against validation loss and metrics like BLEU, NIST, METEOR, ROUGE-L, and CIDEr. The highlighted results suggest that performance improves up to a point and then plateaus, meaning very high ranks may not provide much additional benefit. The slide also notes that the relationship between rank and dataset size needs more empirical data. In practice, rank is a hyperparameter: small ranks are cheaper and often sufficient, while larger ranks may help more complex tasks but cost more memory and training time.

Key concepts: LoRA rank, rank plateau, hyperparameter tuning, validation loss, dataset size relationship

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 100; title: Choosing the LoRA rank; key_concepts: LoRA rank, rank plateau, hyperparameter tuning, validation loss, dataset size relationship; extracted_text: Choosing the LoRA rank ● Effectiveness of higher rank appears to plateau ● Relationship between rank and dataset size needs more empirical data Source: Hu et al. 2021, “LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models” 100
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide discusses the choice of LoRA rank r. The table compares ranks such as 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, and higher values against validation loss and metrics like BLEU, NIST, METEOR, ROUGE-L, and CIDEr. The highlighted results suggest that performance improves up to a point and then plateaus, meaning very high ranks may not provide much additional benefit. The slide also notes that the relationship between rank and dataset size needs more empirical data. In practice, rank is a hyperparameter: small ranks are cheaper and often sufficient, while larger ranks may help more complex tasks but cost more memory and training time.

text:
QLoRA: Quantized LoRA

This slide introduces QLoRA, Quantized LoRA, as a further memory-saving method. It uses 4-bit NormalFloat, or nf4, for quantizing the base model. It also supports double quantization, which reduces memory by about 0.4 bits per parameter, corresponding to roughly 3 GB for a 65B model. Unified GPU-CPU memory management helps reduce GPU memory pressure, and LoRA adapters can be applied at every layer, not only attention layers. The goal is to minimize accuracy trade-off while enabling fine-tuning of very large models on much smaller hardware than full fine-tuning would require.

Key concepts: QLoRA, 4-bit NormalFloat, nf4, double quantization, GPU-CPU memory, large-model fine-tuning

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 101; title: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA; key_concepts: QLoRA, 4-bit NormalFloat, nf4, double quantization, GPU-CPU memory, large-model fine-tuning; extracted_text: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA Source: Dettmers et al. 2023, “QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs” ● Introduces 4-bit NormalFloat (nf4) data type for 4-bit quantization ● Supports double-quantization to reduce memory ~0.4 bits per parameter (~3 GB for a 65B model) ● Unified GPU-CPU memory management reduces GPU memory usage ● LoRA adapters at every layer - not just attention layers ● Minimizes accuracy trade-off 101
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces QLoRA, Quantized LoRA, as a further memory-saving method. It uses 4-bit NormalFloat, or nf4, for quantizing the base model. It also supports double quantization, which reduces memory by about 0.4 bits per parameter, corresponding to roughly 3 GB for a 65B model. Unified GPU-CPU memory management helps reduce GPU memory pressure, and LoRA adapters can be applied at every layer, not only attention layers. The goal is to minimize accuracy trade-off while enabling fine-tuning of very large models on much smaller hardware than full fine-tuning would require.

text:
QLoRA: Quantized LoRA - memory diagram

This slide visually compares full fine-tuning, LoRA, and QLoRA. Full fine-tuning uses a 16-bit Transformer and large 32-bit optimizer states because all parameters are updated. LoRA keeps the 16-bit base model and trains smaller adapter matrices. QLoRA goes further by quantizing the base Transformer model to 4-bit precision and using paged optimizers to handle memory spikes, while still training adapters. The arrows in the diagram distinguish parameter updates, gradient flow, and paging flow. The main lesson is that QLoRA reduces memory more aggressively than LoRA by combining adapter training with low-bit quantization of the frozen base model.

Key concepts: full fine-tuning, LoRA, QLoRA, 4-bit Transformer, paged optimizer, memory comparison

metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 102; title: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA - memory diagram; key_concepts: full fine-tuning, LoRA, QLoRA, 4-bit Transformer, paged optimizer, memory comparison; extracted_text: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA Source: Dettmers et al. 2023, “QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs” 102
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide visually compares full fine-tuning, LoRA, and QLoRA. Full fine-tuning uses a 16-bit Transformer and large 32-bit optimizer states because all parameters are updated. LoRA keeps the 16-bit base model and trains smaller adapter matrices. QLoRA goes further by quantizing the base Transformer model to 4-bit precision and using paged optimizers to handle memory spikes, while still training adapters. The arrows in the diagram distinguish parameter updates, gradient flow, and paging flow. The main lesson is that QLoRA reduces memory more aggressively than LoRA by combining adapter training with low-bit quantization of the frozen base model.

text:
QLoRA: Quantized LoRA - method comparison table

This slide summarizes full fine-tuning, LoRA, and QLoRA in a table. Full fine-tuning uses a 16-bit base model and trains all parameters, requiring very high GPU memory but offering maximum flexibility. LoRA uses a 16-bit base model and trains only small adapter matrices, giving moderate memory usage and faster training. QLoRA uses a 4-bit quantized base model and trains only adapters, giving very low GPU memory usage. The table emphasizes QLoRA's key advantage: it can enable fine-tuning huge models on one GPU by using quantization and paged optimizers to handle memory spikes. This makes QLoRA highly practical for constrained environments.

Key concepts: method comparison, trainable parameters, GPU memory, full finetuning, LoRA, QLoRA

metadata.slide_title: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA - method comparison table
metadata.index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 103; title: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA - method comparison table; key_concepts: method comparison, trainable parameters, GPU memory, full finetuning, LoRA, QLoRA; extracted_text: 103 Method Base Model Trainable Parameters Adapters Used GPU Memory Usage Key Advantage Full Finetuning 16-bit All parameters (entire model) None Very High Maximum accuracy, full flexibility LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) 16-bit Only small adapter matrices Yes Moderate Much lower memory use; faster training QLoRA (Quantized LoRA) 4-bit (quantized) Only adapters Yes Very Low Enables fine-tuning huge models on one GPU; uses paged optimizer to handle memory spikes QLoRA: Quantized LoRA
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes full fine-tuning, LoRA, and QLoRA in a table. Full fine-tuning uses a 16-bit base model and trains all parameters, requiring very high GPU memory but offering maximum flexibility. LoRA uses a 16-bit base model and trains only small adapter matrices, giving moderate memory usage and faster training. QLoRA uses a 4-bit quantized base model and trains only adapters, giving very low GPU memory usage. The table emphasizes QLoRA's key advantage: it can enable fine-tuning huge models on one GPU by using quantization and paged optimizers to handle memory spikes. This makes QLoRA highly practical for constrained environments.

text:
PEFT methods summary

This final content slide summarizes the PEFT taxonomy. Additive methods add trainable layers or parameters, including adapters, soft prompts, and prompt tuning. Selective methods fine-tune only a subset of the original LLM parameters. Reparameterization methods represent updates compactly, and LoRA is the key example shown. The slide ties together the lecture's PEFT section: parameter-efficient methods reduce memory and storage requirements, make task-specific adaptation more flexible, and can reduce catastrophic forgetting compared with full fine-tuning. Students should leave this slide understanding that PEFT is a family of methods, not only LoRA, and that the method choice depends on the task and constraints.

Key concepts: PEFT summary, additive methods, selective methods, reparameterization, LoRA, prompt tuning

text:
Lecture References

This closing slide lists the main reference for the lecture: the Generative AI with Large Language Models course from DeepLearning.AI, Week 2. The reference indicates that the lecture material is based on established educational content about fine-tuning, instruction tuning, evaluation, and PEFT. For a student, this slide is useful because it points to a source for deeper study after the lecture. It also signals that the lecture has covered a complete pipeline: starting from in-context learning limitations, moving to supervised instruction fine-tuning, evaluating generated outputs, and ending with efficient adaptation methods such as LoRA and QLoRA.

Key concepts: lecture reference, DeepLearning.AI, Generative AI with LLMs, fine-tuning, PEFT

text:
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)

This opening slide introduces the lecture topic: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, abbreviated as RLHF. The title signals that the lecture will focus on a training and alignment method used after a language model has already learned general language abilities. The visual of a human head connected to computational components reinforces the central idea that AI systems are not only built from data and algorithms, but can also be shaped by human judgment. In the context of large language models, RLHF is used to move a model from simply predicting likely text toward producing responses that humans judge as useful, safe, honest, and appropriate. The slide is therefore a transition from earlier topics such as pre-training, instruction tuning, and fine-tuning into the alignment stage, where the model's behavior is optimized according to feedback rather than only next-token likelihood.

Key concepts: RLHF, human feedback, model alignment, large language models, post-training

metadata.index_text: Slide 1: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). Dr. Tamer Arafa. Introductory slide about aligning LLM behavior using human feedback.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This opening slide introduces the lecture topic: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, abbreviated as RLHF. The title signals that the lecture will focus on a training and alignment method used after a language model has already learned general language abilities. The visual of a human head connected to computational components reinforces the central idea that AI systems are not only built from data and algorithms, but can also be shaped by human judgment. In the context of large language models, RLHF is used to move a model from simply predicting likely text toward producing responses that humans judge as useful, safe, honest, and appropriate. The slide is therefore a transition from earlier topics such as pre-training, instruction tuning, and fine-tuning into the alignment stage, where the model's behavior is optimized according to feedback rather than only next-token likelihood.

text:
Generative AI Project Lifecycle

This slide places RLHF inside the broader Generative AI project lifecycle. The lifecycle begins with defining the problem, then choosing a model, and then adapting and aligning the model through prompt engineering, fine-tuning, human-feedback alignment, and evaluation. After alignment, the process continues to application integration, where the model is optimized and deployed for inference, and finally augmented or embedded into LLM-powered applications. The key message is that RLHF is not the first step in building a generative AI system; it appears after selecting a model and after earlier adaptation techniques such as prompting and fine-tuning. This matters because human-feedback alignment assumes that the model already has useful language capability, but still needs behavioral correction so that its responses better match human expectations and safety requirements.

Key concepts: Generative AI lifecycle, model selection, fine-tuning, human feedback alignment, deployment

metadata.index_text: Slide 2: Generative AI project lifecycle with stages: define problem, choose model, prompt engineering, fine-tuning, align with human feedback, evaluate, optimize/deploy, augment applications.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide places RLHF inside the broader Generative AI project lifecycle. The lifecycle begins with defining the problem, then choosing a model, and then adapting and aligning the model through prompt engineering, fine-tuning, human-feedback alignment, and evaluation. After alignment, the process continues to application integration, where the model is optimized and deployed for inference, and finally augmented or embedded into LLM-powered applications. The key message is that RLHF is not the first step in building a generative AI system; it appears after selecting a model and after earlier adaptation techniques such as prompting and fine-tuning. This matters because human-feedback alignment assumes that the model already has useful language capability, but still needs behavioral correction so that its responses better match human expectations and safety requirements.

text:
Generative AI Project Lifecycle: Goals of Fine-Tuning

This slide revisits the Generative AI project lifecycle and highlights the goals of fine-tuning. Fine-tuning aims to make a model better at understanding prompts, completing tasks, and producing more natural-sounding language. The lifecycle diagram still shows the major stages: define the problem, choose a model, adapt and align the model, evaluate it, then integrate it into an application. The additional bullet list clarifies why fine-tuning is useful: a base model may have broad general knowledge, but it may not reliably follow a specific instruction style or produce outputs in the desired format. Fine-tuning helps specialize the model's behavior, but the slide prepares the student to see that fine-tuning alone is not always enough. A model can become more fluent and task-capable while still producing unsafe, dishonest, or harmful responses, which motivates the later need for RLHF.

Key concepts: fine-tuning goals, prompt understanding, task completion, natural language generation, model adaptation

metadata.slide_title: Generative AI Project Lifecycle: Goals of Fine-Tuning

The additional bullet list clarifies why fine-tuning is useful: a base model may have broad general knowledge, but it may not reliably follow a specific instruction style or produce outputs in the desired format. Fine-tuning helps specialize the model's behavior, but the slide prepares the student to see that fine-tuning alone is not always enough. A model can become more fluent and task-capable while still producing unsafe, dishonest, or harmful responses, which motivates the later need for RLHF.

text:
Models Behaving Badly

This slide introduces the motivation for alignment by showing that language models can behave in problematic ways. The listed examples are toxic language, aggressive responses, and providing dangerous information. These are not merely technical errors like grammar mistakes; they are behavioral failures that can harm users or create unsafe applications. A model trained only to predict likely text may imitate harmful content from its training data or answer dangerous questions directly if such patterns exist in the data. The slide therefore frames alignment as a safety and quality problem: we do not only want a model that can generate fluent text, but one that follows norms of helpfulness and harm reduction. RLHF is presented later as a method for pushing the model away from these bad behaviors using feedback about what humans prefer.

Key concepts: unsafe model behavior, toxicity, aggressive responses, dangerous information, alignment motivation

metadata.index_text: Slide 4: Models behaving badly: toxic language, aggressive responses, and providing dangerous information.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the motivation for alignment by showing that language models can behave in problematic ways. These are not merely technical errors like grammar mistakes; they are behavioral failures that can harm users or create unsafe applications. A model trained only to predict likely text may imitate harmful content from its training data or answer dangerous questions directly if such patterns exist in the data. The slide therefore frames alignment as a safety and quality problem: we do not only want a model that can generate fluent text, but one that follows norms of helpfulness and harm reduction. RLHF is presented later as a method for pushing the model away from these bad behaviors using feedback about what humans prefer.

text:
Models Behaving Badly: Helpfulness, Honesty, and Harmlessness

This slide gives concrete examples of bad model behavior and introduces the HHH criteria: helpful, honest, and harmless. In the first example, the prompt 'Knock, knock' receives the odd completion 'Clap, clap,' which is neither useful nor contextually appropriate. In the second example, the model incorrectly says coughing can help stop a heart attack; this illustrates a dangerous failure of honesty because the answer presents harmful medical misinformation as if it were true. In the third example, the model responds to a request about hacking a neighbor's Wi-Fi by offering instructions, which violates harmlessness because it assists wrongdoing. The slide shows that alignment is multi-dimensional: a model should be helpful when the request is safe, honest when facts matter, and harmless when the prompt asks for dangerous or unethical assistance. RLHF is introduced as a way to train models toward these human-valued behaviors.

Key concepts: HHH criteria, helpfulness, honesty, harmlessness, harmful completions, unsafe assistance

text:
Generative AI Project Lifecycle: Align with Human Feedback

This slide again shows the Generative AI project lifecycle, emphasizing the position of 'Align with human feedback' in the adaptation and alignment stage. The repeated lifecycle diagram helps students understand that RLHF belongs after prompt engineering and fine-tuning but before final deployment and application integration. This placement is important because alignment is part of preparing a model for real use: the model has already been selected and adapted, but it still needs to be evaluated and refined according to human preferences. The slide functions as a roadmap, reminding students that RLHF is one tool inside a larger engineering pipeline, not a standalone replacement for data preparation, fine-tuning, evaluation, or deployment optimization.

Key concepts: human feedback alignment, project lifecycle, adaptation, evaluation, deployment readiness

text:
Fine-Tuning with Human Feedback

This slide uses a chart from Stiennon et al. 2020 to show why human-feedback fine-tuning is valuable. The y-axis represents the fraction of model-generated results preferred over human responses, while the x-axis represents model size in number of parameters. Three lines are compared: no fine-tuning, initial fine-tuning, and fine-tuning with human feedback. The human-feedback line is highest across the model sizes shown, indicating that models trained using human preferences were preferred more often than models trained without such feedback or with only ordinary fine-tuning. The dashed line at 0.5 represents reference summaries; values above or below this threshold indicate whether model outputs are preferred more or less often than human reference responses in pairwise comparisons. The main lesson is that simply scaling model size is not the only way to improve quality. Human feedback can directly optimize the model toward what people actually prefer, especially for subjective generation tasks like summarization.

Key concepts: human feedback fine-tuning, preference evaluation, model size, summarization quality, Stiennon et al. 2020

metadata.index_text: Slide 7: Chart comparing no fine-tuning, initial fine-tuning, and fine-tuning with human feedback. Human-feedback models are preferred more often over reference summaries.

text:
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)

This slide defines the high-level role of RLHF. An instruction fine-tuned LLM is passed through a process called Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, producing a human-aligned LLM. The bullets describe the desired behavioral direction: maximize helpfulness and relevance, minimize harm, and avoid dangerous topics. This means the model is not being optimized only for matching a dataset of correct completions; it is being optimized according to a reward signal that represents human preferences. The slide also implies that RLHF is typically applied after instruction fine-tuning, because the model first needs the ability to follow instructions before its choices among possible responses can be refined for alignment. The final target is a model that does not merely answer, but answers in a way that is safer and more useful to people.

Key concepts: RLHF, instruction-tuned LLM, human-aligned LLM, helpfulness, harm minimization, safe responses

metadata.index_text: Slide 8: RLHF transforms an instruct fine-tuned LLM into a human-aligned LLM by maximizing helpfulness/relevance and minimizing harm/dangerous topics.

This means the model is not being optimized only for matching a dataset of correct completions; it is being optimized according to a reward signal that represents human preferences. The slide also implies that RLHF is typically applied after instruction fine-tuning, because the model first needs the ability to follow instructions before its choices among possible responses can be refined for alignment. The final target is a model that does not merely answer, but answers in a way that is safer and more useful to people.

text:
Reinforcement Learning (RL)

This slide introduces the basic reinforcement learning framework. The diagram contains an agent and an environment, with the objective stated as maximizing reward received for actions. In reinforcement learning, the agent is the decision-maker: it observes a situation, chooses an action, and receives feedback in the form of reward. The environment is everything the agent interacts with; it responds to the agent's action and determines the next state and reward. The important intuition is that the agent learns behavior not from fixed labels alone, but from trial-and-error feedback about which actions lead to better outcomes. This framework becomes relevant to language models because text generation can be treated as a sequence of actions, where each generated token is an action and the final response can be rewarded according to human preference.

Key concepts: reinforcement learning, agent, environment, reward, action

metadata.index_text: Slide 9: Reinforcement learning framework: agent interacts with environment to maximize reward received for actions.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the basic reinforcement learning framework. The diagram contains an agent and an environment, with the objective stated as maximizing reward received for actions. In reinforcement learning, the agent is the decision-maker: it observes a situation, chooses an action, and receives feedback in the form of reward. The environment is everything the agent interacts with; it responds to the agent's action and determines the next state and reward. The important intuition is that the agent learns behavior not from fixed labels alone, but from trial-and-error feedback about which actions lead to better outcomes. This framework becomes relevant to language models because text generation can be treated as a sequence of actions, where each generated token is an action and the final response can be rewarded according to human preference.

text:
Reinforcement Learning: Agent, State, Action, Reward

This slide expands the reinforcement learning framework by labeling the interaction loop with action, state, and reward. At time step t, the agent chooses an action a_t while being in a state s_t, and the environment returns a reward r_t. The state represents the information available to the agent at that moment. The action is the choice made by the agent. The reward is the feedback signal that tells the agent whether the action was beneficial according to the objective. This notation is essential for later understanding PPO and RLHF: in an LLM, the state can be the current prompt plus generated context, the action can be the next token selected from the vocabulary, and the reward can come from a learned reward model that estimates how desirable the generated response is.

Key concepts: state, action, reward, time step, RL interaction loop

metadata.slide_title: Reinforcement Learning: Agent, State, Action, Reward

text:
Reinforcement Learning: Tic-Tac-Toe

This slide explains reinforcement learning using Tic-Tac-Toe as an intuitive example. The agent uses an RL policy or model to choose actions within an action space, where the actions correspond to legal moves in the game. The environment is the Tic-Tac-Toe board and game rules. The state is the current board configuration, the action is the move selected by the agent, and the reward is determined by the outcome, such as winning, losing, or drawing. The slide includes the terms playout and rollout, which refer to simulating a sequence of actions until an outcome is reached. The objective is simple: win the game. This example prepares students to transfer the same logic to language models, where the 'game' is text generation and the objective is not winning a board game but producing aligned text.

Key concepts: Tic-Tac-Toe, action space, policy, rollout, reward objective

metadata.index_text: Slide 11: RL example with Tic-Tac-Toe: environment, action space, playout/rollout, RL policy model, objective to win the game.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains reinforcement learning using Tic-Tac-Toe as an intuitive example. The agent uses an RL policy or model to choose actions within an action space, where the actions correspond to legal moves in the game. The environment is the Tic-Tac-Toe board and game rules. The state is the current board configuration, the action is the move selected by the agent, and the reward is determined by the outcome, such as winning, losing, or drawing. The slide includes the terms playout and rollout, which refer to simulating a sequence of actions until an outcome is reached. The objective is simple: win the game. This example prepares students to transfer the same logic to language models, where the 'game' is text generation and the objective is not winning a board game but producing aligned text.

text:
Reinforcement Learning: Fine-Tune LLMs

This slide maps the reinforcement learning framework onto language model fine-tuning. The agent is the LLM policy, meaning the language model chooses actions. The action space is the token vocabulary, because at each generation step the model chooses the next token. The state is the current context, which includes the prompt and any tokens already generated. The environment is the LLM context and generation process. A reward model provides a reward r_t that evaluates the generated text. The objective is to generate aligned text. This mapping is central to RLHF: instead of treating the LLM as only a supervised learner, we treat it as a policy that can be optimized to choose better tokens and completions according to a learned reward signal. The reward is not usually provided after every token by a human directly; instead, a reward model trained from human preferences estimates which completions are better.

Key concepts: LLM as policy, token vocabulary, current context, reward model, aligned text generation

metadata.index_text: Slide 12: RL for LLM fine-tuning: action is token choice, state is current context, policy is instruct LLM, reward model scores rollout, objective generate aligned text.
