In practical ANN indexes, parameters control how many candidates are explored and how aggressively the search expands. Larger search effort can improve recall but increases latency. The slide therefore connects the visual algorithm to a common tuning tradeoff in vector databases: search faster with fewer checks, or search more thoroughly to retrieve better nearest neighbors.

text:
Search Algorithm: Near Final Candidate

This slide shows the search near its final candidate. By this point, the algorithm has followed graph connections from the entry point toward a node that appears very close to the query vector. It may still inspect nearby nodes, but fewer improvements remain. This demonstrates how ANN methods reduce work: only a small path and local neighborhood are examined compared with scanning every vector. For large RAG knowledge bases, this can be the difference between an interactive system and an unusably slow one. The slide also reminds students that 'approximate' does not mean random. The algorithm uses the structure of the graph and repeated distance comparisons to make informed moves toward relevant results.

Key concepts: near final candidate, ANN path, efficient vector search, graph connections, retrieval speed, approximate search

This demonstrates how ANN methods reduce work: only a small path and local neighborhood are examined compared with scanning every vector. For large RAG knowledge bases, this can be the difference between an interactive system and an unusably slow one. The slide also reminds students that 'approximate' does not mean random. The algorithm uses the structure of the graph and repeated distance comparisons to make informed moves toward relevant results.

text:
Search Algorithm: No Closer Neighbors

This slide concludes the graph search process with the condition 'No closer neighbors'. Once the current candidate has no neighboring node that is closer to the query vector, the algorithm stops and returns the current candidate or a set of best candidates found during search. This is a local stopping criterion. It does not mathematically prove that no closer vector exists anywhere in the entire database, but it indicates that within the explored graph neighborhood, the search has reached a local optimum. This is the essence of approximate nearest neighbor retrieval. The result is usually good enough and much faster than exact search. In RAG, this candidate or candidate set becomes the retrieved context that will be inserted into the prompt for the LLM.

Key concepts: stopping criterion, no closer neighbors, local optimum, ANN result, retrieved context, graph search

This is a local stopping criterion. It does not mathematically prove that no closer vector exists anywhere in the entire database, but it indicates that within the explored graph neighborhood, the search has reached a local optimum. This is the essence of approximate nearest neighbor retrieval. The result is usually good enough and much faster than exact search. In RAG, this candidate or candidate set becomes the retrieved context that will be inserted into the prompt for the LLM.

text:
Hierarchical Improvement

This slide introduces Hierarchical Navigable Small World, or HNSW, as an improvement over ordinary navigable small world search. HNSW speeds up the early part of search by using a hierarchy of proximity graphs. Instead of searching only one dense graph, the system starts in a sparse upper layer where it can make large jumps across the vector space. It then descends to lower, denser layers for more precise search. The slide explains that this hierarchy accelerates navigation because the search can quickly get near the query region before performing detailed local exploration. HNSW is one of the most widely used ANN algorithms in vector databases because it provides strong speed-recall performance for high-dimensional embeddings. The core idea is to combine fast coarse search with accurate fine search.

Key concepts: HNSW, Hierarchical Navigable Small World, hierarchical graph, ANN, fast navigation, proximity graph

metadata.index_text: Slide 72 | Hierarchical Improvement | HNSW | hierarchical proximity graph | speeds up early search
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces Hierarchical Navigable Small World, or HNSW, as an improvement over ordinary navigable small world search. HNSW speeds up the early part of search by using a hierarchy of proximity graphs. Instead of searching only one dense graph, the system starts in a sparse upper layer where it can make large jumps across the vector space. It then descends to lower, denser layers for more precise search. The slide explains that this hierarchy accelerates navigation because the search can quickly get near the query region before performing detailed local exploration. HNSW is one of the most widely used ANN algorithms in vector databases because it provides strong speed-recall performance for high-dimensional embeddings. The core idea is to combine fast coarse search with accurate fine search.

text:
HNSW Layers

This slide explains the layered structure of HNSW using an example with 1,000 vectors. Layer 1 contains all 1,000 vectors and a complete proximity graph for precise final search. Layer 2 randomly drops to 100 vectors and builds a new proximity graph for intermediate navigation. Layer 3 randomly drops to just 10 vectors and creates a sparse graph for fast navigation at the highest level. The idea is similar to using a map at different zoom levels. At the top layer, the search can make broad movements across the space with very few nodes. At lower layers, it uses more detailed graphs to refine the result. This hierarchy reduces the number of distance comparisons needed while still allowing the search to become precise near the end.

Key concepts: HNSW layers, layered proximity graph, coarse-to-fine search, random sampling, vector index, ANN structure

metadata.index_text: Slide 73 | HNSW layers | Layer 1 all 1000 vectors | Layer 2 100 vectors | Layer 3 10 vectors | proximity graph
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains the layered structure of HNSW using an example with 1,000 vectors. Layer 1 contains all 1,000 vectors and a complete proximity graph for precise final search. Layer 2 randomly drops to 100 vectors and builds a new proximity graph for intermediate navigation. Layer 3 randomly drops to just 10 vectors and creates a sparse graph for fast navigation at the highest level. The idea is similar to using a map at different zoom levels. At the top layer, the search can make broad movements across the space with very few nodes. At lower layers, it uses more detailed graphs to refine the result. This hierarchy reduces the number of distance comparisons needed while still allowing the search to become precise near the end.

text:
HNSW Search Process

This slide explains how search proceeds through the HNSW hierarchy. The algorithm starts with a random candidate vector at the top layer and searches that sparse graph to get as close as possible within that layer. Then it starts at the best candidate from layer 3 and searches through layer 2. Finally, it starts at the best candidate from layer 2 and completes a normal search through the full layer 1 graph. The slide highlights two benefits: make big jumps early and arrive close to the prompt vector once in layer 1. This is a powerful intuition. The upper layers provide speed by covering large distances quickly, while the lower layers provide accuracy by searching dense neighborhoods. HNSW therefore improves vector retrieval performance without needing exhaustive KNN comparisons.

Key concepts: HNSW search, top layer, big jumps, coarse-to-fine, Layer 1 final search, ANN efficiency

Then it starts at the best candidate from layer 3 and searches through layer 2. Finally, it starts at the best candidate from layer 2 and completes a normal search through the full layer 1 graph. The slide highlights two benefits: make big jumps early and arrive close to the prompt vector once in layer 1. This is a powerful intuition. The upper layers provide speed by covering large distances quickly, while the lower layers provide accuracy by searching dense neighborhoods. HNSW therefore improves vector retrieval performance without needing exhaustive KNN comparisons.

text:
Vector Databases

This slide introduces vector databases as the storage and retrieval infrastructure for embeddings. After documents are converted into high-dimensional vectors, a system needs a way to store those vectors, index them, and search them quickly. A standard relational database can store text and metadata, but it is not optimized for high-dimensional nearest-neighbor search at scale. Vector databases are designed specifically for vector search and often implement ANN algorithms such as HNSW. In RAG applications, the vector database stores document chunks, their embeddings, and metadata such as source, title, page number, date, or permissions. When a user asks a question, the application embeds the query and searches the vector database for nearby document vectors. This slide marks the transition from retrieval algorithms to practical database tooling.

Key concepts: vector database, embeddings, vector storage, ANN index, RAG infrastructure, semantic search

Vector databases are designed specifically for vector search and often implement ANN algorithms such as HNSW. In RAG applications, the vector database stores document chunks, their embeddings, and metadata such as source, title, page number, date, or permissions. When a user asks a question, the application embeds the query and searches the vector database for nearby document vectors. This slide marks the transition from retrieval algorithms to practical database tooling.

text:
Vector Database Capabilities

This slide defines what a vector database is and why it is useful. A vector database is designed to store high-dimensional vectors and perform vector search using approximate nearest neighbor algorithms. It can outperform relational databases for this task because relational databases typically perform vector search like inefficient exact KNN unless specialized extensions are added. Vector databases are optimized to build indexes such as HNSW and compute vector distances quickly. The slide mentions Weaviate as a popular open-source vector database used in the course. The key idea is that vector databases are not merely storage systems; they are retrieval engines optimized for embeddings. They often support hybrid search, metadata filtering, object storage, batch imports, and APIs that make them suitable for production RAG pipelines.

Key concepts: vector database, ANN search, HNSW index, Weaviate, relational database limitation, high-dimensional vectors

metadata.index_text: Slide 76 | Vector Database | designed for vector search | outperform relational databases | optimized ANN | Weaviate
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide defines what a vector database is and why it is useful. A vector database is designed to store high-dimensional vectors and perform vector search using approximate nearest neighbor algorithms. It can outperform relational databases for this task because relational databases typically perform vector search like inefficient exact KNN unless specialized extensions are added. Vector databases are optimized to build indexes such as HNSW and compute vector distances quickly. The slide mentions Weaviate as a popular open-source vector database used in the course. The key idea is that vector databases are not merely storage systems; they are retrieval engines optimized for embeddings. They often support hybrid search, metadata filtering, object storage, batch imports, and APIs that make them suitable for production RAG pipelines.

text:
Typical Vector Database Operations

This slide lists the typical operations involved in using a vector database. First, the database must be set up. Then documents are loaded. Sparse vectors can be created for keyword search, and dense vectors can be created for semantic search. An HNSW index can be built to power ANN search. Finally, searches are run. This list summarizes the practical workflow for building retrieval infrastructure. It also shows that modern vector databases often support both sparse and dense retrieval, enabling hybrid search. In a RAG project, these operations usually happen in two phases: an offline indexing phase, where documents are processed and stored, and an online inference phase, where user queries are embedded and searched. Understanding this workflow helps students move from theory to implementation.

Key concepts: database setup, load documents, sparse vectors, dense vectors, HNSW index, run searches

This list summarizes the practical workflow for building retrieval infrastructure. It also shows that modern vector databases often support both sparse and dense retrieval, enabling hybrid search. In a RAG project, these operations usually happen in two phases: an offline indexing phase, where documents are processed and stored, and an online inference phase, where user queries are embedded and searched. Understanding this workflow helps students move from theory to implementation.

text:
Connect to Database and Create a Collection

This slide shows a Weaviate code example for creating a collection. The code imports configuration classes and calls client.collections.create with the collection name 'Article'. The annotation 'Creates collection' points to the collection creation call. The vectorizer configuration specifies text2vec_openai, meaning the database will use an OpenAI-compatible text vectorizer to create embeddings. The properties section defines fields such as title and body, both with text data type. Conceptually, a collection is like a table or class that stores objects with a defined schema. In a RAG system, each object may represent an article, document chunk, or knowledge item. Defining properties is important because metadata and text fields determine what can be searched, filtered, displayed, and cited later.

Key concepts: Weaviate, collection, vectorizer, properties, schema, text2vec_openai

text:
Adding Objects to a Collection

This slide shows how documents or objects are added to a vector database collection in batches. The code uses a fixed-size batch with batch_size=200 and loops over data rows, adding each object with its properties. Batch insertion is important because adding many documents one at a time can be slow. The slide also highlights error handling: if the number of errors exceeds 10, the import stops, and failed objects are collected and printed. This is a practical implementation detail that matters in real RAG projects, where document ingestion may fail due to malformed rows, missing fields, invalid data types, or API issues. The annotations 'Batch adding data', 'Detecting errors', and 'Handling failures' emphasize that building a knowledge base is not only about embeddings but also about reliable data engineering.

Key concepts: batch import, Weaviate collection, error detection, failed objects, data ingestion, RAG indexing

Vector Search in Action

This slide demonstrates how the earlier embedding and ANN concepts appear in actual code. In a RAG pipeline, the returned article properties would be inserted into the augmented prompt as retrieved context.

Key concepts: vector search, near_text, Weaviate query, vector distance, Article collection, semantic retrieval

The metadata query asks for vector distances, allowing the user to inspect how close each result was to the query. The annotations identify the collection selection, the near_text vector search, and the request for vector distances. This slide demonstrates how the earlier embedding and ANN concepts appear in actual code. In a RAG pipeline, the returned article properties would be inserted into the augmented prompt as retrieved context.

text:
Keyword Search in Action

This slide shows keyword search using BM25 in Weaviate. The code calls articles.query.bm25 with the query 'Vancouver hotel capacity' and limit=3. BM25 is a strong lexical retrieval method that ranks documents based on keyword matching, term frequency, inverse document frequency, and length normalization. Unlike near_text vector search, BM25 focuses on exact terms in the query. The loop prints the properties of each returned object. This slide helps students connect the earlier theoretical discussion of keyword search and BM25 to actual implementation. In RAG, BM25 can be useful when exact words such as 'Vancouver', 'hotel', and 'capacity' are important. It can also be combined with semantic retrieval in hybrid search to improve robustness.

Key concepts: BM25, keyword search, Weaviate, lexical retrieval, exact terms, limit results

metadata.index_text: Slide 81 | Keyword Search in Action | articles.query.bm25 | Vancouver hotel capacity | BM25 keyword search
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows keyword search using BM25 in Weaviate. The code calls articles.query.bm25 with the query 'Vancouver hotel capacity' and limit=3. BM25 is a strong lexical retrieval method that ranks documents based on keyword matching, term frequency, inverse document frequency, and length normalization. Unlike near_text vector search, BM25 focuses on exact terms in the query. The loop prints the properties of each returned object. This slide helps students connect the earlier theoretical discussion of keyword search and BM25 to actual implementation. In RAG, BM25 can be useful when exact words such as 'Vancouver', 'hotel', and 'capacity' are important. It can also be combined with semantic retrieval in hybrid search to improve robustness.

text:
Hybrid Search in Action

This slide shows hybrid search in Weaviate. The code calls articles.query.hybrid with the query 'Vancouver hotel capacity', alpha=0.25, and limit=3. The annotation explains that this weights the hybrid search to 25 percent vector search and 75 percent keyword search. This means exact keyword matching has stronger influence than semantic similarity in the final ranking. Hybrid search is useful because it can retrieve documents that share exact important terms while also considering meaning. The alpha parameter is a practical tuning knob: increasing alpha gives more weight to vector similarity, while decreasing it gives more weight to keyword matching. This slide is valuable because it shows that hybrid retrieval is not just a concept; it is directly supported by vector database APIs and can be adjusted based on application needs.

Key concepts: hybrid search, alpha, vector weight, keyword weight, Weaviate, retrieval tuning

metadata.index_text: Slide 82 | Hybrid Search in Action | articles.query.hybrid | alpha 0.25 | 25% vector 75% keyword

The annotation explains that this weights the hybrid search to 25 percent vector search and 75 percent keyword search. This means exact keyword matching has stronger influence than semantic similarity in the final ranking. Hybrid search is useful because it can retrieve documents that share exact important terms while also considering meaning. The alpha parameter is a practical tuning knob: increasing alpha gives more weight to vector similarity, while decreasing it gives more weight to keyword matching. This slide is valuable because it shows that hybrid retrieval is not just a concept; it is directly supported by vector database APIs and can be adjusted based on application needs.

text:
Filtered Search in Action

This slide shows hybrid search combined with a metadata filter. The query is 'History of urban development in Vancouver', alpha is set to 0.3, and limit is 4. The filter uses Filter.by_property('title').contains_any(['Vancouver']), meaning results are restricted to objects whose title contains Vancouver. This demonstrates how metadata filters can enforce strict criteria while hybrid search ranks relevant content within that constrained subset. In practice, filters may restrict results by date, source, department, user permissions, language, document type, or title. The slide's annotation 'Adding a metadata filter' emphasizes that filtering is often essential in production RAG, especially when the knowledge base is large or contains documents from multiple domains. The retrieved documents are more likely to be relevant and appropriate because irrelevant titles are excluded before ranking.

Key concepts: filtered search, metadata filter, hybrid search, title filter, Vancouver, Weaviate

text:
Complete Workflow

This slide summarizes the complete retrieval workflow in three steps: configure the database, load and index data, and perform searches. Configuration includes creating collections, schemas, vectorizers, and indexes. Loading and indexing data includes inserting documents, generating embeddings, creating sparse vectors, and building ANN structures such as HNSW. Performing searches includes vector search, keyword search, hybrid search, and filtered search. This workflow captures the engineering lifecycle of the retrieval side of RAG. The LLM can only generate grounded answers if the knowledge base has been prepared correctly. Poor ingestion, missing metadata, bad chunking, or incorrect indexes can lead to irrelevant retrieval and weak final answers. The slide therefore serves as a practical checklist for implementing vector database retrieval.

Key concepts: complete workflow, configure database, load data, index data, perform searches, RAG implementation

The LLM can only generate grounded answers if the knowledge base has been prepared correctly. Poor ingestion, missing metadata, bad chunking, or incorrect indexes can lead to irrelevant retrieval and weak final answers. The slide therefore serves as a practical checklist for implementing vector database retrieval.

text:
Using Your Own Prompts

This slide introduces the section on custom prompts. After building the retrieval system, the next step is to construct prompts that tell the LLM how to use the retrieved information. In RAG, prompting is not just a user question; it is an assembled input containing system instructions, conversation history, retrieved documents, metadata, and the current query. Using your own prompts means designing the behavior of the LLM for the application. For example, an assistant may be instructed to answer in detail, answer concisely, cite sources, admit missing information, or avoid using outside knowledge. This section is important because even excellent retrieval can fail if the prompt does not clearly tell the LLM how to interpret and use the retrieved context.

Key concepts: custom prompts, prompt design, RAG prompting, system instructions, retrieved context, application behavior

metadata.index_text: Slide 85 | Using your own prompts | prompt design | custom system prompt | RAG context
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the section on custom prompts. After building the retrieval system, the next step is to construct prompts that tell the LLM how to use the retrieved information. In RAG, prompting is not just a user question; it is an assembled input containing system instructions, conversation history, retrieved documents, metadata, and the current query. Using your own prompts means designing the behavior of the LLM for the application. For example, an assistant may be instructed to answer in detail, answer concisely, cite sources, admit missing information, or avoid using outside knowledge. This section is important because even excellent retrieval can fail if the prompt does not clearly tell the LLM how to interpret and use the retrieved context.

text:
Your Own System Prompt

This slide gives principles for constructing a system prompt. The system prompt can instruct the LLM to respond in great detail or answer succinctly. For RAG applications, the slide recommends telling the language model to use only retrieved documents to answer prompts, judge whether a document is relevant, and cite sources in its response. It also notes that system prompts are added to every prompt the LLM processes. This makes the system prompt a persistent behavioral layer. In RAG, these instructions are critical for reducing hallucinations. If the retrieved documents do not contain the answer, the model should admit that information is missing rather than inventing an answer. A strong system prompt therefore defines not only tone and style but also evidence discipline, citation behavior, and boundaries on what sources the model may use.

Key concepts: system prompt, RAG instructions, use retrieved documents, cite sources, admit missing information, behavioral guidance

metadata.index_text: Slide 86 | Your own system prompt | respond in detail or succinctly | use only retrieved documents | judge relevance | cite sources

For RAG applications, the slide recommends telling the language model to use only retrieved documents to answer prompts, judge whether a document is relevant, and cite sources in its response. It also notes that system prompts are added to every prompt the LLM processes. This makes the system prompt a persistent behavioral layer. In RAG, these instructions are critical for reducing hallucinations. If the retrieved documents do not contain the answer, the model should admit that information is missing rather than inventing an answer. A strong system prompt therefore defines not only tone and style but also evidence discipline, citation behavior, and boundaries on what sources the model may use.

text:
Prompt Templates

This slide presents a structured prompt template for RAG. The template includes system instructions, conversation history, retrieved information, and the user prompt. The system instructions provide behavioral guidance. The conversation history preserves prior turns when relevant. The retrieved information section inserts document chunks and their sources, such as [DOCUMENT 1] with chunk text and source metadata. The final user prompt contains the current query. The slide emphasizes that prompt templates provide structure and decide where content gets injected. This is essential because RAG prompts include many different pieces of information. Without a template, the model may confuse retrieved documents with user text, ignore citations, or fail to follow instructions. A good template makes the augmented prompt consistent, debuggable, and easier to improve.

Key concepts: prompt template, system instructions, conversation history, retrieved information, source metadata, augmented prompt

metadata.index_text: Slide 87 | Prompt Templates | system_prompt | conversation_history | retrieved information | chunk source | user_query

The slide emphasizes that prompt templates provide structure and decide where content gets injected. This is essential because RAG prompts include many different pieces of information. Without a template, the model may confuse retrieved documents with user text, ignore citations, or fail to follow instructions. A good template makes the augmented prompt consistent, debuggable, and easier to improve.

text:
Prompt Template Example

This slide shows a concrete RAG prompt template for geographic information. The system instructions say the assistant should use only retrieved documents, cite sources as [DOC X], and admit if information is missing. The conversation history includes a previous question about Canada's largest city and an answer citing [DOC 1]. The retrieved documents contain facts about Ottawa: one states that Ottawa is the capital city of Canada in Ontario, another says Queen Victoria chose it as capital in 1857, and another says Parliament is located on Parliament Hill. The current query asks, 'What is the capital of Canada?' The correct response should be grounded in [DOC 1], not generated from unsupported memory. This slide demonstrates how a RAG prompt organizes evidence so the model can answer accurately, cite the supporting document, and avoid unsupported claims.

Key concepts: RAG prompt example, geographic information, retrieved documents, citations, Ottawa, source-grounded answer

Lecture References

This final slide lists the lecture reference: the DeepLearning.AI course on Retrieval Augmented Generation, covering Modules 1 to 4. For students, it also points to where they can study the topics in more depth, especially if they want hands-on exercises with vector databases, hybrid search, and RAG prompt templates. The slide closes the lecture after moving from the basic need for retrieval, through keyword and semantic search, to approximate nearest neighbor algorithms, vector database implementation, and prompt design for grounded generation.

Key concepts: lecture references, DeepLearning.AI, RAG course, retrieval foundations, vector databases, prompt templates

text:
Understanding Agentic AI

This opening slide introduces the lecture topic, Understanding Agentic AI. The visual theme shows a human head surrounded by digital devices, a CPU, gears, messages, and connected circuits, which suggests AI systems that do more than generate text: they interact with tools, information sources, and workflows. The title signals that the lecture will focus on agentic AI, meaning LLM-based systems that can perform multi-step tasks, make intermediate decisions, use tools, reflect on outputs, and coordinate actions toward a goal. Unlike a simple chatbot that only answers one prompt at a time, an agentic system is designed as a process: it can break a task into steps, execute those steps, inspect results, and continue until the task is completed. This slide therefore frames the course topic as a move from single-response language modeling toward practical AI systems that behave more like task-solving assistants.

Key concepts: Agentic AI, LLM applications, multi-step workflows, tool use, AI assistants

metadata.index_text: Slide 1 | Understanding Agentic AI | Dr. Tamer Arafa | lecture title | agentic workflows
metadata.slide_paragraph: This opening slide introduces the lecture topic, Understanding Agentic AI. The visual theme shows a human head surrounded by digital devices, a CPU, gears, messages, and connected circuits, which suggests AI systems that do more than generate text: they interact with tools, information sources, and workflows. The title signals that the lecture will focus on agentic AI, meaning LLM-based systems that can perform multi-step tasks, make intermediate decisions, use tools, reflect on outputs, and coordinate actions toward a goal. Unlike a simple chatbot that only answers one prompt at a time, an agentic system is designed as a process: it can break a task into steps, execute those steps, inspect results, and continue until the task is completed. This slide therefore frames the course topic as a move from single-response language modeling toward practical AI systems that behave more like task-solving assistants.

text:
Introduction to Agentic Workflows: What is Agentic AI?

This slide begins the first section of the lecture by asking what Agentic AI is. It acts as a transition from the title slide into the conceptual definition of agentic workflows. The phrase 'agentic workflows' indicates that the lecture is not only about a model, but about a structured process in which an LLM-based application performs a sequence of actions. The slide prepares the student to think about autonomy, planning, tool usage, reflection, and task decomposition. In this context, 'agentic' does not necessarily mean fully independent or human-like; it means that the system has some ability to decide or execute intermediate steps rather than simply producing a single final answer in one pass.

Key concepts: agentic workflows, Agentic AI definition, autonomy, LLM-based application, task process

metadata.slide_title: Introduction to Agentic Workflows: What is Agentic AI?
metadata.index_text: Slide 2 | Introduction to Agentic Workflows | What is Agentic AI | autonomy and workflow
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide begins the first section of the lecture by asking what Agentic AI is. It acts as a transition from the title slide into the conceptual definition of agentic workflows. The phrase 'agentic workflows' indicates that the lecture is not only about a model, but about a structured process in which an LLM-based application performs a sequence of actions. The slide prepares the student to think about autonomy, planning, tool usage, reflection, and task decomposition. In this context, 'agentic' does not necessarily mean fully independent or human-like; it means that the system has some ability to decide or execute intermediate steps rather than simply producing a single final answer in one pass.

text:
Agentic AI

This slide contrasts a non-agentic workflow with an agentic workflow using essay writing as the example. In the non-agentic zero-shot workflow, the user asks the model to type an essay from start to finish in one go without using backspace. This represents direct generation: the model must produce the final answer immediately, with no explicit planning, research, checking, or revision. The agentic workflow, by contrast, breaks the task into multiple stages: write an outline, decide whether web research is needed, write a first draft, inspect which parts require revision or more research, and then revise the draft. The diagram emphasizes that agentic AI adds intermediate thinking, research, and revision loops. This is closer to how humans solve difficult tasks: we usually plan, gather information, draft, critique, and improve rather than expecting the first attempt to be final. The key lesson is that agentic systems improve output quality by organizing LLM work into iterative steps.

Key concepts: non-agentic workflow, zero-shot generation, agentic workflow, essay writing, revision loop, research

metadata.index_text: Slide 3 | Non-agentic workflow vs agentic workflow | essay outline | web research | first draft | revise
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide contrasts a non-agentic workflow with an agentic workflow using essay writing as the example. In the non-agentic zero-shot workflow, the user asks the model to type an essay from start to finish in one go without using backspace. This represents direct generation: the model must produce the final answer immediately, with no explicit planning, research, checking, or revision. The agentic workflow, by contrast, breaks the task into multiple stages: write an outline, decide whether web research is needed, write a first draft, inspect which parts require revision or more research, and then revise the draft. The diagram emphasizes that agentic AI adds intermediate thinking, research, and revision loops. This is closer to how humans solve difficult tasks: we usually plan, gather information, draft, critique, and improve rather than expecting the first attempt to be final. The key lesson is that agentic systems improve output quality by organizing LLM work into iterative steps.

text:
Agentic AI Workflows

This slide defines an agentic AI workflow as a process where an LLM-based application executes multiple steps to complete a task. The essay-writing example shows several LLM calls and possible tool interactions: one LLM step writes an outline, another determines whether web research is needed, another writes a first draft, another considers revision needs, and another may request human review. The diagram makes clear that an agentic workflow can combine pure language generation with tool use, web search, and human-in-the-loop review. The same model may be called repeatedly, or different models and tools may be used at different stages. The important idea is that the intelligence of the application comes not only from the LLM itself but from the workflow design around it. By decomposing the task, the system can handle complexity more reliably than a single prompt-response interaction.

Key concepts: agentic workflow, multi-step execution, web search, human review, LLM orchestration, task decomposition

metadata.index_text: Slide 4 | Agentic AI workflows | LLM executes multiple steps | essay-writing example | web search | human review
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide defines an agentic AI workflow as a process where an LLM-based application executes multiple steps to complete a task. The essay-writing example shows several LLM calls and possible tool interactions: one LLM step writes an outline, another determines whether web research is needed, another writes a first draft, another considers revision needs, and another may request human review. The diagram makes clear that an agentic workflow can combine pure language generation with tool use, web search, and human-in-the-loop review. The same model may be called repeatedly, or different models and tools may be used at different stages. The important idea is that the intelligence of the application comes not only from the LLM itself but from the workflow design around it. By decomposing the task, the system can handle complexity more reliably than a single prompt-response interaction.

text:
Degrees of Agenticness

This slide introduces the idea that agentic AI is not a strict yes-or-no category. The slide references Andrew Ng and includes the message that instead of arguing about whether a system is a true agent, it is more useful to acknowledge different degrees of agentic behavior. The image from a talk and the social-media-style cartoon both reinforce this idea: some systems may be more agentic than others depending on how much autonomy they have, how many decisions they make, and how much they can interact with tools or the environment. This is important because many real-world systems fall between a simple chatbot and a fully autonomous agent. A system that follows a fixed chain of tool calls may be only mildly agentic, while a system that plans, chooses tools, evaluates results, and revises its strategy is more agentic. The slide encourages a spectrum-based understanding of agentic AI.

Key concepts: degrees of autonomy, agenticness, Andrew Ng, agent spectrum, autonomous systems

metadata.index_text: Slide 5 | Degrees of agenticness | Andrew Ng | Sequoia Ascent | different degrees systems can be agentic
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the idea that agentic AI is not a strict yes-or-no category. The slide references Andrew Ng and includes the message that instead of arguing about whether a system is a true agent, it is more useful to acknowledge different degrees of agentic behavior. The image from a talk and the social-media-style cartoon both reinforce this idea: some systems may be more agentic than others depending on how much autonomy they have, how many decisions they make, and how much they can interact with tools or the environment. This is important because many real-world systems fall between a simple chatbot and a fully autonomous agent. A system that follows a fixed chain of tool calls may be only mildly agentic, while a system that plans, chooses tools, evaluates results, and revises its strategy is more agentic. The slide encourages a spectrum-based understanding of agentic AI.

text:
Degrees of Autonomy

This slide illustrates increasing levels of autonomy using the task 'write an essay about black holes.' On the less autonomous side, the workflow is mostly predetermined: the user asks for the essay, the system may perform a fixed web search and fetch pages, and then the LLM writes the essay. The middle of the slide shows a more structured workflow where tools such as web fetch and PDF-to-text are available, and the system may retrieve the five best sources, draft the essay, and reflect to improve the draft. On the more autonomous side, the LLM itself can write web search queries, decide which search tool to use, search the web or arXiv, fetch information, and refine the essay. The diagram therefore explains that autonomy depends on how many decisions are delegated to the agent. More autonomy means the agent chooses steps and tools dynamically rather than simply executing a hard-coded path.

Key concepts: degrees of autonomy, tool choice, web search, PDF-to-text, reflection, dynamic planning

metadata.index_text: Slide 6 | Degrees of autonomy | less autonomous to more autonomous | black holes essay | tools web search arXiv pdf to text
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide illustrates increasing levels of autonomy using the task 'write an essay about black holes.' On the less autonomous side, the workflow is mostly predetermined: the user asks for the essay, the system may perform a fixed web search and fetch pages, and then the LLM writes the essay. The middle of the slide shows a more structured workflow where tools such as web fetch and PDF-to-text are available, and the system may retrieve the five best sources, draft the essay, and reflect to improve the draft. On the more autonomous side, the LLM itself can write web search queries, decide which search tool to use, search the web or arXiv, fetch information, and refine the essay. The diagram therefore explains that autonomy depends on how many decisions are delegated to the agent. More autonomy means the agent chooses steps and tools dynamically rather than simply executing a hard-coded path.

text:
Degrees of Agenticness

This slide summarizes the autonomy spectrum using three categories: less autonomous, semi-autonomous, and highly autonomous. In less autonomous systems, all steps are predetermined and all tool use is hard-coded; the main autonomy is only in text generation. For example, a fixed pipeline may always search, retrieve, summarize, and answer in the same order. In semi-autonomous systems, the agent can make some decisions and choose among predefined tools, but it cannot invent or add new tools on its own. In highly autonomous systems, the agent can make many decisions independently and may even create new tools on the fly. The slide helps students understand that agentic AI design involves deciding how much freedom to give the model. More autonomy can increase flexibility but also increases the need for evaluation, safety controls, monitoring, and clear boundaries.

Key concepts: less autonomous, semi-autonomous, highly autonomous, tool selection, hard-coded workflow, autonomy spectrum

metadata.index_text: Slide 7 | Degrees of agenticness | all steps predetermined | choose tools | create new tools on the fly
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes the autonomy spectrum using three categories: less autonomous, semi-autonomous, and highly autonomous. In less autonomous systems, all steps are predetermined and all tool use is hard-coded; the main autonomy is only in text generation. For example, a fixed pipeline may always search, retrieve, summarize, and answer in the same order. In semi-autonomous systems, the agent can make some decisions and choose among predefined tools, but it cannot invent or add new tools on its own. In highly autonomous systems, the agent can make many decisions independently and may even create new tools on the fly. The slide helps students understand that agentic AI design involves deciding how much freedom to give the model. More autonomy can increase flexibility but also increases the need for evaluation, safety controls, monitoring, and clear boundaries.

text:
Agentic Systems on Coding Benchmarks

This slide presents evidence that agentic systems can improve performance on coding benchmarks such as HumanEval. The chart compares non-agentic systems with agentic design patterns including reflection, tool use, planning, multiagent collaboration, tree search, Reflexion, CodeT, AgentCoder, MetaGPT, and related approaches. The slide indicates that for GPT-3.5, non-agentic performance is around 67%, and for GPT-4, non-agentic performance is around 48% in the displayed comparison, while several agentic methods achieve higher scores. The exact numbers are less important than the pattern: systems that can reason through steps, critique outputs, use tools, or involve multiple agents often perform better than direct generation. The slide also shows that agentic AI is not one technique; it is a family of workflow patterns. Students should understand this as empirical motivation for building structured LLM applications rather than relying only on a single prompt.

Key concepts: HumanEval, coding benchmark, reflection, tool use, planning, multiagent systems, performance improvement

metadata.index_text: Slide 8 | Non-agentic vs agentic systems | Coding benchmark HumanEval | Reflection Tool Use Planning Multiagent
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide presents evidence that agentic systems can improve performance on coding benchmarks such as HumanEval. The chart compares non-agentic systems with agentic design patterns including reflection, tool use, planning, multiagent collaboration, tree search, Reflexion, CodeT, AgentCoder, MetaGPT, and related approaches. The slide indicates that for GPT-3.5, non-agentic performance is around 67%, and for GPT-4, non-agentic performance is around 48% in the displayed comparison, while several agentic methods achieve higher scores. The exact numbers are less important than the pattern: systems that can reason through steps, critique outputs, use tools, or involve multiple agents often perform better than direct generation. The slide also shows that agentic AI is not one technique; it is a family of workflow patterns. Students should understand this as empirical motivation for building structured LLM applications rather than relying only on a single prompt.

text:
Parallelization for Speed

This slide shows how agentic workflows can become faster through parallelization. In the essay-writing example, instead of performing a single search and then fetching pages one at a time, the system can run three web searches in parallel, fetch the top three results from each search in parallel, and then pass the collected material to LLM steps that synthesize the essay. The diagram contains multiple LLM and web fetch boxes running side by side, illustrating that tasks which do not depend on each other can be executed simultaneously. This is one practical advantage of agentic design: a human researcher may open sources one by one, but an AI workflow can launch multiple searches and downloads at the same time. Parallelization can reduce latency and improve coverage, especially for research tasks, data collection, document review, and multi-source retrieval.

Key concepts: parallelization, web search, web fetch, speed, agentic workflow, source collection

metadata.index_text: Slide 9 | Parallelization for speed | 3 parallel searches | 9 parallel web page downloads | write essay
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows how agentic workflows can become faster through parallelization. In the essay-writing example, instead of performing a single search and then fetching pages one at a time, the system can run three web searches in parallel, fetch the top three results from each search in parallel, and then pass the collected material to LLM steps that synthesize the essay. The diagram contains multiple LLM and web fetch boxes running side by side, illustrating that tasks which do not depend on each other can be executed simultaneously. This is one practical advantage of agentic design: a human researcher may open sources one by one, but an AI workflow can launch multiple searches and downloads at the same time. Parallelization can reduce latency and improve coverage, especially for research tasks, data collection, document review, and multi-source retrieval.

text:
Modular Workflows

This slide explains that agentic workflows are modular, meaning individual components can be added, removed, or replaced without redesigning the entire system. The diagram again uses the essay-writing workflow, but it highlights that a new tool such as a news search tool can be added alongside web search and web fetch. Modularity is important because real applications evolve: a developer may replace one search API with another, add a PDF parser, swap an LLM for a stronger model, add a reranker, or insert a human-review stage. The workflow architecture separates the task into components, and each component can be improved independently. This makes agentic systems flexible and maintainable. Rather than building a single monolithic prompt, developers can treat the application as a pipeline of tools, models, prompts, and decision points.

Key concepts: modularity, tool swapping, workflow components, web search, news tool, maintainability

metadata.index_text: Slide 10 | Modular add swap components | new web news tool | web search | web fetch | LLM essay workflow

Modularity is important because real applications evolve: a developer may replace one search API with another, add a PDF parser, swap an LLM for a stronger model, add a reranker, or insert a human-review stage. The workflow architecture separates the task into components, and each component can be improved independently. This makes agentic systems flexible and maintainable. Rather than building a single monolithic prompt, developers can treat the application as a pipeline of tools, models, prompts, and decision points.

text:
Key Benefits of Agentic Workflows

This slide summarizes three major benefits of agentic workflows. First, they can produce much better performance because difficult tasks are broken down into planning, retrieval, execution, reflection, and revision instead of relying on a single model call. Second, they can be faster than humans because independent steps can be parallelized, such as running multiple searches or processing multiple documents at the same time. Third, they are modular, allowing developers to add tools, update tools, swap models, or change individual workflow components without rewriting the whole application. These benefits explain why agentic AI is useful for real applications: it improves quality, speed, and engineering flexibility. However, the same benefits also imply that agentic systems require careful design, logging, evaluation, and safety controls because they involve multiple moving parts.

Key concepts: performance, parallelization, modularity, tool updates, workflow design, agentic benefits

text:
Agentic AI Applications

This slide introduces the next section of the lecture, focusing on practical applications of agentic AI. After defining agentic workflows and their benefits, the lecture now moves toward concrete examples where LLM-based systems perform useful business or productivity tasks. The phrase 'Agentic AI applications' signals that the upcoming slides will show workflows such as invoice processing, customer email handling, customer service, information extraction, and database updates. The main educational purpose of this transition is to move from abstract concepts like autonomy and modularity to implementation thinking: what real tasks can be decomposed into model calls, tools, and decision steps? Students should prepare to analyze each example by identifying the goal, required data, tools, workflow steps, and points where human review or validation may be needed.

Key concepts: agentic applications, workflow examples, business automation, task decomposition, LLM tools

text:
Example: Invoice Processing Workflow

This slide presents an invoice-processing workflow as a simple agentic application. The invoice image contains information from TechFlow Solutions LLC, including address, phone, email, due date, invoice date, item description, quantity, unit price, line total, and total due. The slide identifies four required fields: biller, biller address, amount due, and due date. The workflow converts the PDF to text, passes the extracted text to an LLM, and then uses an update-database tool to create a record. The diagram emphasizes that the LLM is not acting alone; it is part of a pipeline that includes document conversion and a database action. This is a good example of a relatively structured workflow because the required fields and final action are known ahead of time. The agentic behavior is mainly in extracting the correct fields and invoking the database update correctly.

Key concepts: invoice processing, PDF-to-text, information extraction, database update, required fields, structured workflow

text:
Example: Responding to Customer Email

This slide shows an agentic workflow for responding to a customer email about a wrong item being shipped. The email from Susan Jones says she ordered a blue KitchenPro blender with order number 8847 but received a red toaster instead, and she needs help because the blender is for her daughter's birthday party. The workflow has three steps: extract key information, find relevant customer records, and draft a response for human review. The tools include an orders database query and a request-review tool. This example is more complex than invoice extraction because it requires understanding the customer's problem, using the order number to verify records, and producing a helpful response. The human review step is important because sending customer-facing messages or making fulfillment decisions may require approval. The slide illustrates how agentic AI can combine language understanding, database access, and human-in-the-loop supervision.

Key concepts: customer email, order lookup, database query, human review, response drafting, customer support

The workflow has three steps: extract key information, find relevant customer records, and draft a response for human review. The tools include an orders database query and a request-review tool. This example is more complex than invoice extraction because it requires understanding the customer's problem, using the order number to verify records, and producing a helpful response. The human review step is important because sending customer-facing messages or making fulfillment decisions may require approval. The slide illustrates how agentic AI can combine language understanding, database access, and human-in-the-loop supervision.

text:
More Challenging: Customer Service Agent

This slide explains why a general customer service agent is harder than a fixed workflow. The first customer asks whether black jeans or blue jeans are available, so the system must check inventory for both colors and respond. The second customer wants to return a beach towel, so the system must verify the purchase, check return policies, and if the return is allowed, issue a return packing slip and update the database status to return pending. The key phrase is that required steps are not known ahead of time. Unlike invoice processing, where the fields are fixed, customer service involves many possible intents: product questions, returns, refunds, shipping issues, complaints, and policy questions. Therefore, the agent must plan dynamically, choose tools based on the situation, and possibly branch depending on results. This makes the workflow more agentic and more difficult to evaluate safely.

Key concepts: customer service agent, dynamic planning, inventory check, return policy, branching workflow, unknown steps

metadata.index_text: Slide 15 | Customer service agent | black jeans blue jeans | return beach towel | required steps not known ahead of time

The key phrase is that required steps are not known ahead of time. Unlike invoice processing, where the fields are fixed, customer service involves many possible intents: product questions, returns, refunds, shipping issues, complaints, and policy questions. Therefore, the agent must plan dynamically, choose tools based on the situation, and possibly branch depending on results. This makes the workflow more agentic and more difficult to evaluate safely.

text:
What Tasks Is Agentic AI Suited To?

This slide compares easier and harder tasks for agentic AI. Easier tasks have a clear step-by-step process, standard procedures to follow, and mainly text-based assets. Examples include invoice extraction, structured email handling, simple data lookup, or report generation from documents. Harder tasks have steps that are not known ahead of time, require planning or solving as the system goes, and may involve multimodal inputs such as sound or vision. The slide helps students understand that agentic AI is not equally suitable for every problem. When the process is predictable, developers can build robust workflows with predefined tools and checks. When the process is open-ended, the agent needs more autonomy and more careful evaluation. Multimodal tasks add additional difficulty because the system must interpret images, audio, or video before acting.

Key concepts: task suitability, standard procedures, planning, multimodal tasks, difficulty spectrum, workflow design

metadata.slide_title: What Tasks Is Agentic AI Suited To?

The slide helps students understand that agentic AI is not equally suitable for every problem. When the process is predictable, developers can build robust workflows with predefined tools and checks. When the process is open-ended, the agent needs more autonomy and more careful evaluation. Multimodal tasks add additional difficulty because the system must interpret images, audio, or video before acting.

text:
Task Decomposition: Identifying Workflow Steps

This slide introduces task decomposition as a core skill for building agentic workflows. Task decomposition means breaking a large goal into smaller steps that can be handled by an LLM, a tool, another AI model, a database, or a human reviewer. The section title emphasizes that before building an agent, the developer must identify the workflow steps. Without decomposition, the system may rely on a single broad prompt, which is often unreliable for complex tasks. Decomposition also helps developers decide where tools are needed, where outputs should be validated, and where failures may occur. This slide prepares students for the examples that follow, where essay writing, email response, and invoice processing are broken into sequential or branching components.

Key concepts: task decomposition, workflow steps, agent design, tool assignment, failure analysis

metadata.index_text: Slide 17 | Task decomposition | identifying steps in a workflow | agentic workflow design
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces task decomposition as a core skill for building agentic workflows. Task decomposition means breaking a large goal into smaller steps that can be handled by an LLM, a tool, another AI model, a database, or a human reviewer. The section title emphasizes that before building an agent, the developer must identify the workflow steps. Without decomposition, the system may rely on a single broad prompt, which is often unreliable for complex tasks. Decomposition also helps developers decide where tools are needed, where outputs should be validated, and where failures may occur. This slide prepares students for the examples that follow, where essay writing, email response, and invoice processing are broken into sequential or branching components.

text:
Example: Writing an Essay

This slide uses essay writing to show how direct generation can be improved through decomposition but may still be insufficient. A single prompt such as 'Write an essay on topic X' produces a surface-level answer that covers only obvious facts. A three-step workflow improves structure by asking the model to write an outline, search the web, and then write the essay. A five-step workflow adds more quality control: write an outline, search the web, write a first draft, consider what parts need revision, and revise the draft. The slide notes that even this may still not be good enough, which is important because task decomposition is not magic; the quality depends on the steps, prompts, tools, and evaluation criteria. The diagram shows that agentic workflows allow repeated LLM calls and tool calls, but good design still requires deciding what each step should accomplish.

Key concepts: essay writing, direct generation, outline, web search, revision, quality improvement

metadata.index_text: Slide 18 | Example Writing an Essay | surface level | 3-step workflow | 5-step workflow | revise draft
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide uses essay writing to show how direct generation can be improved through decomposition but may still be insufficient. A single prompt such as 'Write an essay on topic X' produces a surface-level answer that covers only obvious facts. A three-step workflow improves structure by asking the model to write an outline, search the web, and then write the essay. A five-step workflow adds more quality control: write an outline, search the web, write a first draft, consider what parts need revision, and revise the draft. The slide notes that even this may still not be good enough, which is important because task decomposition is not magic; the quality depends on the steps, prompts, tools, and evaluation criteria. The diagram shows that agentic workflows allow repeated LLM calls and tool calls, but good design still requires deciding what each step should accomplish.

text:
Recap: Breaking Down Essay Writing Into Steps

This slide summarizes the progression from direct generation to increasingly detailed workflows. Direct generation uses one step: write an essay on topic X, but the slide states this is not good enough. The three-step workflow adds an outline, web search, and essay writing, but it may still be insufficient. The five-step workflow further separates first drafting, revision analysis, and final revision. The educational point is that decomposition can be made more granular when the current output quality is inadequate. However, adding steps has tradeoffs: it increases cost, latency, complexity, and the number of potential failure points. A well-designed agentic workflow should add steps that meaningfully improve quality, not simply add complexity. Students should learn to treat decomposition as an iterative design process guided by evaluation results.

Key concepts: workflow granularity, direct generation, three-step workflow, five-step workflow, revision, evaluation-driven design

text:
Example: Responding to Customer Email

This slide decomposes the customer email response task into three operational steps. First, the LLM extracts key information from the email, such as sender identity, order number, ordered item, received item, urgency, and requested help. Second, the system queries an orders database to find relevant customer records and verify what the customer purchased. Third, the LLM writes and sends a response. The diagram shows LLM calls connected to a database query tool and an email-sending tool. This example demonstrates how task decomposition clarifies responsibilities: the LLM handles understanding and language generation, the database provides factual records, and the email tool performs the external action. It also reveals risk points, such as sending an incorrect response if the database query fails or if the LLM misreads the order details. In production, a human review or validation step may be added before sending.

Key concepts: email response, information extraction, orders database, send email, tool use, customer support workflow

Second, the system queries an orders database to find relevant customer records and verify what the customer purchased. Third, the LLM writes and sends a response. The diagram shows LLM calls connected to a database query tool and an email-sending tool. This example demonstrates how task decomposition clarifies responsibilities: the LLM handles understanding and language generation, the database provides factual records, and the email tool performs the external action. It also reveals risk points, such as sending an incorrect response if the database query fails or if the LLM misreads the order details. In production, a human review or validation step may be added before sending.

text:
Example: Extracting Information From Invoice

This slide decomposes invoice extraction into two steps: find required information and create a new database entry. The invoice image is shown on the left, while the right side shows that an LLM can identify the necessary fields and then work with an update-database tool to save the result. This example highlights a common agentic pattern: the LLM converts unstructured or semi-structured input into structured data, and a tool commits that data to a system of record. The first step is semantic and document-oriented: the model must understand which text corresponds to biller, due date, amount due, and other fields. The second step is operational: the application must update a database in a reliable format. The slide also implies that the database update should not be done from raw text alone; the extracted fields should be validated before insertion.

Key concepts: invoice extraction, structured data, database entry, LLM extraction, update database, document workflow

The first step is semantic and document-oriented: the model must understand which text corresponds to biller, due date, amount due, and other fields. The second step is operational: the application must update a database in a reliable format. The slide also implies that the database update should not be done from raw text alone; the extracted fields should be validated before insertion.

text:
What Building Blocks Do You Have?

This slide lists the main building blocks available when designing agentic systems: models and tools. Under models, it includes LLMs and other AI models. LLMs are useful for text generation, tool use, and information extraction, while other AI models may handle PDF-to-text, text-to-speech, image analysis, and other specialized tasks. Under tools, the slide lists APIs, information retrieval, and code execution. APIs can perform real-world actions such as web search, getting real-time data, sending email, or checking a calendar. Information retrieval includes databases and RAG systems. Code execution can support calculators and data analysis. The table teaches that agentic AI is built by combining specialized capabilities rather than expecting one LLM to do everything. Good architecture means assigning each step to the component best suited for it.

Key concepts: building blocks, LLMs, AI models, APIs, RAG, code execution, tools

metadata.slide_title: What Building Blocks Do You Have?

Under tools, the slide lists APIs, information retrieval, and code execution. APIs can perform real-world actions such as web search, getting real-time data, sending email, or checking a calendar. Information retrieval includes databases and RAG systems. Code execution can support calculators and data analysis. The table teaches that agentic AI is built by combining specialized capabilities rather than expecting one LLM to do everything. Good architecture means assigning each step to the component best suited for it.

text:
Tips for Task Decomposition

This slide gives practical guidance for decomposing tasks. Using the customer email example, it shows three steps: extract key information, find relevant customer records, and write and send a response. The slide asks whether an LLM, a tool, or both can complete each step. This question is central to designing agentic workflows. If a step cannot be reliably completed by the LLM or available tools, the developer should break it into smaller steps or add human-in-the-loop review. For example, 'respond to customer' may be too broad, but 'extract order number', 'query database', 'draft response', and 'request approval' are more manageable. The slide encourages students to think like system designers: each step needs an owner, input, output, success criteria, and failure handling strategy.

Key concepts: task decomposition tips, LLM vs tool, human-in-the-loop, workflow reliability, step design, customer email

metadata.index_text: Slide 23 | Tips for task decomposition | Can LLM tool or both complete each step | break down further | human in loop

Using the customer email example, it shows three steps: extract key information, find relevant customer records, and write and send a response. The slide asks whether an LLM, a tool, or both can complete each step. This question is central to designing agentic workflows. If a step cannot be reliably completed by the LLM or available tools, the developer should break it into smaller steps or add human-in-the-loop review. For example, 'respond to customer' may be too broad, but 'extract order number', 'query database', 'draft response', and 'request approval' are more manageable. The slide encourages students to think like system designers: each step needs an owner, input, output, success criteria, and failure handling strategy.

text:
Evaluating Agentic AI

This slide introduces the evaluation section for agentic AI systems. Evaluation is especially important for agentic workflows because the system may involve multiple LLM calls, tools, branching decisions, and external actions. A simple final answer may hide errors that occurred earlier in the workflow, so developers need both end-to-end evaluation and component-level evaluation. The section title prepares students to learn how to identify low-quality outputs, create objective checks, use LLMs as judges, inspect traces, and perform error analysis. In agentic AI, evaluation is not optional: it is the mechanism that tells the developer whether the decomposition, prompts, tools, and autonomy level are actually improving outcomes.

Key concepts: agent evaluation, evals, end-to-end evaluation, component evaluation, error analysis

metadata.index_text: Slide 24 | Evaluating Agentic AI | evals | agentic workflows | quality measurement
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the evaluation section for agentic AI systems. Evaluation is especially important for agentic workflows because the system may involve multiple LLM calls, tools, branching decisions, and external actions. A simple final answer may hide errors that occurred earlier in the workflow, so developers need both end-to-end evaluation and component-level evaluation. The section title prepares students to learn how to identify low-quality outputs, create objective checks, use LLMs as judges, inspect traces, and perform error analysis. In agentic AI, evaluation is not optional: it is the mechanism that tells the developer whether the decomposition, prompts, tools, and autonomy level are actually improving outcomes.

text:
Look for Low-Quality Outputs

This slide shows why agentic workflows need evaluation by giving examples of bad customer-service responses. In the email workflow, the customer complains about receiving the wrong item. A low-quality response might mention competitors, such as saying 'we're much better than our competitor CompCo' or 'Unlike RivalCo, we make returns easy.' These statements are inappropriate because they distract from the customer's issue, may violate brand guidelines, and could create legal or reputational risk. The slide demonstrates that even if the workflow completes all technical steps, the final response may still be poor. Therefore, evaluation should check not only correctness but also tone, policy compliance, professionalism, and undesired content. This example motivates automated checks for known failure modes.

Key concepts: low-quality output, customer response, competitor mention, brand safety, policy compliance, quality control

metadata.index_text: Slide 25 | Look for low-quality outputs | competitor mentions | CompCo RivalCo | customer email response
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows why agentic workflows need evaluation by giving examples of bad customer-service responses. In the email workflow, the customer complains about receiving the wrong item. A low-quality response might mention competitors, such as saying 'we're much better than our competitor CompCo' or 'Unlike RivalCo, we make returns easy.' These statements are inappropriate because they distract from the customer's issue, may violate brand guidelines, and could create legal or reputational risk. The slide demonstrates that even if the workflow completes all technical steps, the final response may still be poor. Therefore, evaluation should check not only correctness but also tone, policy compliance, professionalism, and undesired content. This example motivates automated checks for known failure modes.

text:
Add an Evaluation to Track the Error

This slide shows an objective evaluation for tracking competitor mentions in customer responses. The workflow takes a customer inquiry and LLM response, then uses code to check whether any competitor names appear in the response. A list of competitors such as CompCo, RivalCo, and The Other Co is used, and if a competitor is found, the counter num_competitor_mentions is increased. This is an example of an objective, code-based eval because the error can be detected with pattern matching. The slide teaches that not all evaluations require another LLM; some errors are easier and more reliable to check with simple code. Code-based evaluations are useful for forbidden words, required fields, length limits, formatting rules, JSON validity, citation presence, and policy-specific constraints.

Key concepts: objective eval, code-based evaluation, pattern matching, competitor detection, quality metric, automated testing

text:
Using LLM as a Judge

This slide introduces LLM-as-a-judge evaluation for outputs that are more subjective. The diagram shows an agentic research-and-writing workflow involving web search, web fetch, PDF-to-text, and essay drafting. The final essay can be passed to another LLM with a prompt such as 'Assign the following essay a quality score between 1 and 5, where 5 is the best.' The slide gives example topics such as black holes and robotic harvesting with LLM-assigned scores. This kind of evaluation is useful when quality cannot be easily measured with code, such as coherence, helpfulness, writing quality, completeness, or relevance. However, students should understand that LLM judges are not perfect. Their scores depend on the rubric, prompt wording, model quality, and possible biases, so they should be used carefully and consistently.

Key concepts: LLM-as-judge, subjective evaluation, essay scoring, quality score, rubric, agent evaluation

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces LLM-as-a-judge evaluation for outputs that are more subjective. The final essay can be passed to another LLM with a prompt such as 'Assign the following essay a quality score between 1 and 5, where 5 is the best.' The slide gives example topics such as black holes and robotic harvesting with LLM-assigned scores. This kind of evaluation is useful when quality cannot be easily measured with code, such as coherence, helpfulness, writing quality, completeness, or relevance. However, students should understand that LLM judges are not perfect. Their scores depend on the rubric, prompt wording, model quality, and possible biases, so they should be used carefully and consistently.

text:
Evaluating Agentic AI Summary

This slide summarizes key evaluation practices for agentic AI. It states that evaluation can use code for objective checks or LLM-as-judge for subjective assessments. It also distinguishes end-to-end evaluation from component-level evaluation. End-to-end evaluation asks whether the entire workflow achieved the desired result, while component-level evaluation checks individual steps such as extraction, retrieval, tool selection, or final writing. The slide also recommends examining traces to perform error analysis. A trace is the record of what the agent did: prompts, model outputs, tool calls, observations, intermediate decisions, and final response. Traces are essential because agentic systems can fail in many places. By inspecting traces, developers can identify whether the problem came from retrieval, planning, tool execution, reasoning, or final generation.

Key concepts: objective evals, subjective evals, end-to-end evaluation, component-level evaluation, traces, error analysis

It also distinguishes end-to-end evaluation from component-level evaluation. End-to-end evaluation asks whether the entire workflow achieved the desired result, while component-level evaluation checks individual steps such as extraction, retrieval, tool selection, or final writing. The slide also recommends examining traces to perform error analysis. A trace is the record of what the agent did: prompts, model outputs, tool calls, observations, intermediate decisions, and final response. Traces are essential because agentic systems can fail in many places. By inspecting traces, developers can identify whether the problem came from retrieval, planning, tool execution, reasoning, or final generation.

text:
Agentic Design Patterns

This slide introduces the section on agentic design patterns. A design pattern is a reusable solution structure for a common type of problem. In agentic AI, design patterns describe ways to organize model calls, tools, feedback, planning, and collaboration. The upcoming slides focus on reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent collaboration. This transition is important because it moves from examples and evaluation into reusable methods students can apply when building their own systems. Instead of treating each agentic application as completely new, developers can recognize patterns: let a model critique its own output, let the model call tools, let the model plan before acting, or let multiple agents debate or collaborate. These patterns are building blocks for more complex AI systems.

Key concepts: design patterns, reflection, tool use, planning, multi-agent collaboration, reusable workflow

This transition is important because it moves from examples and evaluation into reusable methods students can apply when building their own systems. Instead of treating each agentic application as completely new, developers can recognize patterns: let a model critique its own output, let the model call tools, let the model plan before acting, or let multiple agents debate or collaborate. These patterns are building blocks for more complex AI systems.

text:
Agentic Design Patterns Overview

This slide lists four core agentic design patterns: reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent collaboration. Reflection means the model or another model reviews an output and improves it. Tool use means the LLM can call external functions, APIs, databases, code interpreters, search engines, or other systems. Planning means the agent breaks a task into steps and decides what to do next. Multi-agent collaboration means multiple LLM agents interact, debate, specialize, or coordinate to solve a problem. These four patterns often appear together. For example, a research assistant may plan search steps, use web tools, draft an answer, reflect on missing evidence, and ask a critic agent to review the final response. The slide provides a compact taxonomy for understanding agentic workflows.

Key concepts: reflection, tool use, planning, multi-agent collaboration, agentic patterns, taxonomy

Planning means the agent breaks a task into steps and decides what to do next. Multi-agent collaboration means multiple LLM agents interact, debate, specialize, or coordinate to solve a problem. These four patterns often appear together.
