text:
Reflection

This slide introduces reflection using a code-writing example. A user asks a coder agent to write code for a task, producing an initial function such as def do_task(x). The output is then checked carefully for correctness, style, and efficiency, and constructive criticism is generated. The slide shows feedback such as 'There's a bug on line 5. Fix it by...' and 'It failed Unit Test 3. Try changing...' The coder can then produce improved versions such as do_task_v2 and do_task_v3. The essential idea is that the first answer is treated as a draft, not the final result. Reflection creates an iterative improvement loop where the model evaluates or receives evaluation of its own work and revises accordingly. This is especially useful for code, writing, reasoning, and structured outputs.

Key concepts: reflection, coder agent, critique, unit tests, iterative improvement, code revision

metadata.index_text: Slide 31 | Reflection | coder agent | check correctness style efficiency | bug line 5 | failed Unit Test 3

The output is then checked carefully for correctness, style, and efficiency, and constructive criticism is generated. The slide shows feedback such as 'There's a bug on line 5. Fix it by...' and 'It failed Unit Test 3. Try changing...' The coder can then produce improved versions such as do_task_v2 and do_task_v3. The essential idea is that the first answer is treated as a draft, not the final result. Reflection creates an iterative improvement loop where the model evaluates or receives evaluation of its own work and revises accordingly. This is especially useful for code, writing, reasoning, and structured outputs.

text:
Reflection With Coder and Critic Agents

This slide expands the reflection pattern by separating the coder agent and critic agent. The coder agent receives the task and produces code drafts, while the critic agent reviews the code and provides constructive feedback. The diagram shows a user request, multiple code versions, and feedback bubbles pointing out bugs or failed tests. This separation can improve quality because the critic role is specialized: instead of trying to both create and evaluate in the same step, the system gives one model or prompt the job of writing and another the job of reviewing. The critic can check correctness, style, efficiency, and test failures. The coder then uses this feedback to revise. The slide illustrates an important design principle: different LLM calls can use different roles, instructions, or even different models to improve the final output.

Key concepts: coder agent, critic agent, reflection loop, role specialization, constructive feedback, code improvement

text:
Tool Use

This slide introduces tool use by listing categories of tools that an LLM agent can call. The example code calculates compound interest using principal, interest_rate, and years, showing code execution as a tool for analysis. The slide groups tools into analysis tools such as code execution, Wolfram Alpha, and code interpreters; information-gathering tools such as web search, Wikipedia, and database access; productivity tools such as email, calendar, and messaging; and image tools such as image generation, image captioning, and OCR. The key idea is that tools extend the capabilities of the LLM beyond text prediction. Instead of guessing calculations, current facts, or database records, the agent can call the appropriate tool and use its output. Tool use is one of the most important foundations of agentic AI.

Key concepts: tool use, code execution, web search, database access, productivity tools, image tools, OCR

Instead of guessing calculations, current facts, or database records, the agent can call the appropriate tool and use its output. Tool use is one of the most important foundations of agentic AI.

text:
Planning

This slide illustrates planning with a multimodal task adapted from HuggingGPT. The user asks the system to generate an image where a girl is reading a book and has the same pose as a boy in an example image, then describe the new image with voice. The workflow requires several specialized models: an OpenPose model for pose determination, a pose-to-image model for generating the image, a vision-language or image-to-text model such as ViT-GPT2 for captioning, and a text-to-speech model such as FastSpeech for spoken output. This is a strong example of planning because no single model directly solves the whole request. The agent must decompose the request into subtasks, select the appropriate models, execute them in order, and pass outputs between steps. The final caption 'A girl is sitting on a bed reading a book' demonstrates how multimodal tool coordination can satisfy a complex user request.

Key concepts: planning, HuggingGPT, multimodal workflow, OpenPose, pose-to-image, image captioning, text-to-speech

This is a strong example of planning because no single model directly solves the whole request. The agent must decompose the request into subtasks, select the appropriate models, execute them in order, and pass outputs between steps. The final caption 'A girl is sitting on a bed reading a book' demonstrates how multimodal tool coordination can satisfy a complex user request.

text:
Multi-Agentic Workflows

This slide introduces multi-agentic workflows and shows a comparison from multiagent debate research. The visual on the left suggests a simulated collaborative environment where different agents may take different roles, such as coding, testing, designing, or documentation. The table on the right compares single-agent and multi-agent performance on tasks including biographies, MMLU, and chess moves. In the displayed results, multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems: biographies improve from 66.0% to 73.8%, MMLU from 63.9% to 71.1%, and chess moves from 29.3% to 45.2%. The lesson is that multiple agents can improve factuality and reasoning by debating, checking each other, or bringing different perspectives. However, multi-agent workflows also increase complexity, cost, and evaluation needs, so they should be used when collaboration adds measurable value.

Key concepts: multi-agent workflows, multiagent debate, MMLU, biographies, chess move, factuality, reasoning

In the displayed results, multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems: biographies improve from 66.0% to 73.8%, MMLU from 63.9% to 71.1%, and chess moves from 29.3% to 45.2%. The lesson is that multiple agents can improve factuality and reasoning by debating, checking each other, or bringing different perspectives. However, multi-agent workflows also increase complexity, cost, and evaluation needs, so they should be used when collaboration adds measurable value.

text:
Reflection Design Pattern

This slide starts a deeper section on the reflection design pattern, described as reflection to improve outputs of a task. Reflection means the system creates an initial output, reviews it against criteria or feedback, and produces an improved version. The phrase 'improve outputs' is important because reflection is not a separate goal; it is a mechanism for enhancing quality. It can be used for emails, code, charts, essays, structured outputs, reasoning tasks, and any output that benefits from review. This section will explain why reflection is useful, how humans naturally use it, how LLMs can implement it, and how to evaluate its impact. Students should understand reflection as an iterative refinement loop.

Key concepts: reflection design pattern, output improvement, iterative refinement, review, quality control

metadata.index_text: Slide 36 | Reflection Design Pattern | Reflection to improve outputs of a task
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide starts a deeper section on the reflection design pattern, described as reflection to improve outputs of a task. Reflection means the system creates an initial output, reviews it against criteria or feedback, and produces an improved version. The phrase 'improve outputs' is important because reflection is not a separate goal; it is a mechanism for enhancing quality. It can be used for emails, code, charts, essays, structured outputs, reasoning tasks, and any output that benefits from review. This section will explain why reflection is useful, how humans naturally use it, how LLMs can implement it, and how to evaluate its impact. Students should understand reflection as an iterative refinement loop.

text:
Reflection - Humans

This slide uses human email writing to make reflection intuitive. The first email draft says the writer will be in New York next month and asks Tommy to be free for dinner, but it has issues: vague timing, a typo, and no signature. The slide labels these problems as missing specific dates, typo, and not signing the name. The revised email fixes these problems by specifying the dates from the 5th to the 7th, improving wording, and signing 'Andrew.' This example shows that humans rarely produce perfect outputs on the first attempt. We draft, notice errors, revise, and improve. The slide uses this everyday process to justify LLM reflection: if humans benefit from reviewing and revising, LLM outputs can also benefit from explicit critique and improvement steps.

Key concepts: human reflection, email revision, typo correction, specific dates, draft improvement, self-editing

metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide uses human email writing to make reflection intuitive. The slide labels these problems as missing specific dates, typo, and not signing the name. The revised email fixes these problems by specifying the dates from the 5th to the 7th, improving wording, and signing 'Andrew.' This example shows that humans rarely produce perfect outputs on the first attempt. We draft, notice errors, revise, and improve. The slide uses this everyday process to justify LLM reflection: if humans benefit from reviewing and revising, LLM outputs can also benefit from explicit critique and improvement steps.

text:
Reflection to Improve Code

This slide applies reflection to code generation. The workflow begins with an LLM asked to write code for task X. It produces a first draft, such as def do_task(args). A second LLM call, possibly a different LLM such as a stronger reasoning model, checks for bugs and writes an improved second draft. The output becomes def do_task_v2(args). This process separates generation from review: the first step creates a candidate solution, and the second step inspects it and improves it. The slide also notes that the reflection model can be different from the original model, which is useful because a cheaper model may draft while a stronger model critiques. Reflection is particularly valuable for code because code has objective properties such as syntax, test correctness, edge cases, and efficiency.

Key concepts: code reflection, first draft, second draft, reasoning model, bug checking, LLM review

metadata.index_text: Slide 38 | Reflection to improve code | write first draft | check bugs | improved second draft | different LLM
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide applies reflection to code generation. The workflow begins with an LLM asked to write code for task X. It produces a first draft, such as def do_task(args). A second LLM call, possibly a different LLM such as a stronger reasoning model, checks for bugs and writes an improved second draft. The output becomes def do_task_v2(args). This process separates generation from review: the first step creates a candidate solution, and the second step inspects it and improves it. The slide also notes that the reflection model can be different from the original model, which is useful because a cheaper model may draft while a stronger model critiques. Reflection is particularly valuable for code because code has objective properties such as syntax, test correctness, edge cases, and efficiency.

text:
Reflection With External Feedback

This slide adds external feedback to the reflection loop. The LLM writes a first code draft, then the code is executed. If execution produces an error, such as a SyntaxError for an unterminated string literal, the error output is passed back to the LLM. The LLM then reflects on the code output or error and writes an improved second draft. This is more powerful than self-reflection alone because the feedback comes from an external tool that can objectively detect failure. Execution feedback can reveal syntax errors, runtime errors, failing tests, wrong outputs, or performance issues. The slide teaches a key agentic pattern: use tools not only to perform tasks, but also to generate feedback that helps the model improve its next attempt.

Key concepts: external feedback, code execution, SyntaxError, reflection loop, tool feedback, debugging

metadata.index_text: Slide 39 | Reflection with external feedback | execute code | code output errors | SyntaxError unterminated string literal

If execution produces an error, such as a SyntaxError for an unterminated string literal, the error output is passed back to the LLM. The LLM then reflects on the code output or error and writes an improved second draft. This is more powerful than self-reflection alone because the feedback comes from an external tool that can objectively detect failure. Execution feedback can reveal syntax errors, runtime errors, failing tests, wrong outputs, or performance issues. The slide teaches a key agentic pattern: use tools not only to perform tasks, but also to generate feedback that helps the model improve its next attempt.

text:
Why Not Just Direct Generation?

This slide introduces a question that motivates reflection: why not simply ask the model to generate the final answer directly? The question is important because direct generation is simpler, cheaper, and faster. However, as the upcoming slides show, direct generation often fails for tasks requiring accuracy, completeness, formatting, reasoning, or high-quality writing. Reflection adds additional steps, but those steps can catch omissions, improve structure, correct errors, and apply criteria that were not fully satisfied in the first draft. This slide prepares students to compare direct prompting with reflective workflows and to understand when the additional cost of reflection is justified.

Key concepts: direct generation, reflection motivation, quality improvement, tradeoff, prompting strategy

However, as the upcoming slides show, direct generation often fails for tasks requiring accuracy, completeness, formatting, reasoning, or high-quality writing. Reflection adds additional steps, but those steps can catch omissions, improve structure, correct errors, and apply criteria that were not fully satisfied in the first draft. This slide prepares students to compare direct prompting with reflective workflows and to understand when the additional cost of reflection is justified.

text:
Direct Generation

This slide gives examples of direct generation: asking an LLM to write an essay about black holes or write a Python function to calculate compound interest annually. In direct generation, the LLM receives a prompt and immediately produces the final output. The code example returns principal * (1 + rate) ** time, which is the standard compound interest formula when time is measured in years and rate is expressed as a decimal. The essay example begins with a general statement about black holes. Direct generation is useful for simple tasks and quick drafts, but it provides no explicit review step. If the generated code has a hidden bug, the explanation is incomplete, or the essay lacks evidence, the system does not automatically detect or fix the issue. This is the limitation reflection is meant to address.

Key concepts: direct generation, compound interest, essay generation, Python function, single-step prompting, draft quality

metadata.index_text: Slide 41 | Direct generation | essay about black holes | python compound interest function | one-step output
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide gives examples of direct generation: asking an LLM to write an essay about black holes or write a Python function to calculate compound interest annually. In direct generation, the LLM receives a prompt and immediately produces the final output. The code example returns principal * (1 + rate) ** time, which is the standard compound interest formula when time is measured in years and rate is expressed as a decimal. The essay example begins with a general statement about black holes. Direct generation is useful for simple tasks and quick drafts, but it provides no explicit review step. If the generated code has a hidden bug, the explanation is incomplete, or the essay lacks evidence, the system does not automatically detect or fix the issue. This is the limitation reflection is meant to address.

text:
Zero, One, and Few-Shot Prompting

This slide reviews prompting styles using date conversion to MM/DD/YYYY format. A zero-shot prompt gives only the instruction and the input date, with no examples. A one-shot prompt includes one example, such as converting Jan 1st, 2025 to 01/01/2025, before asking the model to convert another date. A two-shot or few-shot prompt includes multiple examples, such as also converting 21st June, 2025 to 06/21/2025. The educational point is that examples guide the model by showing the desired input-output pattern. However, few-shot prompting is still direct generation because the model produces the final answer in one pass after reading the examples. Reflection differs because it adds review and revision after the first output has been produced.

Key concepts: zero-shot prompting, one-shot prompting, few-shot prompting, date conversion, examples, prompt design

metadata.index_text: Slide 42 | Zero one few-shot prompting | Convert to MM/DD/YYYY | Jan 1st 2025 | 21st June 2025
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide reviews prompting styles using date conversion to MM/DD/YYYY format. A zero-shot prompt gives only the instruction and the input date, with no examples. A one-shot prompt includes one example, such as converting Jan 1st, 2025 to 01/01/2025, before asking the model to convert another date. A two-shot or few-shot prompt includes multiple examples, such as also converting 21st June, 2025 to 06/21/2025. The educational point is that examples guide the model by showing the desired input-output pattern. However, few-shot prompting is still direct generation because the model produces the final answer in one pass after reading the examples. Reflection differs because it adds review and revision after the first output has been produced.

text:
Direct Generation as Zero-Shot Prompting

This slide connects direct generation to zero-shot prompts. The examples ask the model to write an essay about black holes or write a Python function for compound interest without providing demonstrations, intermediate steps, or feedback. The slide labels these as zero-shot prompts because the model is expected to infer the task from the instruction alone. Zero-shot prompting can be effective when the task is familiar and simple, but it is weaker when the task requires careful checking, exact constraints, or domain-specific expectations. This slide helps students see that direct generation is the simplest form of LLM use, while agentic workflows add structure around it. Reflection can be viewed as an upgrade from zero-shot direct generation because it adds explicit self-checking or tool-based checking.

Key concepts: zero-shot prompts, direct generation, compound interest, essay writing, reflection contrast, prompt simplicity

text:
Reflection Has Been Tested

This slide provides empirical evidence that reflection can improve performance. The chart, adapted from the Self-Refine paper, compares direct generation and self-refinement across tasks such as sentiment reversal, dialogue response, code optimization, code readability, math reasoning, acronym generation, and constrained generation. The main message on the slide is that reflection consistently outperforms direct generation on a variety of tasks. The chart shows performance gains for different models and tasks, demonstrating that iterative feedback is not only an intuitive idea but also experimentally supported. The reason reflection helps is that a model's first output often contains correct partial information but may miss constraints, make small errors, or choose a weaker structure. A critique-and-revise loop can target those weaknesses and improve the final answer.

Key concepts: Self-Refine, reflection evaluation, performance improvement, iterative refinement, math reasoning, code readability

The main message on the slide is that reflection consistently outperforms direct generation on a variety of tasks. The chart shows performance gains for different models and tasks, demonstrating that iterative feedback is not only an intuitive idea but also experimentally supported. The reason reflection helps is that a model's first output often contains correct partial information but may miss constraints, make small errors, or choose a weaker structure. A critique-and-revise loop can target those weaknesses and improve the final answer.

text:
Tasks Where Reflection Works Better

This slide lists examples of tasks where reflection is especially useful. For HTML table generation, reflection can validate the HTML code and catch errors such as a missing greater-than symbol. For instructions like brewing a perfect cup of tea, reflection can check whether steps are missing and whether the instructions are coherent and complete. For generating domain names, reflection can evaluate whether a name has unintended meanings, negative connotations, or pronunciation difficulties. The key pattern is that reflection works well when there are clear criteria for judging the output. If the model can inspect the output against those criteria, or if a tool can provide feedback, the second draft can improve. The slide teaches students to ask what can be checked after the first output is generated, because those checks become the basis of the reflection prompt.

Key concepts: reflection use cases, HTML validation, instruction completeness, domain names, negative connotations, output criteria

For instructions like brewing a perfect cup of tea, reflection can check whether steps are missing and whether the instructions are coherent and complete. For generating domain names, reflection can evaluate whether a name has unintended meanings, negative connotations, or pronunciation difficulties. The key pattern is that reflection works well when there are clear criteria for judging the output. If the model can inspect the output against those criteria, or if a tool can provide feedback, the second draft can improve. The slide teaches students to ask what can be checked after the first output is generated, because those checks become the basis of the reflection prompt.

text:
Tips for Writing Reflection Prompts

This slide gives practical guidance for writing reflection prompts. The first example asks the model to review domain names, check whether each name is easy to pronounce, consider whether it may have negative meanings in other languages, and output only the names that meet the criteria. The second example asks the model to review an email draft, check whether the tone is professional, identify rude or insensitive phrases, verify facts, dates, and promises, and then write the next draft. The slide highlights two principles: clearly indicate the reflection action and specify the criteria to check. A weak reflection prompt such as 'make it better' is vague, while a strong reflection prompt tells the model what quality dimensions matter. This makes the revision more targeted, consistent, and evaluable.

Key concepts: reflection prompts, criteria, professional tone, fact verification, domain names, revision instruction

The second example asks the model to review an email draft, check whether the tone is professional, identify rude or insensitive phrases, verify facts, dates, and promises, and then write the next draft. The slide highlights two principles: clearly indicate the reflection action and specify the criteria to check. A weak reflection prompt such as 'make it better' is vague, while a strong reflection prompt tells the model what quality dimensions matter. This makes the revision more targeted, consistent, and evaluable.

text:
Visualizing Coffee Sales

This slide introduces a data visualization task: create a plot comparing Q1 coffee sales in 2024 and 2025. The sample data table contains columns such as date, price, and coffee_name, with examples including Latte, Hot Chocolate, and Cappuccino across dates in January, February, March, and later March 2025. The task is not simply to write text; it requires reading tabular data, filtering for Q1 months, grouping or aggregating sales, comparing years, and producing a plot. This makes it a strong example for an agentic workflow because the LLM must write code, execute it, inspect the resulting chart, and possibly revise the code if the visualization is unclear. The slide sets up the next workflow where reflection is applied to chart generation.

Key concepts: data visualization, coffee sales, Q1 comparison, tabular data, Python plotting, agentic workflow

The task is not simply to write text; it requires reading tabular data, filtering for Q1 months, grouping or aggregating sales, comparing years, and producing a plot. This makes it a strong example for an agentic workflow because the LLM must write code, execute it, inspect the resulting chart, and possibly revise the code if the visualization is unclear. The slide sets up the next workflow where reflection is applied to chart generation.

text:
Chart Generation Agentic Workflow

This slide shows a full agentic workflow for generating a chart. The user asks for a plot comparing Q1 coffee sales in 2024 and 2025 using coffee_sales.csv. The LLM first writes Python code, which is executed to produce V1 code and plot.png. Then another LLM step critiques the image and updates the code, producing V2 code. The new code is executed to create plot_v2.png. The diagram shows that the first chart is less clear, while the revised chart is more readable and better structured. The workflow demonstrates reflection with external artifacts: the model does not only read its code, it also sees the generated plot and critiques the visualization. This is a practical example of using execution plus visual feedback to improve an output that would be difficult to perfect in a single prompt.

Key concepts: chart generation, Python code, execute code, visual reflection, plot critique, iterative visualization

metadata.index_text: Slide 48 | Chart generation agentic workflow | coffee_sales.csv | V1 code plot.png | critique image | V2 code plot_v2.png
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows a full agentic workflow for generating a chart. The user asks for a plot comparing Q1 coffee sales in 2024 and 2025 using coffee_sales.csv. The LLM first writes Python code, which is executed to produce V1 code and plot.png. Then another LLM step critiques the image and updates the code, producing V2 code. The new code is executed to create plot_v2.png. The diagram shows that the first chart is less clear, while the revised chart is more readable and better structured. The workflow demonstrates reflection with external artifacts: the model does not only read its code, it also sees the generated plot and critiques the visualization. This is a practical example of using execution plus visual feedback to improve an output that would be difficult to perfect in a single prompt.

text:
Reflection With a Different LLM

This slide shows that reflection can be performed by a different LLM from the one that generated the original output. The first prompt asks an LLM to write Python code to generate a visualization that answers the user's question. The second prompt frames another model as an expert data analyst who provides constructive feedback on visualizations. It receives the V1 code, the generated plot image, and conversation history, then follows two steps: critique the chart for readability, clarity, and completeness, and write new code to implement improvements. This separation lets the system use specialized roles: one model call focuses on code generation, while another focuses on visualization critique. In real systems, the reflection model could be stronger, more expensive, multimodal, or simply prompted differently to behave like a reviewer.

Key concepts: different LLM, code generation, reflection model, data analyst, visualization critique, role specialization

metadata.index_text: Slide 49 | Reflection with a different LLM | expert data analyst | critique chart | write new code
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows that reflection can be performed by a different LLM from the one that generated the original output. The first prompt asks an LLM to write Python code to generate a visualization that answers the user's question. The second prompt frames another model as an expert data analyst who provides constructive feedback on visualizations. It receives the V1 code, the generated plot image, and conversation history, then follows two steps: critique the chart for readability, clarity, and completeness, and write new code to implement improvements. This separation lets the system use specialized roles: one model call focuses on code generation, while another focuses on visualization critique. In real systems, the reflection model could be stronger, more expensive, multimodal, or simply prompted differently to behave like a reviewer.

text:
Evaluating the Impact of Reflection

This slide begins a section on measuring whether reflection actually improves performance. Reflection adds cost and complexity, so developers should not assume it helps in every case. The purpose of evaluation is to compare outputs with and without reflection using a dataset of prompts and expected answers or quality criteria. The upcoming examples show objective tasks, such as generating SQL queries and comparing answers to ground truth, and subjective tasks, such as judging whether a chart is clearer after revision. This slide reminds students that agentic design should be evaluation-driven. A reflection loop is useful only if it measurably improves accuracy, quality, safety, or user satisfaction compared with direct generation.

Key concepts: reflection evaluation, impact measurement, baseline comparison, with reflection, without reflection, agentic evaluation

metadata.index_text: Slide 50 | Reflection Design Pattern | Evaluating the impact of reflection | compare with and without reflection
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide begins a section on measuring whether reflection actually improves performance. Reflection adds cost and complexity, so developers should not assume it helps in every case. The purpose of evaluation is to compare outputs with and without reflection using a dataset of prompts and expected answers or quality criteria. The upcoming examples show objective tasks, such as generating SQL queries and comparing answers to ground truth, and subjective tasks, such as judging whether a chart is clearer after revision. This slide reminds students that agentic design should be evaluation-driven. A reflection loop is useful only if it measurably improves accuracy, quality, safety, or user satisfaction compared with direct generation.

text:
Create a Dataset of Prompts and Answers

This slide explains how to evaluate reflection on objective tasks using a dataset of prompts and ground-truth answers. The example contains questions such as number of items sold in May 2025, most expensive item, and how many styles are carried. The workflow asks the LLM to generate a SQL query, reflect on the first SQL query to write a second version, execute the SQL, and then answer the question from the query results. The table compares no-reflection and with-reflection performance, showing improvement from 87% correct to 95% correct in the example. The key lesson is that objective tasks can be evaluated systematically: build a benchmark set, run the workflow each time the reflection prompt changes, execute outputs, and compare final answers to known ground truth. This makes prompt and workflow engineering measurable rather than subjective.

Key concepts: objective evaluation, ground truth, SQL generation, reflection prompt, accuracy, benchmark dataset

The workflow asks the LLM to generate a SQL query, reflect on the first SQL query to write a second version, execute the SQL, and then answer the question from the query results. The table compares no-reflection and with-reflection performance, showing improvement from 87% correct to 95% correct in the example. The key lesson is that objective tasks can be evaluated systematically: build a benchmark set, run the workflow each time the reflection prompt changes, execute outputs, and compare final answers to known ground truth. This makes prompt and workflow engineering measurable rather than subjective.

text:
What About Subjective Tasks?

This slide asks how to evaluate subjective tasks such as chart quality. The workflow is the same chart-generation loop: write Python code, execute it, produce a first plot, critique the image, update the code, and execute again to create a revised plot. The slide asks whether we can measure which chart is better with an eval. This is harder than SQL correctness because there may not be a single ground-truth answer. A chart can be judged by readability, clarity, appropriate chart type, label quality, color use, axis scaling, and whether it answers the user's question. The slide prepares students for rubric-based LLM evaluation, where subjective quality is broken into smaller criteria that can be scored more consistently.

Key concepts: subjective evaluation, chart quality, visualization, rubric, LLM judge, before and after reflection

metadata.index_text: Slide 52 | What about subjective tasks | chart generation workflow | before reflection after reflection | measure chart quality
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide asks how to evaluate subjective tasks such as chart quality. The workflow is the same chart-generation loop: write Python code, execute it, produce a first plot, critique the image, update the code, and execute again to create a revised plot. The slide asks whether we can measure which chart is better with an eval. This is harder than SQL correctness because there may not be a single ground-truth answer. A chart can be judged by readability, clarity, appropriate chart type, label quality, color use, axis scaling, and whether it answers the user's question. The slide prepares students for rubric-based LLM evaluation, where subjective quality is broken into smaller criteria that can be scored more consistently.

text:
Using an LLM as a Judge

This slide shows a simple approach to judging subjective outputs: ask an LLM which image is better, given plot.png and plot_v2.png. The slide also warns about known issues. LLMs may not always provide high-quality comparisons, and they can suffer from position bias, meaning they may prefer the first or second option based on order rather than true quality. The visual note that the LLM picks A more often illustrates this bias. This slide is important because it prevents students from treating LLM-as-judge as automatically reliable. Direct pairwise comparison can be useful, but it should be controlled carefully, perhaps by randomizing order, using multiple judges, or replacing broad comparison questions with rubric-based grading.

Key concepts: LLM-as-judge, image comparison, position bias, subjective scoring, plot evaluation, evaluation reliability

metadata.slide_title: Using an LLM as a Judge
metadata.index_text: Slide 53 | Using an LLM as a judge | Which image is better | plot.png plot_v2.png | position bias
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows a simple approach to judging subjective outputs: ask an LLM which image is better, given plot.png and plot_v2.png. The slide also warns about known issues. LLMs may not always provide high-quality comparisons, and they can suffer from position bias, meaning they may prefer the first or second option based on order rather than true quality. The visual note that the LLM picks A more often illustrates this bias. This slide is important because it prevents students from treating LLM-as-judge as automatically reliable. Direct pairwise comparison can be useful, but it should be controlled carefully, perhaps by randomizing order, using multiple judges, or replacing broad comparison questions with rubric-based grading.

text:
Grading With a Rubric

This slide explains that rubric-based grading gives more consistent subjective evaluations. Instead of asking the LLM which chart is better, the prompt asks it to assess an attached image against a quality rubric, assigning each item a score of 1 for true or 0 for false and returning the scores as JSON. Example rubric criteria include clear title, axis labels present, appropriate chart type, and axes using an appropriate numerical range. The table compares no-reflection and with-reflection scores for multiple user queries, showing that the rubric can be run repeatedly when changing the reflection prompt. The key idea is to decompose subjective quality into specific checkable criteria. This makes LLM judging more structured, easier to compare across experiments, and more useful for prompt improvement.

Key concepts: rubric grading, structured evaluation, JSON scores, chart rubric, subjective eval, reflection prompt testing

metadata.index_text: Slide 54 | Grading with a rubric | clear title | axis labels | appropriate chart type | JSON object scores
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide explains that rubric-based grading gives more consistent subjective evaluations. Instead of asking the LLM which chart is better, the prompt asks it to assess an attached image against a quality rubric, assigning each item a score of 1 for true or 0 for false and returning the scores as JSON. Example rubric criteria include clear title, axis labels present, appropriate chart type, and axes using an appropriate numerical range. The table compares no-reflection and with-reflection scores for multiple user queries, showing that the rubric can be run repeatedly when changing the reflection prompt. The key idea is to decompose subjective quality into specific checkable criteria. This makes LLM judging more structured, easier to compare across experiments, and more useful for prompt improvement.

text:
Evaluating Reflection

This slide summarizes how to evaluate reflection. For objective evaluations, code-based checks are easier and should be used when possible. Developers should build a dataset of ground-truth examples so they can measure accuracy before and after reflection. For subjective evaluations, an LLM can be used as a judge, but rubric-based grading is better than vague comparison because it gives clearer criteria and more consistent results. The slide reinforces a practical engineering principle: reflection should be tested, not assumed. If reflection improves results, the evaluation should show it. If it does not, the workflow may need better prompts, better criteria, stronger feedback, or fewer unnecessary steps.

Key concepts: reflection evaluation, objective evals, subjective evals, code-based evals, ground truth, rubric-based grading

metadata.index_text: Slide 55 | Evaluating reflection | objective evals | subjective evals | LLM as judge | rubric-based grading
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes how to evaluate reflection. For objective evaluations, code-based checks are easier and should be used when possible. Developers should build a dataset of ground-truth examples so they can measure accuracy before and after reflection. For subjective evaluations, an LLM can be used as a judge, but rubric-based grading is better than vague comparison because it gives clearer criteria and more consistent results. The slide reinforces a practical engineering principle: reflection should be tested, not assumed. If reflection improves results, the evaluation should show it. If it does not, the workflow may need better prompts, better criteria, stronger feedback, or fewer unnecessary steps.

text:
Using External Feedback

This slide introduces the next part of the reflection design pattern: using external feedback. External feedback means the system does not rely only on the model's own judgment; it uses signals from tools, tests, execution results, search results, validators, or other systems. This is important because self-reflection may miss errors that an external check can catch. For example, a model may believe code is correct, but running the code can reveal a syntax error. A model may think a fact is true, but a web search can show it is false. This section will show how external feedback can make reflection more effective and more reliable by grounding the revision step in observable results.

Key concepts: external feedback, tool feedback, validation, reflection reliability, execution results, fact checking

metadata.index_text: Slide 56 | Reflection Design Pattern | Using external feedback | tools tests validators
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the next part of the reflection design pattern: using external feedback. External feedback means the system does not rely only on the model's own judgment; it uses signals from tools, tests, execution results, search results, validators, or other systems. This is important because self-reflection may miss errors that an external check can catch. For example, a model may believe code is correct, but running the code can reveal a syntax error. A model may think a fact is true, but a web search can show it is false. This section will show how external feedback can make reflection more effective and more reliable by grounding the revision step in observable results.

text:
Return on Investment on Prompt Engineering

This slide presents a conceptual graph of performance versus time spent on prompt engineering. The graph compares no reflection, reflection, and reflection with external feedback. The idea is that direct prompting improves slowly or reaches a lower performance ceiling, while reflection can achieve better performance, and reflection with external feedback can provide even higher returns. External feedback gives the model concrete information about what went wrong, reducing the amount of manual prompt tuning needed to discover failures. This slide teaches a practical lesson: agentic patterns can increase the return on prompt engineering because the workflow itself helps detect and correct errors. Rather than endlessly refining one prompt, developers can design a loop that generates, checks, and revises outputs.

Key concepts: prompt engineering, return on investment, external feedback, performance, reflection, workflow optimization

metadata.slide_title: Return on Investment on Prompt Engineering

The idea is that direct prompting improves slowly or reaches a lower performance ceiling, while reflection can achieve better performance, and reflection with external feedback can provide even higher returns. External feedback gives the model concrete information about what went wrong, reducing the amount of manual prompt tuning needed to discover failures. This slide teaches a practical lesson: agentic patterns can increase the return on prompt engineering because the workflow itself helps detect and correct errors. Rather than endlessly refining one prompt, developers can design a loop that generates, checks, and revises outputs.

text:
Reflection With External Feedback

This slide repeats the code-execution reflection workflow to emphasize the role of external feedback. The LLM writes a first draft of code, the code is executed, and the resulting output or error message is given back to the LLM. The shown error is a SyntaxError for an unterminated string literal, which gives a precise signal about where the code failed. The LLM then reflects on that error and writes an improved second draft. The slide reinforces that reflection is strongest when the model receives specific feedback rather than vague instructions. External feedback can come from compilers, unit tests, linters, validators, search engines, calculators, or databases, depending on the task.

Key concepts: code execution, error feedback, SyntaxError, reflection loop, debugging, external validator

metadata.index_text: Slide 58 | Reflection with external feedback | execute code | code output errors | improved second draft
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide repeats the code-execution reflection workflow to emphasize the role of external feedback. The LLM writes a first draft of code, the code is executed, and the resulting output or error message is given back to the LLM. The shown error is a SyntaxError for an unterminated string literal, which gives a precise signal about where the code failed. The LLM then reflects on that error and writes an improved second draft. The slide reinforces that reflection is strongest when the model receives specific feedback rather than vague instructions. External feedback can come from compilers, unit tests, linters, validators, search engines, calculators, or databases, depending on the task.

text:
Other Examples of Tools to Help Reflection

This slide lists additional sources of external feedback for reflection. If the problem is competitor mentions, the system can use pattern matching to detect competitor names such as RivalCo. If the problem is fact checking, such as whether the Taj Mahal was built in 1648, the system can use web search results as evidence. If the LLM fails to follow output length guidelines, a word count tool can measure whether the essay is over the limit. These examples show that reflection can be assisted by simple specialized tools, not only complex AI models. The best feedback tool depends on the failure mode: pattern matching for forbidden terms, retrieval for facts, word count for length, code execution for programs, and schema validation for structured output.

Key concepts: tool-assisted reflection, pattern matching, fact checking, web search, word count, failure modes

If the problem is fact checking, such as whether the Taj Mahal was built in 1648, the system can use web search results as evidence. If the LLM fails to follow output length guidelines, a word count tool can measure whether the essay is over the limit. These examples show that reflection can be assisted by simple specialized tools, not only complex AI models. The best feedback tool depends on the failure mode: pattern matching for forbidden terms, retrieval for facts, word count for length, code execution for programs, and schema validation for structured output.

text:
Tool Use: What Are Tools?

This slide begins the tool-use section by asking what tools are. In agentic AI, tools are external functions, APIs, programs, databases, or services that the LLM can request to use. Tools allow the model to access capabilities it does not have internally, such as current time, web search, database queries, calculations, code execution, email sending, calendar scheduling, OCR, or image generation. This section will explain how tool execution works, how the model decides when to use a tool, and how tools can be described to the model. Understanding tools is essential because tool use is what turns an LLM from a text generator into an application component that can act on the world or retrieve reliable information.

Key concepts: tool use, external functions, APIs, code execution, databases, agentic AI

metadata.index_text: Slide 60 | Tool Use | What are tools | functions APIs external systems
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide begins the tool-use section by asking what tools are. In agentic AI, tools are external functions, APIs, programs, databases, or services that the LLM can request to use. Tools allow the model to access capabilities it does not have internally, such as current time, web search, database queries, calculations, code execution, email sending, calendar scheduling, OCR, or image generation. This section will explain how tool execution works, how the model decides when to use a tool, and how tools can be described to the model. Understanding tools is essential because tool use is what turns an LLM from a text generator into an application component that can act on the world or retrieve reliable information.

text:
Simple Tool Execution

This slide illustrates a simple tool-execution problem using the question 'What time is it?' Without tool access, the LLM says it does not have access to the current time, because the current time changes continuously and is not stored in the model parameters. With a get_current_time function, the system can execute code using Python's datetime module and return the current time as a string. The contrast shows the difference between model knowledge and tool capability. The LLM should not guess the current time; it should call a tool that can compute or retrieve it. This example is deliberately simple, but it captures the general pattern: when the answer depends on live data, calculation, private data, or external state, the agent should use a tool instead of relying on language generation.

Key concepts: simple tool execution, current time, datetime, function call, live data, tool capability

metadata.index_text: Slide 61 | Simple tool execution | What time is it | get_current_time function | datetime now
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide illustrates a simple tool-execution problem using the question 'What time is it?' Without tool access, the LLM says it does not have access to the current time, because the current time changes continuously and is not stored in the model parameters. With a get_current_time function, the system can execute code using Python's datetime module and return the current time as a string. The contrast shows the difference between model knowledge and tool capability. The LLM should not guess the current time; it should call a tool that can compute or retrieve it. This example is deliberately simple, but it captures the general pattern: when the answer depends on live data, calculation, private data, or external state, the agent should use a tool instead of relying on language generation.

text:
LLMs Can Choose Tools When Appropriate

This slide shows that an LLM should use tools selectively. When the user asks 'What time is it?', the LLM chooses the get_current_time tool, receives the output such as 15:20:45, and then responds in natural language. When the user asks how much caffeine is in green tea, the model does not need the time tool and can answer from general knowledge. The lesson is that tool availability does not mean every prompt requires a tool call. The agent must decide whether a tool is relevant to the user's request. Good tool-using systems therefore require clear tool descriptions, appropriate selection logic, and safeguards against unnecessary or incorrect tool calls. The slide demonstrates the decision-making aspect of agentic tool use.

Key concepts: tool selection, get_current_time, relevance, natural language response, selective tool use, agent decision

metadata.slide_title: LLMs Can Choose Tools When Appropriate
metadata.index_text: Slide 62 | LLMs choose tools | What time is it | green tea caffeine | get_current_time only when appropriate
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows that an LLM should use tools selectively. When the user asks 'What time is it?', the LLM chooses the get_current_time tool, receives the output such as 15:20:45, and then responds in natural language. When the user asks how much caffeine is in green tea, the model does not need the time tool and can answer from general knowledge. The lesson is that tool availability does not mean every prompt requires a tool call. The agent must decide whether a tool is relevant to the user's request. Good tool-using systems therefore require clear tool descriptions, appropriate selection logic, and safeguards against unnecessary or incorrect tool calls. The slide demonstrates the decision-making aspect of agentic tool use.

text:
Tool Use Examples

This slide gives three examples of prompts, tools, and outputs. For finding Italian restaurants near Mountain View, California, the system can call a web_search tool with a location-specific query and return restaurant results. For calculating compound interest on a $500 deposit at 5% for 10 years, the system can call an interest_calc tool or execute the expression 500 * (1 + 0.05) ** 10, producing $814.45. For finding customers who bought white sunglasses, the system can query a sales database with product and color filters. These examples show that tools can support information retrieval, mathematical calculation, and database lookup. The LLM's role is to interpret the user's request, choose the right tool, pass correct parameters, and explain the result clearly.

Key concepts: web search, interest calculator, database query, tool parameters, restaurant search, compound interest

metadata.index_text: Slide 63 | Examples | restaurants near Mountain View | interest_calc | query_database sales sunglasses white | $814.45
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide gives three examples of prompts, tools, and outputs. For finding Italian restaurants near Mountain View, California, the system can call a web_search tool with a location-specific query and return restaurant results. For calculating compound interest on a $500 deposit at 5% for 10 years, the system can call an interest_calc tool or execute the expression 500 * (1 + 0.05) ** 10, producing $814.45. For finding customers who bought white sunglasses, the system can query a sales database with product and color filters. These examples show that tools can support information retrieval, mathematical calculation, and database lookup. The LLM's role is to interpret the user's request, choose the right tool, pass correct parameters, and explain the result clearly.

text:
Multiple Tools

This slide shows a multi-tool workflow for scheduling an appointment with Alice. The user asks the system to find a free slot on Thursday and make an appointment. The LLM has access to tools such as check_calendar, make_appointment, and delete_appointment. The workflow first checks the calendar and receives available times such as Thursday at 3 PM, 4 PM, and 6 PM. Then it uses make_appointment to create the meeting, receives a success output, and finally tells the user that the appointment is set up at 3 PM Thursday. This example demonstrates that tools can be chained: one tool's output becomes input to the next decision. It also shows why agentic systems need careful action validation, because calendar changes affect the user's real world.

Key concepts: multiple tools, calendar, make appointment, check calendar, tool chaining, action validation

metadata.index_text: Slide 64 | Multiple tools | check_calendar | make_appointment | delete_appointment | appointment with Alice Thursday
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows a multi-tool workflow for scheduling an appointment with Alice. The user asks the system to find a free slot on Thursday and make an appointment. The LLM has access to tools such as check_calendar, make_appointment, and delete_appointment. The workflow first checks the calendar and receives available times such as Thursday at 3 PM, 4 PM, and 6 PM. Then it uses make_appointment to create the meeting, receives a success output, and finally tells the user that the appointment is set up at 3 PM Thursday. This example demonstrates that tools can be chained: one tool's output becomes input to the next decision. It also shows why agentic systems need careful action validation, because calendar changes affect the user's real world.

text:
Your Code as a Tool

This slide states that tools are just code the LLM can request to be executed. The example function get_current_time uses datetime.now().strftime to return the current time as a string. This is a simple but important point: developers can expose their own functions as tools, allowing the LLM to call controlled pieces of application logic. A tool does not have to be a large external service; it can be a small function that performs a calculation, queries a database, formats data, validates an input, sends an email, or checks policy rules. The developer defines what the tool can do, what parameters it accepts, and what output it returns. This gives the system controlled access to capabilities while keeping the LLM itself focused on deciding and communicating.

Key concepts: custom tools, Python function, get_current_time, controlled execution, developer-defined tools, function interface

metadata.index_text: Slide 65 | Your code as a tool | get_current_time | tools are code LLM can request to execute
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide states that tools are just code the LLM can request to be executed. The example function get_current_time uses datetime.now().strftime to return the current time as a string. This is a simple but important point: developers can expose their own functions as tools, allowing the LLM to call controlled pieces of application logic. A tool does not have to be a large external service; it can be a small function that performs a calculation, queries a database, formats data, validates an input, sends an email, or checks policy rules. The developer defines what the tool can do, what parameters it accepts, and what output it returns. This gives the system controlled access to capabilities while keeping the LLM itself focused on deciding and communicating.

text:
Prompting an LLM to Use Tools

This slide shows one way to prompt an LLM to call a tool using a specific output format. The system prompt tells the model that it has access to get_current_time and must return exactly 'FUNCTION: get_current_time()' to use it. The application then checks whether 'FUNCTION' appears in the model output. If it does, the tool is executed, producing a time such as 08:00:00. The LLM then receives the tool result and responds to the user, for example 'It's 8am.' This slide explains the mechanics behind simple function calling before more formal tool APIs. The main idea is that the model outputs a structured request, the application executes the function, and the result is fed back to the model for final response generation.

Key concepts: tool prompting, function call, system prompt, get_current_time, tool result, structured output

metadata.slide_title: Prompting an LLM to Use Tools
metadata.index_text: Slide 66 | Prompting an LLM to use tools | FUNCTION get_current_time | if FUNCTION in output | 08:00:00
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows one way to prompt an LLM to call a tool using a specific output format. The system prompt tells the model that it has access to get_current_time and must return exactly 'FUNCTION: get_current_time()' to use it. The application then checks whether 'FUNCTION' appears in the model output. If it does, the tool is executed, producing a time such as 08:00:00. The LLM then receives the tool result and responds to the user, for example 'It's 8am.' This slide explains the mechanics behind simple function calling before more formal tool APIs. The main idea is that the model outputs a structured request, the application executes the function, and the result is fed back to the model for final response generation.

text:
Prompting an LLM to Use Tools With Parameters

This slide extends tool calling to a function with parameters. The user asks what time it is in New Zealand, and the system prompt tells the model that get_current_time requires a timezone argument written as get_current_time('timezone'). The LLM returns a structured function call such as get_current_time('Pacific/Auckland'). The tool uses Python's ZoneInfo to compute the current time in that timezone and returns a result such as 04:00:00. The final response is then written in natural language. This example shows why tool schemas need parameter descriptions: the model must know what arguments are valid and how to format them. It also shows that tools can convert vague user language, like 'New Zealand', into formal API parameters, such as an IANA timezone string.

Key concepts: tool parameters, timezone, Pacific/Auckland, ZoneInfo, function schema, structured tool call

metadata.slide_title: Prompting an LLM to Use Tools With Parameters
metadata.index_text: Slide 67 | Prompting LLM to use tools | get_current_time timezone | Pacific/Auckland | New Zealand time
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide extends tool calling to a function with parameters. The user asks what time it is in New Zealand, and the system prompt tells the model that get_current_time requires a timezone argument written as get_current_time('timezone'). The LLM returns a structured function call such as get_current_time('Pacific/Auckland'). The tool uses Python's ZoneInfo to compute the current time in that timezone and returns a result such as 04:00:00. The final response is then written in natural language. This example shows why tool schemas need parameter descriptions: the model must know what arguments are valid and how to format them. It also shows that tools can convert vague user language, like 'New Zealand', into formal API parameters, such as an IANA timezone string.

text:
Defining Tool Syntax

This slide shows how tools can be passed to an LLM client in code. The Python function get_current_time contains a docstring describing what it returns. In the aisuite example, the function is included in the tools list when calling client.chat.completions.create with model openai:gpt-4o, messages, and max_turns. The slide notes that the function get_current_time is automatically described to the LLM so it can decide when to use it. This is important because modern tool-calling APIs often convert function names, docstrings, and parameter types into structured descriptions. The model does not execute Python directly; it selects a tool call based on the tool description, and the surrounding application executes the function and returns the result.

Key concepts: tool syntax, aisuite, function description, docstring, chat completions, tool calling API

metadata.index_text: Slide 68 | Defining tools syntax | aisuite client | tools=[get_current_time] | function automatically described
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide shows how tools can be passed to an LLM client in code. The Python function get_current_time contains a docstring describing what it returns. In the aisuite example, the function is included in the tools list when calling client.chat.completions.create with model openai:gpt-4o, messages, and max_turns. The slide notes that the function get_current_time is automatically described to the LLM so it can decide when to use it. This is important because modern tool-calling APIs often convert function names, docstrings, and parameter types into structured descriptions. The model does not execute Python directly; it selects a tool call based on the tool description, and the surrounding application executes the function and returns the result.

text:
Behind the Scenes

This slide reveals the JSON schema representation behind a function tool. The get_current_time function has a name, a description taken from the docstring, and an empty parameters object because it takes no arguments. The diagram shows that the function name and description are added automatically when the tool is passed to the model. The JSON schema gives the LLM a structured description of what tools exist and how to call them. This structured format is more reliable than relying only on plain-text instructions because the application can validate tool names and parameters. The slide helps students understand the implementation layer behind high-level tool calling: functions become machine-readable tool specifications that guide the model's tool-use decisions.

Key concepts: JSON schema, function metadata, tool specification, tool name, description, parameters

metadata.index_text: Slide 69 | Behind the scenes | JSON Schema | get_current_time | name description parameters
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide reveals the JSON schema representation behind a function tool. The get_current_time function has a name, a description taken from the docstring, and an empty parameters object because it takes no arguments. The diagram shows that the function name and description are added automatically when the tool is passed to the model. The JSON schema gives the LLM a structured description of what tools exist and how to call them. This structured format is more reliable than relying only on plain-text instructions because the application can validate tool names and parameters. The slide helps students understand the implementation layer behind high-level tool calling: functions become machine-readable tool specifications that guide the model's tool-use decisions.

text:
Behind the Scenes: Functions With Parameters

This slide shows a tool schema for a function with parameters. The get_current_time(timezone) function is described as returning current time for a given timezone. The JSON schema includes a timezone parameter of type string, with a description explaining that it should be an IANA time zone string such as America/New_York or Pacific/Auckland. This metadata helps the LLM produce valid tool calls. The slide also shows the Python implementation using ZoneInfo to convert the timezone string into a timezone object and return the time formatted as hours, minutes, and seconds. The key lesson is that parameter descriptions matter: if the schema is clear, the model is more likely to call the tool with valid arguments. Tool design is therefore part API engineering and part prompt engineering.

Key concepts: function parameters, JSON schema, IANA timezone, ZoneInfo, tool arguments, parameter description

text:
A Simple Calculator

This slide illustrates the limitations of predefined tools using a calculator example. If the user asks to add 13.2 and 18.9, the LLM can choose the add tool from a list containing add, subtract, multiply, and divide, then return 32.1. However, if the user asks for the square root of 2, none of the listed tools directly supports square root. The slide asks whether we should make a new tool. This highlights a design challenge: if every operation requires a separate tool, the toolset may become large and inflexible. For narrow applications, predefined tools are useful and safe. For broader mathematical tasks, it may be better to allow code execution or a more general calculator tool. The slide motivates the next alternative: letting the model write code to solve the user's query.

Key concepts: calculator tools, add tool, limited toolset, square root, tool design, predefined functions

metadata.index_text: Slide 71 | A simple calculator | add subtract multiply divide | Add 13.2 and 18.9 | square root of 2 | make new tool

This highlights a design challenge: if every operation requires a separate tool, the toolset may become large and inflexible. For narrow applications, predefined tools are useful and safe. For broader mathematical tasks, it may be better to allow code execution or a more general calculator tool. text:
Alternative Approach: Writing Code

This slide presents code execution as a more flexible alternative to creating many small tools. The system prompt tells the model to write Python code to solve the user's query and return it between execute_python tags. When the user asks for the square root of 2, the model writes code that imports math and prints math.sqrt(2). The application executes the code, receives the output 1.4142135623730951, and the LLM converts that into a natural-language answer: the square root of 2 is approximately 1.4142. This approach is powerful because Python can handle many calculations, data transformations, and analyses without defining a separate tool for each operation. However, it also introduces security risks, which is why later slides discuss secure code execution and sandboxing.

Key concepts: code execution, Python interpreter, math.sqrt, execute_python tags, flexible tools, calculator alternative

The application executes the code, receives the output 1.4142135623730951, and the LLM converts that into a natural-language answer: the square root of 2 is approximately 1.4142. This approach is powerful because Python can handle many calculations, data transformations, and analyses without defining a separate tool for each operation. However, it also introduces security risks, which is why later slides discuss secure code execution and sandboxing.

text:
Reflection With External Feedback Revisited

This slide returns to the reflection-with-code-execution pattern. The LLM writes code for a task, execution produces output or errors, and the LLM uses that external feedback to generate an improved second draft. The repeated slide reinforces the connection between tool use and reflection: tools are not only for answering user questions, but also for testing intermediate outputs. For coding tasks, execution feedback is one of the most reliable ways to improve quality because it provides concrete evidence of failure or success. In a full agentic system, the same idea can be extended to unit tests, schema validators, linters, web search fact checks, database constraints, or simulation results.

Key concepts: reflection, external feedback, code execution, debugging loop, tool-based validation, iterative improvement

metadata.index_text: Slide 73 | Reflection with external feedback | write code | execute code | errors | improved second draft

The repeated slide reinforces the connection between tool use and reflection: tools are not only for answering user questions, but also for testing intermediate outputs. For coding tasks, execution feedback is one of the most reliable ways to improve quality because it provides concrete evidence of failure or success. In a full agentic system, the same idea can be extended to unit tests, schema validators, linters, web search fact checks, database constraints, or simulation results.

text:
Secure Code Execution

This slide warns that running code outside a sandbox can be risky. If an LLM can generate code and the application executes it directly, the code might accidentally or maliciously read files, delete data, access secrets, consume excessive resources, or connect to unauthorized systems. Sandboxes help protect against catastrophic errors by isolating execution, limiting permissions, restricting file system access, controlling network access, and setting time or memory limits. This is a critical safety consideration for agentic AI because tool use gives the model operational power. The lesson is that code execution should be treated as a controlled capability, not an unrestricted permission. Developers should use secure execution environments whenever agents can run generated code.

Key concepts: secure code execution, sandbox, tool safety, permission control, risk mitigation, generated code

text:
Patterns for Highly Autonomous Agents: Planning Workflows

This slide acts as a section divider and announces that the next topic is planning workflows for highly autonomous agents. Planning is one of the most important capabilities that distinguishes a simple LLM call from an agentic system. In a non-agentic setup, the user gives a prompt and the model directly produces an answer. In a planning workflow, the agent first decides what steps are needed, which tools or sub-agents should be used, what information must be collected, and in what order the task should proceed. The phrase highly autonomous agents implies that the system is expected to make more decisions by itself rather than relying on a developer to hard-code every step. Therefore, this section prepares the student to understand how LLMs can generate structured plans before execution.

Key concepts: planning workflows, high autonomy, agent planning, step-by-step execution, LLM agents

metadata.slide_title: Patterns for Highly Autonomous Agents: Planning Workflows
metadata.index_text: Slide 2. Patterns for highly autonomous agents. Planning workflows.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide acts as a section divider and announces that the next topic is planning workflows for highly autonomous agents. In a planning workflow, the agent first decides what steps are needed, which tools or sub-agents should be used, what information must be collected, and in what order the task should proceed. The phrase highly autonomous agents implies that the system is expected to make more decisions by itself rather than relying on a developer to hard-code every step. Therefore, this section prepares the student to understand how LLMs can generate structured plans before execution.

text:
Planning Example: Customer Service Agent

This slide gives a concrete example of planning in a customer service setting. The user asks: Do you have any round sunglasses in stock that are under $100? The slide shows an inventory database with product IDs, names, descriptions, prices, and stock counts. The inventory includes Aviator sunglasses, Catseye sunglasses, Moon sunglasses, and Classic sunglasses, each with a different description, price, and stock amount. To answer correctly, the agent must reason over several constraints: the sunglasses must be round, they must be in stock, and they must cost less than $100. The final response shown says that the Classic sunglasses meet the request because they have a classic round profile, are in stock, and cost $60. This example illustrates that even a simple customer question can require structured reasoning over database fields rather than direct text generation. The agent must identify the relevant attribute in the description, verify availability using the stock column, compare price against the user’s budget, and then formulate a helpful answer.

Key concepts: customer service agent, inventory database, constraint checking, planning, tool-based reasoning, structured data

metadata.index_text: Slide 3. Planning example customer service agent. User asks for round sunglasses in stock under $100. Inventory table with id, name, description, price, stock. Answer selects Classic sunglasses.

To answer correctly, the agent must reason over several constraints: the sunglasses must be round, they must be in stock, and they must cost less than $100. The final response shown says that the Classic sunglasses meet the request because they have a classic round profile, are in stock, and cost $60. This example illustrates that even a simple customer question can require structured reasoning over database fields rather than direct text generation. The agent must identify the relevant attribute in the description, verify availability using the stock column, compare price against the user’s budget, and then formulate a helpful answer.

text:
Planning Example: Customer Service Agent with Tools

This slide expands the previous customer service example by showing how an LLM can produce a step-by-step tool-use plan. The user query asks for round sunglasses that are in stock and under $100. The system prompt tells the model that it has access to tool descriptions and asks it to return a step-by-step plan. The model then decomposes the task into three logical operations: first, use get_item_descriptions to find round sunglasses; second, use check_inventory to verify whether the candidate items are in stock; third, use get_item_price to check whether the available results are below $100. The diagram shows a loop where the LLM generates step text, calls a tool, receives tool output, and then uses that output to form the next step. The tool list includes check_inventory, check_past_transactions, process_item_return, process_item_sale, get_item_descriptions, and get_item_price. The key educational point is that the model does not simply answer from memory; it creates a plan, selects the appropriate tools, and uses intermediate results to decide what to do next.

Key concepts: tool selection, step-by-step plan, system prompt, customer service tools, intermediate outputs, agent execution

metadata.slide_title: Planning Example: Customer Service Agent with Tools
metadata.index_text: Slide 4. Planning with tools for sunglasses query. Tools include get_item_descriptions, check_inventory, get_item_price. LLM creates sequential plan.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide expands the previous customer service example by showing how an LLM can produce a step-by-step tool-use plan. The user query asks for round sunglasses that are in stock and under $100. The system prompt tells the model that it has access to tool descriptions and asks it to return a step-by-step plan. The model then decomposes the task into three logical operations: first, use get_item_descriptions to find round sunglasses; second, use check_inventory to verify whether the candidate items are in stock; third, use get_item_price to check whether the available results are below $100. The diagram shows a loop where the LLM generates step text, calls a tool, receives tool output, and then uses that output to form the next step. The tool list includes check_inventory, check_past_transactions, process_item_return, process_item_sale, get_item_descriptions, and get_item_price. The key educational point is that the model does not simply answer from memory; it creates a plan, selects the appropriate tools, and uses intermediate results to decide what to do next.

text:
Planning Example: Item Return Request

This slide shows another customer service planning scenario, but the task is more complex because the user wants to return a specific purchased item: the gold frame glasses, but not the metal frame ones. The LLM must infer that it cannot directly process the return until it identifies the customer’s previous purchases and distinguishes between similar products. The generated plan uses check_past_transactions first to find which glasses the customer bought. Then it uses get_item_descriptions to identify which purchased item has a gold frame. Finally, it uses process_item_return to return only the gold-framed glasses. The diagram reinforces the same agent loop as the previous slide: the LLM produces step text, calls a tool, observes the output, and continues to the next step. This example is important because it shows why planning is needed when user instructions contain constraints, exceptions, or references to past actions. The phrase but not the metal frame ones requires the agent to avoid over-generalizing and to process only the correct item.

Key concepts: return workflow, past transactions, item disambiguation, tool orchestration, constraint-aware planning, customer support

metadata.index_text: Slide 5. Customer wants to return gold frame glasses but not metal frame ones. Plan uses check_past_transactions, get_item_descriptions, process_item_return.
