They provide a clearer signal for specific errors, avoid noise from the full end-to-end system, allow a focused team to optimize one component efficiently, and let developers work on smaller targeted problems faster. This is a key engineering principle: when a system is complex, isolate variables. If an end-to-end score changes, it may be unclear which part caused the change. But if a search-component score improves after changing the search query strategy, the causal connection is easier to understand. Component evals therefore complement end-to-end evals rather than replacing them.

text:
How to Address Problems You Identify

This section divider introduces the next practical question: once error analysis identifies a problem, how should developers fix it? The answer depends on whether the weak component is an LLM component or a non-LLM component. Different interventions are appropriate for search engines, retrieval systems, OCR, code execution, trained ML models, prompts, model choice, task decomposition, and fine-tuning. This section prepares students to connect diagnosis with action.

Key concepts: fixing problems, workflow improvement, LLM components, non-LLM components, intervention strategies

metadata.slide_title: How to Address Problems You Identify
metadata.index_text: Slide 54. Practical tips. How to address problems you identify.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This section divider introduces the next practical question: once error analysis identifies a problem, how should developers fix it? The answer depends on whether the weak component is an LLM component or a non-LLM component. Different interventions are appropriate for search engines, retrieval systems, OCR, code execution, trained ML models, prompts, model choice, task decomposition, and fine-tuning. This section prepares students to connect diagnosis with action.

text:
Improving Non-LLM Component Performance

This slide focuses on improving components that are not the LLM itself. Examples include web search, RAG text retrieval, code execution, and trained machine learning models for speech recognition or people detection. The slide suggests two main strategies: tune the component’s hyperparameters or replace the component. For web search, hyperparameters might include number of results and date range. For RAG, developers might adjust similarity threshold or chunk size. For machine learning models, they might adjust a detection threshold. If tuning is insufficient, developers can switch to a different web search engine, RAG provider, or model. The key lesson is that not all agent failures should be solved by prompt engineering. Sometimes the external component is the bottleneck.

Key concepts: non-LLM components, hyperparameter tuning, web search, RAG retrieval, chunk size, component replacement

metadata.index_text: Slide 55. Improving non-LLM components. Tune hyperparameters or replace component. Examples: web search results/date range, RAG threshold/chunk size, ML detection threshold.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This slide focuses on improving components that are not the LLM itself. Examples include web search, RAG text retrieval, code execution, and trained machine learning models for speech recognition or people detection. The slide suggests two main strategies: tune the component’s hyperparameters or replace the component. For web search, hyperparameters might include number of results and date range. For RAG, developers might adjust similarity threshold or chunk size. For machine learning models, they might adjust a detection threshold. If tuning is insufficient, developers can switch to a different web search engine, RAG provider, or model. The key lesson is that not all agent failures should be solved by prompt engineering. Sometimes the external component is the bottleneck.

text:
Improving LLM Component Performance

This slide focuses on improving LLM-based components. The suggested options are improving prompts, trying a new model, splitting the step into smaller steps, or fine-tuning a model. Prompt improvements may include more explicit instructions or concrete few-shot examples. Trying multiple LLMs and using evals to select the best model helps when different models have different strengths. Splitting a step is useful when the original task is too complex, such as asking one model call to extract information, reason, write, and format all at once. Fine-tuning can help when there is enough internal data and the task is repeated often. The slide teaches that LLM improvement is a toolbox: developers should choose the simplest intervention that solves the measured problem.

Key concepts: prompt improvement, model selection, task decomposition, fine-tuning, few-shot prompting, LLM component

text:
Instruction Following Prompt for PII Redaction

This slide gives a concrete instruction-following task. The input is a customer call summary containing personally identifiable information, including a full name, Social Security Number, and physical address. The prompt asks the model to identify all PII, return a list classified by type, redact all identified PII with five asterisks, and separate the list and redacted text with the label REDACTED:. This task tests multiple abilities at once: recognizing sensitive information, classifying it, following exact output formatting, and correctly redacting every occurrence. The example is useful because it shows why evaluation of LLM components must include both content correctness and instruction compliance. A model can partially identify PII but fail the formatting instruction, or follow format but miss a PII item.

Key concepts: PII redaction, instruction following, format constraints, classification, redacted text, sensitive information

The prompt asks the model to identify all PII, return a list classified by type, redact all identified PII with five asterisks, and separate the list and redacted text with the label REDACTED:. This task tests multiple abilities at once: recognizing sensitive information, classifying it, following exact output formatting, and correctly redacting every occurrence. The example is useful because it shows why evaluation of LLM components must include both content correctness and instruction compliance. text:
Instruction Following Results: Llama 3.1 8B

This slide shows an example result from Llama 3.1 8B on the PII redaction task. The model identifies a Social Security Number and an address, but the slide notes that it did not follow the instructions properly and missed some PII. In the visible output, the model does not clearly separate the list and redacted text using the requested REDACTED: delimiter, and it appears to miss the person’s full name. It also incompletely redacts the address by leaving Boulder, CO 80301 visible. This illustrates a common limitation of smaller or weaker models: they may understand part of the task but fail on strict formatting or complete coverage. The slide supports the idea that model choice matters, especially for tasks involving compliance, privacy, and exact output requirements.

Key concepts: Llama 3.1 8B, instruction failure, missed PII, formatting error, redaction quality, model limitations

text:
Instruction Following Results: GPT-5

This slide compares the previous result with GPT-5 on the same PII redaction task. The output identifies the full name Jessica T. Alvarez, the Social Security Number, and the physical address. It also follows the requested formatting by separating the identified PII list from the redacted text using REDACTED:. The redacted text replaces sensitive values with asterisks and removes the visible address. The slide labels the result as having followed the formatting instruction and identified all PII. The educational point is that more capable models often perform better on complex instruction-following tasks, especially when the task requires both semantic recognition and exact formatting. However, the slide should also remind students that model choice should be validated with evals rather than assumed.

Key concepts: GPT-5, instruction following, complete PII detection, format compliance, redaction, model comparison

text:
Developing Intuition for Model Intelligence

This slide gives practical advice for understanding which models work best for which tasks. It recommends playing with models often, maintaining a personal set of evals, reading other people’s prompts, using different models in agentic workflows, and asking which models work for which types of tasks. It also mentions that aisuite makes it easy to swap models quickly. The main lesson is that model intelligence is not one-dimensional. A model that is good at writing may be weaker at structured extraction, and a model that is strong at reasoning may be slower or more expensive. Developers build intuition by experimenting systematically and comparing outputs with evals.

Key concepts: model intuition, personal evals, prompt examples, model swapping, aisuite, task-model fit

text:
Latency and Cost Optimization

This section divider introduces latency and cost optimization. Agentic systems often involve multiple LLM calls, tool calls, retrieval operations, code execution, and external APIs. Even if the final answer is correct, the workflow may be too slow or expensive for production use. Optimizing latency means reducing response time, often through parallelism, caching, faster models, or fewer steps. Optimizing cost means reducing token usage, API calls, compute time, and unnecessary tool use. This section teaches that practical agentic AI requires engineering trade-offs, not only accuracy improvement.

Key concepts: latency, cost optimization, production systems, token cost, API cost, workflow efficiency

text:
Research Agent Runtime Analysis

This slide annotates the research agent workflow with runtime values. The stages include web search, web fetch, PDF-to-text conversion, source selection, and essay drafting. Visible times include 11 seconds, 7 seconds, 5 seconds, 18 seconds, and 3 seconds for different steps. The slide asks whether parallelism should be considered and whether LLM steps are too long. It suggests trying a smaller or less intelligent model, or a faster LLM provider, when LLM steps dominate runtime. The key lesson is that latency optimization begins by measuring time per component. If web fetches can run in parallel, total time may decrease. If one LLM call takes most of the time, changing model, prompt length, or provider may be more effective.

Key concepts: runtime analysis, parallelism, LLM latency, research agent, faster model, workflow timing

text:
Costing Your Workflow

This slide lists the main sources of cost in an agentic workflow. LLM steps are often priced per token, so both prompt length and output length matter. API-calling tools may be priced per request, meaning frequent web search or data provider calls can add cost. Compute steps depend on server capacity and time, such as running code, processing PDFs, or hosting models. The slide encourages developers to view an agentic workflow as a cost graph, not just a logic graph. Every step has a financial or computational cost, and optimization may require reducing unnecessary calls, batching operations, caching repeated results, or using smaller models for simpler subtasks.

Key concepts: workflow costing, token pricing, API calls, compute cost, cost graph, agent economics

The slide encourages developers to view an agentic workflow as a cost graph, not just a logic graph. text:
Research Agent Cost Breakdown

This slide provides a concrete cost breakdown for the research agent workflow. It assigns small token costs to LLM steps, such as $0.0004 and $0.009, API call costs such as $0.016 and $0.002, and a PDF-to-text cost of $0.03. The exact values are examples, but they show how costs accumulate across a multi-step workflow. A single inexpensive LLM call may not be the main cost; PDF processing or external API calls might dominate. The slide teaches that cost optimization should be based on measurement. Developers should identify the expensive steps and decide whether to reduce, replace, cache, or parallelize them. This is especially important when workflows run at scale for many users.

Key concepts: cost breakdown, research agent, token cost, API call cost, PDF-to-text cost, scaling cost

text:
Development Process Summary

This section divider introduces the final summary of the development process. The previous slides covered evaluation, error analysis, component-level testing, improvement strategies, latency, and cost. This section condenses those ideas into a repeatable build-and-analyze cycle. The goal is to give students a practical mental model for developing agentic AI systems systematically rather than relying on trial-and-error prompting.

Key concepts: development process, build and analyze, agentic AI engineering, workflow iteration, summary

metadata.index_text: Slide 65. Practical tips. Development process summary.
metadata.slide_paragraph: This section divider introduces the final summary of the development process. The previous slides covered evaluation, error analysis, component-level testing, improvement strategies, latency, and cost. This section condenses those ideas into a repeatable build-and-analyze cycle. The goal is to give students a practical mental model for developing agentic AI systems systematically rather than relying on trial-and-error prompting.

text:
Build-Analyze Improvement Loop

This slide summarizes the recommended development process as a loop between building and analyzing. On the build side, developers create an end-to-end system and improve individual components. On the analyze side, they examine outputs and traces, build evals, compute metrics, perform error analysis, and create component-level evals. This loop captures the core engineering workflow for agentic AI. First, build something complete enough to observe. Second, analyze where it fails. Third, improve the component most responsible for failure. Fourth, measure again. The diagram emphasizes that agentic AI development is iterative. Because workflows involve LLMs, tools, data sources, and external systems, developers should expect repeated cycles of measurement and refinement.

Key concepts: build-analyze loop, end-to-end system, traces, metrics, error analysis, component improvement
